- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-06-03T16:31:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<adg5bt$2t7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <49UI8.52393$%o.5150507@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <fcd09c56.0205291103.612e81d8@posting.google.com> <ad868c$bc8$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3CF7AB2B.A1F15E7A@shaw.ca> <ad8m40$3qr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3CF7EB87.FEE13DCD@shaw.ca>`

```

On 31-May-2002, "JamesJ. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> Where angels fear to tread.... <G>.  So OO only adds to the nightmare ?
> Put a file access class between YOU and the rotten Vendor's software.

First I need to persuade my employers that we should become the first
company to include OO CoBOL in with existing enterprise CoBOL.

But I am curious - how hard is it to put such a class in between the
existing non OO calling programs and the existing non OO called programs?


> Don't think USAF have two seats up front in fighters do they ? Gawd, I'd
> hate to be your co-pilot <G>.

I have to admit that I missed whatever point you were making here but..

Actually, I did fly T37 jets that had side by side seating.  While I was in
pilot training we had some officers (from Chile?) who didn't advance to the
T38 flying because they were going to fly A37s.   But their A37s didn't have
a right seat.  I've never seen one in person.  I really don't see any
difference in how we would fly whether the fighter plane had tandem or
side-by-side seating.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-01T16:44:14+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF8EBCE.1EB93E29@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <49UI8.52393$%o.5150507@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <fcd09c56.0205291103.612e81d8@posting.google.com> <ad868c$bc8$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3CF7AB2B.A1F15E7A@shaw.ca> <ad8m40$3qr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
> Only the programmers didn't anticipate this and only check for '00'.  

Note that 'success' is indicated entirely by the first character being
'0' and checking both for '00' may result in erroneous failure
indications.

For example on a read or write a file-status of '02' indicates
successful operation but there are valid duplicate alternative keys.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-06-03T16:45:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<adg65f$36v$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <49UI8.52393$%o.5150507@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <fcd09c56.0205291103.612e81d8@posting.google.com> <ad868c$bc8$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3CF7AB2B.A1F15E7A@shaw.ca> <ad8m40$3qr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3CF8EBCE.1EB93E29@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

On  1-Jun-2002, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> > Only the programmers didn't anticipate this and only check for '00'.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> successful operation but there are valid duplicate alternative keys.

Here's the open paragraph:

 OPEN-FILE.
     IF OPEN-SW = 'Y'
         MOVE '2' TO RTN-CODE
         GO TO EXIT-THIS-PGM.
     IF READ-TYPE = 'U'
         OPEN INPUT RND-FILE
       ELSE
         OPEN I-O RND-FILE.
     MOVE 'Y' TO OPEN-SW.
     IF FILE-STATUS NOT = '00'
         MOVE '7' TO RTN-CODE.
     GO TO EXIT-THIS-PGM.

We are gradually getting rid of it - by getting rid of the VSAM files.

I really never understood the advantage of using this kind of call in the
first place - maybe it was because the vendor didn't want to train its
programmers on how to do VSAM I/O.  They also have called programs to write
reports that are MUCH harder to debug than simple writes are.  And they
sometimes are impossible to change to what the user wants - giving us the
excuse to replace the calls with simple CoBOL.

At any rate it is this type of nonsense that makes enterprises leery about
buying a vendor supplied OO system (if such were available).  We just KNOW
that they will have programmers of this quality doing it.  I would rather
fix a poorly designed isolated system than a poorly designed integrated
system.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <49UI8.52393$%o.5150507@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ad2i48$q3p$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <fcd09c56.0205291103.612e81d8@posting.google.com> <ad868c$bc8$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3CF7AB2B.A1F15E7A@shaw.ca> <ad8m40$3qr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3CF8EBCE.1EB93E29@Azonic.co.nz> <adg65f$36v$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
> Here's the open paragraph:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> that they will have programmers of this quality doing it.  

I note that it leaves the 'Open-SW' set to "Y" when it has failed to
open the file.  Good work, the OPEN can't even be retried. 

Of course the advantage of a set of centralised routines is that they
can be fixed in one place rather than in every instance in several
hundred programs, but they should get it right.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-01T07:26:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF8771B.8F62F5AE@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3d7858be.0205282233.ef4eacd@posting.google.com> <ad2i5p$pb3$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <gL6J8.67127$Gs.6168744@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3CF5BF7E.9B2B71D8@worldnet.att.net> <3CF7DF12.7000007@optonline.net>`

```
Liam Devlin wrote:
> 
> Arnold Trembley wrote:
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> I've written/maintained a LOT like that:-)

Actually, my example used a flag and condition name that was *never*
set true, so it really was an infinite loop.  It was an unusual
application, a started task using the H&W SYSB product to capture
transaction data from one CICS system's log files (and thus allowing
another TCB) and STARTing the messages over to a network monitor CICS
region.  Since both CICS systems were (and are) 24 by 7, the data
capture process could only be stopped by cancelling the started task.

Later we added a refinement to read a temp storage queue in the
network monitor CICS system that could be posted with a request to
shut down the data capture system.  Then we set INFINITE-LOOP-DONE to
true.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-04T07:48:12+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CFC62AC.CC72024D@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3d7858be.0205282233.ef4eacd@posting.google.com> <ad2i5p$pb3$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <gL6J8.67127$Gs.6168744@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3CF6A332.1030605@optonline.net> <ad86q3$bi1$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <G5SJ8.17863$4i.2084778@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <adg456$dc$1@clematis.singnet.com.sg>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com> wrote 

> 4. PERFORM procedure-name UNTIL terminal-condition
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> statement, the code pointed to by the PERFORM statement is skipped. 

This may confuse some.  You should have said "In #4, the terminal .."

It is true that the terminal condition is tested in #5 but only after
the FROM phrase, so in #5 the code will not be skipped regardless of the
state of 'MyTndx' prior to the PERFORM.

> > READ MYFILE.    *>so-called 'priming read'
> > PERFORM UNTIL MYFILE-STATUS NOT = '00'
> >     process record
> >    READ MYFILE
> > END-PERFORM.

A file status should only be tested for success in the first byte. 
Testing both bytes may result in false failures being indicated.  For
example a status of "02" is a successful read and there are valid
duplicates in an alternate key.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-03T21:34:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VhRK8.108552$Kp.11000645@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3d7858be.0205282233.ef4eacd@posting.google.com> <ad2i5p$pb3$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <gL6J8.67127$Gs.6168744@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3CF6A332.1030605@optonline.net> <ad86q3$bi1$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <G5SJ8.17863$4i.2084778@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <adg456$dc$1@clematis.singnet.com.sg> <3CFC62AC.CC72024D@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3CFC62AC.CC72024D@Azonic.co.nz...
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com> wrote
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > In #5, the terminal condition is tested at the beginning of the
procedure.
> > So, if the terminal condition is true when the computer hits the PERFORM
> > statement, the code pointed to by the PERFORM statement is skipped.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
If you're referring to the example, the original condition of MYINDX, I
agree, is irrelevant since MYINDX is under complete control of the PERFORM
construct.

Otherwise, the original #5 and it's explanation is correct.

The terminal condition is tested before the perform. If the terminal
condition is satisfied, the perform code is not executed. Not even once.

MOVE -1 TO TERMINAL-CONDITION.
PERFORM VARYING INDX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL
    INDX > TERMINAL-CONDITION
   (some code)
END-PERFORM.

(some code) is never executed.

Now you could force (some code) to be executed at least once by including
the WITH TEST AFTER phrase.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: dynamic index

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-04T17:29:29+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CFCEAE9.FEE2B7E9@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ad03p7$i2b$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <3d7858be.0205282233.ef4eacd@posting.google.com> <ad2i5p$pb3$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <gL6J8.67127$Gs.6168744@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3CF6A332.1030605@optonline.net> <ad86q3$bi1$1@coco.singnet.com.sg> <G5SJ8.17863$4i.2084778@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <adg456$dc$1@clematis.singnet.com.sg> <3CFC62AC.CC72024D@Azonic.co.nz> <VhRK8.108552$Kp.11000645@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:

> > > 5. PERFORM procedure-name VARYING x FROM y BY z UNTIL terminal-condition
> > > (example: PERFORM MYLOOP VARYING MYINDX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL MYINDX > 100)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > statement, the code pointed to by the PERFORM statement is skipped.

> Otherwise, the original #5 and it's explanation is correct.

But misleading.  Your example in #5 would be performed 100 times
regardless of whether the 'terminal condition is true when it hits the
PERFORM statement'. 

If 'MyIndx' were 200 when it 'hit the perform' the terminal condition is
true but it still does the perform 100 times (other things being equal).
 
> The terminal condition is tested before the perform. If the terminal
> condition is satisfied, the perform code is not executed. Not even once.

No. Wrong. The terminal condition is _NOT_ tested _before_ the PERFORM,
it is tested _during_ the PERFORM, after any VARYING .. FROM phrases and
before executing the specified procedure name or inline code.

> MOVE -1 TO TERMINAL-CONDITION.
> PERFORM VARYING INDX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> (some code) is never executed.

But the VARYING ... FROM phrase is.  That is: the PERFORM will be
executed at least as far as INDX being set to 1 before the test is
done.  This is not the same as 'Before the PERFORM' or 'when it hits the
PERFORM'.

In your revised example the 'terminal condition' remains true after the
VARYING phrase, in the original example #5, the terminal condition may
have been true when it 'hits' the PERFORM, but the VARYING phrase
changed this, so your reference to 'before the PERFORM' was misleading
(ie wrong).
```

```
PERFORM

Forget the example.

…[27 more quoted lines elided]…

OK. I surrender to the protestations of the pedantic fuddy-duddy cartel.

comprising the scope of the perform are executed."

admonitions of precision way beyond that which is required for government
work.
```

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com> wrote 

JM > Otherwise, the original #5 and it's explanation is correct.

I can't comment on whether your 'precision' is adequate for the work
that you do, that should be an issue between you and your employer.

In general, your precision would have been adequate if you were
conversing with the experienced members of this group, they may
understand that it was imprecise but would know what actually
occurred.

However, you were responding to a student who had no experience or
knowledge that would allow him to filter out misleading or wrong
information.  You were attempting to teach a particular aspect of the
language and for this a higher standard of correctness is required.
```

```
>
> Code such as WHEN TRUE ALSO FALSE is the epitome of
> esoteric coding.  This could only make sense to a Zen Buddhist.
> Grace Hopper is rolling over in her grave.

IF END-FILE
   AND NOT END-JOB
   ......

There, that's not so hard, is it? It's just another way of saying:

EVALUATE TRUE ALSO FALSE
   WHEN END-FILE ALSO END-JOB
   ......
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Evaluate readability

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamDD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-02T06:00:54+00:00

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> Having I said this before <G>, ...
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>          ...


I don't think this is what you want. If it's numeric & > 3, you'll 
always take the Numeric case.


This is the sort of situation in which I'd code:

Evaluate 
True
When 
	(printSnapshot and sendEmail)
	process both
When 
	printSnapshot
	process Snapshot only
When 
	sendEmail
	process email only
When 
	Other
	...
End-Evaluate


I think it's a little more straightforward, more easily followed by most 
COBOLers.
```

#### ↳ Re: Evaluate readability

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-01T07:40:16+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF86C50.A5FB2219@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ad7sq7$kco$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
>  Evaluate printSnapshot Also sendEmail
>    When true also true
>      move both-snapshots-and-email       to retcode
>      move ' both snapshots and email'    to retcode-text
...
>  End-Evaluate
 
> I found this interesting - Do you like this code or dislike this code?

It is a decision table coded as an evaluate. Having used decision tables
some decades ago with pre-processors I find that evaluates tables such
as this are 'sideways'.  If pre-processors were still around, or I could
be bothered writing one, then I might use a real decision table, but I
usually find that nested evaluates are more understandable than single
combined ones.

Of corse in this case where there are only a few options and the action
is entirely repetitive I would probably do it in a data table.  If the
text descriptions were actually used, for example in printing an
invoice, then I would put these into my 'decode file' where the user can
maintain them.  The evaluate would then degrade to:

         MOVE "REQUIRED"       TO Decode-Field
         MOVE Required-Flags   TO Decode-Key
         PERFORM Decode
         MOVE Decode-Text      TO RetCode-Text
         MOVE Decode-Flag      TO Retcode

'Decode' basically does an index file read on a key that concatenates
the 'field' and 'key'.  Many small items fit into one file that in my
systems is always open.  The file is maintained by one program that gets
the record layout for each 'field' from a control file.

In this particular case the decode may also contain the charge to apply
for sending the EMail and/or Snapshots.
```

#### ↳ Re: Evaluate readability

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-02T18:46:32+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cfb34a8_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<ad7sq7$kco$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In my opinion this is junk.

The condition names are good, but the Evaluate is too convoluted. I believe
Evaluates with ALSO are best avoided...(I have NEVER used ALSO in an
Evaluate)...

As I like general solutions, I have one for such instances. I have used it
for the past 30 years with great success in both High and Low Level
Languages.

The problem can be stated as:

1. A number of conditions must be detected which are non-mutually exclusive
and non-implicative. In other words, they are not dependent on each other (P
does NOT imply Q) and if any one is true, it does NOT preclude ANY others
being true (In this case, there are 2 such conditons, plus the "boundary
condition" that nothing is true.)

2. Any number of them may be true at any given time. (In this case, UP to
2...)

As 2 is a fairly trivial quantity, let's demonstrate with 4. I'll call them
C1 thru C4 and you can substitute any conditions you like that meet the
above constraints.

Here's my code for the same "problem"...

(set up a field in WS called truthValue. It has to hold a number and will be
involved in arithmetic, so, after carefully reading all the threads on
efficiency posted in CLC over the past 5 years, make it floating point...
<G> No, it should be whatever is most efficient on your machine IF YOU CARE
ABOUT THAT SORT OF THING! (It is not for me to say whether you should or
shouldn't care about that sort of thing, any more than it is my place to
tell you whether you should care about the rainforest, or regularly changing
your underwear...))

In my environment it is a field called truthValue defined as PIC s9(4)
comp-5. (On the mainframe I'd use a halfword...)
...
move zero to truthValue
if C1 move 1 to truthValue end-if
if C2 move 2 to truthValue end-if
if C3 move 4 to truthValue end-if
if C4 move 8 to truthValue end-if
evaluate truthValue
     when 0
            *> nothing is true...
     when 1
            *> C1 ONLY is true
     when 2
            *> C2 ONLY is true
     when 3
            *> C1 AND C2 are true

Note: This would be the extent required for the example given. (Exactly the
same number of WHENs but ALSO is not required)...

     when 4
            *> C3 ONLY is true
     when 5
            *> C3 AND C1 are true
     when 6
            *> C3 AND C2 are true
     when 7
            *> C3 AND C2 AND C1are true
     when 8
            *> C4 ONLY is true
     when 9
            *> C4 AND C1 are true
     when 10
            *> C4 AND C2 are true
     when 11
            *> C4 AND C2 AND C1 are true
     when 12
            *> C4 AND C3 are true
     when 13
            *> C4 AND C3 AND C1 are true
     when 14
            *> C4 AND C3 AND C2 are true
     when 15
            *> C4 AND C3 AND C2  AND C1 are true    (ALL conditions true)
     when other
            *>  there was an internal haemhorrage and the CPU lies broken
and bleeding...
            *> the state of your conditions
            *> pales into insignificance as you realise the machine is not
insured and your last
            *> backup was just before the Millennium party...
end-evaluate
(You should arrange your WHEN clauses in the order of what is most likely,
rather than numerical sequence, given that you KNOW which combinations are
most likely and given that you CARE... (see above).)

This is a relatively simple way of handling a number of conditions without
going to the overhead of decision tables (although I reckon these are
excellent for complex decisions, PROVIDED there has been a Boolean
Simplification of the conditions before they are loaded to the decision
tables...but that's another story...).

The most valuable use I found for this technique was in the old days when we
needed to update/merge multiple sequential files against a Master (the
classic 3-way-split operation). It was very useful to control what was
"high" in the various 3-way-splits of the files against each other and the
Master... I liked it because you could do a bunch of key compares, then
split to the correct processing without having to do multiple in-line
3-way-splits. Then they went and spoiled it all by inventing disk drives
<G>....

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Evaluate readability

- **From:** "William Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-19T01:46:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4oRP8.30730$uk2.13022908@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Can I have comments on another approach. I, also, am not that comfortable
with ALSO.

What do people think of this? Is this bad, since, in the second and third
WHENs, it may not be obvious the first one (as a whole) is false?

Evaluate true
   When printSnapshot AND sendEmail
     move both-snapshots-and-email       to retcode
     move ' both snapshots and email'    to retcode-text
   When printSnapshot    (===>SendEmail must be false if you are here)
     move snapshots-only                 to retcode
     move ' snapshots only          '    to retcode-text
   When sendEmail          (===>printSnapshot must be false if you are here)
     move email-only                     to retcode
     move ' email only              '    to retcode-text
   When Other
     move neither-snapshots-nor-email    to retcode
     move ' neither snapshots nor email' to retcode-text
 End-Evaluate
```

```
William Bub wrote:

> Can I have comments on another approach. I, also, am not that comfortable
> with ALSO.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>  End-Evaluate

Now your option is one I could probably have a problem with at either 2am or 2pm
<G>. But, with a qualifier. If your lines with "===>" were in fact comments "*>"
then I could grudgingly live with it - but in the wee small hours the
maintenance programmer still has to read your comments to get the hang of it..

However, there's another aspect - what if it was 8 or 16 conditions ? Plus what
we haven't discussed - frequency  (Pareto - 20% of conditions account for 80% of
the actions). Given that there was a genuine need to put them in descending, by
'highest frequency', I can see your approach might well run into trouble. Given
8 or 16 conditions, they could of course be nested Evaluates.

In summary - Nah ! Don't like it ! <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5p_P8.64158$uX3.4235@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<ad7sq7$kco$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4oRP8.30730$uk2.13022908@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
William Bub <fathafluff@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:4oRP8.30730$uk2.13022908@twister.nyroc.rr.com...
> Can I have comments on another approach. I, also, am not that comfortable
> with ALSO.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>    When sendEmail          (===>printSnapshot must be false if you are
here)
>    When Other
>  End-Evaluate

In general  I do not like source code which relies on "negative" testing;
i.e, "if you get here, (condition) must be (whatever)".  I much prefer
explicit "positive" tests.....

Assuming I went this way, just for readability I'd probably use...

 Evaluate true
    When printSnapshot    AND sendEmail
    When printSnapshot    AND NOT SendEmail
    When sendEmail         AND NOT PrintSnapshot
   When Other
  End-Evaluate

.. which of course, leads right back to ALSO.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Evaluate readability

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-06-22T00:02:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0206212302.1ad623cd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ad7sq7$kco$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
I use this all the time.  In fact, I wrote an article about it for
"Enterprise Systems Journal" in 1997.  One thing I recommend is LINE
UP THE CODE ELEMENTS.  It makes it much more readable, particularly if
there are more elements than this example.  If the lines get too long,
the new standard will let you code beyond column 72. (The devil made
me say it!  I've been opposing the new formats since I joined the
Committee!)

  Evaluate printSnapshot Also sendEmail
    When   true          also true
      move both-snapshots-and-email       to retcode
      move ' both snapshots and email'    to retcode-text
    When   true          also false
      move snapshots-only                 to retcode
      move ' snapshots only          '    to retcode-text
    When   false         also true
      move email-only                     to retcode
      move ' email only              '    to retcode-text
    When Other
      move neither-snapshots-nor-email    to retcode
      move ' neither snapshots nor email' to retcode-text
  End-Evaluate
  
This is standard syntax, not a cutesy-poo kludge.  Anyone who has
trouble with this code at 2am is not yet a professional COBOL
programmer.

Stephen J Spiro
J4 COBOL Stabdards Committee
```

#### ↳ Re: Evaluate readability

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-26T10:12:59-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B93F442B0110CA3610E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<ad7sq7$kco$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Fri, 31 May 2002 9:16:51 -0400, Howard Brazee wrote
> I recently came across the following code:
> 
>  Evaluate printSnapshot Also sendEmail

I too like ALSO, but I have found that the typical COBOL programmer 
does not.

And despite the power for implementing decision tables, I have found 
that the EVALUATE which I use most often -- even when I'm following my 
own nose -- is EVALUATE TRUE. Far too many actual problems involve 
messy and overlapping conditions which must be tested sequentially, and 
the decision table model does not fit these well. When I last taught 
COBOL74->85 update, I spent some time on decision tables. On the next 
iteration, I will replace this with rules-based programming (see 
below).

Interestingly, in my rather small experience in the matter, I've found 
that it's the older COBOL programmers who know decision tables, or 
perhaps the ones who've been trained in business analysis rather than 
just COBOL.

Any EVALUATE can be rewritten as EVALUATE TRUE, although it often 
becomes quite a bit wordier and (to me) less understandable. But at 
least typical COBOL programmers are delighted with EVALUATE data-name, 
and accept EVALUATE TRUE as preferable to what was used in COBOL74.

I have no objection at all to WHEN TRUE ALSO FALSE, and I think that 
any COBOL programmer who takes the time to become familiar with it will 
follow it easily. EVALUATE TRUE ALSO FALSE is a different matter, and I 
would use it only in very limited, formal contexts with lots of 
explanation. Of course, EVALUATE FALSE used to avoid writing NOT is 
simply a game -- it only avoids the word NOT; it fails to avoid the 
kind of logic that some COBOL programmers find problematic and is even 
harder to follow than a simple NOT.

I have also found, in converting a lot of COBOL74 to COBOL85, that I 
can often take complex, convoluted, nearly impenetrable, sometimes 
nested IF statements and convert them to a simple list of WHENs. This 
gets into the area of rules-based programming. I think that all COBOL 
programmers ought to be exposed to rules-based programming, simply 
because (like decision tables) it arises so often in the application 
domains where COBOL is commonly used. The ease and clarity of 
implementing a set of rules with EVALUATE -- even just EVALUATE TRUE -- 
just reinforces this as a good idea.

As far as professional programmers, the fact is that many -- perhaps 
most -- COBOL programmers are not professional programmers in any 
strong sense of the word. The good ones are mostly generalists -- they 
know some COBOL, some programming, a good bit about their agency's 
function and operation, a good bit about the logic of the systems they 
work with, something about their organization and its people, etc. It 
would be ideal if they were experts in everything, but we have to 
respect the environment in which they actually work.

Edward Reid
```

```
Thanks.

Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net> wrote in article
<01HW.B93F442B0110CA3610E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com>...
> On Fri, 31 May 2002 9:16:51 -0400, Howard Brazee wrote
> > I recently came across the following code:
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Evaluate readability

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-27T11:32:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B940A85001646B9A10E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<ad7sq7$kco$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <01HW.B93F442B0110CA3610E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com> <01c21d73$d044a780$da95f243@chottel>`

```
On Wed, 26 Jun 2002 20:45:34 -0400, Charles Hottel wrote
> Can you give us some references to good info on rules base programming? 

The term "rule based programming" (rather than "rules") seems to be the 
norm. I googled it and found lots of references, including a lot of 
commercial products, but no good introduction. The best article seems 
to be

  Rule-based Systems, by Frederick Hayes-Roth, Communications
  of the ACM, Sept 1985 (v28 n9) p921.

It's available on the ACM Digital Library (http://www.acm.org). I'm not 
sure whether just ACM membership is enough to access it (since it's in 
CACM, which all members receive) or if you have to buy the Digital 
Library add-on. Since the PDF version is technically restricted, I 
can't make a public offer to email it to you.

Rule-based programming is a lot more than I was talking about, and 
complete rule based systems don't look like procedural programming at 
all. In particular, it includes storage of facts as well as rules, and 
the concept of an inference engine which analyzes rules and enables 
complex inferences. I was talking -- and rather loosely at that -- 
about the simpler case where you have a list of rules -- conditions and 
actions:

  IF this THEN do that
  IF the-other THEN do something-else

and you pick the first that applies. Quite similar to decision tables 
when you drop the inference part.

The piece of the concept that I was picking up was, it's a lot simpler 
to follow and modify the program (business and COBOL) if you simply 
have a one-layer list of rules explaining what to do in each case. Once 
you nest the decisions, it's harder to follow what's happening, and 
often a LOT harder to modify. As with decision tables, you restrict and 
simplify the structure, and if properly applied then this simplifies 
the tasks of program creation, understanding, and modification.

So I really took a small part of the rule-based concept and applied it 
to COBOL usage patterns.

Edward
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paulpigott@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-02T22:10:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <280520022044520394%josephk@iinet.net.au> <ad0nie$hum$1@helle.btinternet.com> <3CF3FF67.59DAB44A@shaw.ca>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3CF3FF67.59DAB44A@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I think that supports one of Bill's original guesses - "IBM mainframe
COBOL
> > programmers want to put in a "day's work" and then stop thinking about
> > COBOL". And was also true before the rise of the Internet.
>
> Certainly Alan's comment is valid. Here in Calgary there is the same
corporate
> stringent approach to ensure non-computer people don't just spend their
days
> whistling around the Internet. And of course, you are gonzo if you hit the
> X-sites !
>
> Although mainframers in a large installation can swap ideas - without
getting
> outside your own particular turf, just how do you discover new techniques.
Has
> the mainframe world reached a situation, (particularly geared to batch
> processing), where there are no new techniques to be found ?

I must concur.  There are only so many ways to write a report, update a
keyed file, perform an internal sort, do a match/merge, or a CICS update
screen.  Obviously, every program is different, but the underlying premises
do not change drastically, particularly in the "business" world that is
oriented to COBOL.


> However, for those of us, who still put in our eight hour day, and get
involved
> here - what does that make us, geeks <G>.

LONG LIVE THE GEEKS!!!


> One provocative suggestion - for the most part mainframe programming is
BORING
> - while there are certainly challenges, just like Doc has highlighted with
his
> DB problem, you can do more 'exciting' stuff with PCs. That's not meant as
a
> put down - but does it state a reality ?  (How often does a programmer in
a
> large installation take an application from StartProgram to FinalProgram
in an
> application.)

You raise an interesting point.  If your focus is the logical problem, and
not the syntax of the language it's being solved in, that are only a few
basic problems.  An infinity of variations to be sure, but only a few basic
problems.  Yes, CICS screens are boring.  Yes, PC GUIs are much prettier.
Yes, mainframes (for the most part) do not crash.  Yes, PCs (all to
frequently) lock up.  But PC development is the "sexy" job, and a colorful
GUI will dazzle a user in a way that a green CICS screen never could.

But, one thing I think is being overlooked.  When I first began working with
CICS in the early 80s, we were on version 1.5, or 1.7.  It's been a while.
But it wasn't the nice stable platform then that it is now.  So history
seems to indicate that the PC world may ultimately stabilize.  Maybe.  Any
wagers anyone? (Hehehehehe).

Our VP came down to give us a pep talk one day, and his basic take on PCs
was that, as soon as they run for months without requiring a re-boot or a
complete re-install, he might start taking them seriously.  I certainly
couldn't disagree with him.  (Before I get lots of flames, I know there are
some platforms, such as UNIX, that have demonstrated a remarkable stability.
The Windows world, however, leaves something to be desired in that
department).

>
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
>
>

Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-26T11:39:35-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B93F587701158E4210E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3CF47911.189318CE@worldnet.att.net> <DDB1600CF8008320.A59EBC97492D6A6F.FC43E6956D137C6B@lp.airnews.net> <3CF5CC2A.FF040E5C@worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Thu, 30 May 2002 2:52:14 -0400, Arnold Trembley wrote
> Well, I think his statements are inflammatory, and I still don't
> understand his point. 

Dijkstra is a purist and a gadfly. He wants people to write correct 
programs. His statements are based on long observation of factors that 
lead to incorrect programs. He doesn't tend to let his views be 
modified by the necessities of the real world.

I notice that he got your attention.

> "Programming is one of the most difficult branches of applied
> mathematics; the poorer mathematicians had better remain pure
> mathematicians."  I do not agree that programming is a branch of
> applied mathematics, unless you can also call building a house or
> cabinet-making a branch of applied mathematics.

The point is that you have to use hard logic for programming. Logic 
*is* a branch of mathematics, and programming is based on logic. Most 
programming is not *formally* based on logic (Dijkstra would have it 
so) but is logic nonetheless.

Many non-mathematicians think of mathematics as just arithmetic. It's 
much more.

In this comment I believe that he was referring more to the study of 
programming, design of programming languages and algorithms, than to 
actual coding.

Note however that Dijksta himself has solved some VERY hard problems, 
such as much of the early work on process synchronization which we now 
take for granted.

> Why is this now his province and not mine?

Well, he *was* the first person to call himself a Computer Scientist. 
He's been studying these issues for much longer than most of us.

> "The easiest machine applications are the technical/scientific
> computations."  Give me an example of a technical/scientific
> computation, and I can probably name you another that is quite
> difficult.

The point is that technical and scientific problems are generally well 
specified. Many other problems, especially those involving a human 
interface, are much more difficult to specify precisely. Numerical 
problems tend to have some uniformity to their structure; operating 
system problems tend to be much more non-uniform.

> "The tools we use have a profound (and devious!) influence on our
> thinking habits, and, therefore, on our thinking abilities."  Pure
> horsefeathers. 

I'm quite surprised to see anyone disagreeing with this. While you may 
disagree on whether the effect of a particular tool is good or bad, 
it's strikingly true that our thinking habits *are* influenced deeply 
by the tools we use. It's one of the most universal truths I've 
encountered. This is why educational environments, from pre-school on, 
are designed with a rich set of tools, to develop rich thinking 
behaviors. The elucidation of this sentence could fill an entire course 
in human psychology.

COBOL does *not* have a good influence on our thinking habits. 
Programmers who write good COBOL invariably have exposure to 
programming ideas beyond COBOL, whether that's in the form of another 
programming language or simply in how to use techniques in COBOL which 
are not supported directly by the language.

> And why is it that computer science departments will have a class to
> teach students how to write a C compiler in C, but never seem to ask
> how programming languages should be designed?

Dijksta would certainly ask the same question. If he were writing that 
article today, I'm sure he'd be at least as harsh on C as on COBOL. 

> I once worked for a programming supervisor who told me the most
> incompetent programmer he ever hired had a BS in computer science.  He
> claimed he got much better programming out of data processing students
> from the local Junior College.

Dijksta has never had any love for the quality of most computer science 
programs. You'll note that many of the comments in this article are 
very strongly critical of computer science *teaching*.

Edward Reid
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

```
In article <01HW.B93F587701158E4210E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com>,
Edward Reid  <edwardreid@spamcop.net> wrote:
>On Thu, 30 May 2002 2:52:14 -0400, Arnold Trembley wrote

[snip]

>> "Programming is one of the most difficult branches of applied
>> mathematics; the poorer mathematicians had better remain pure
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>*is* a branch of mathematics, and programming is based on logic.

'Logic *is* a branch of mathematics'? (emphasis original)  This is... 
something different, to say the least; the last time I looked logic was of 
the trivium while things like arithmetic and geometry (number-stuff and 
measuring-of-the-earth, considered to be some of the manthanomai, or 
knowable-things)) were quadrivial.

Now, Mr Reid, I realise that education is not what it once was... but of 
the Seven Liberal Arts the trivium was the 'lower division' while the 
quadrivium was the 'upper'; one is most definitely *not* (emphasis 
original) a 'branch' of the other any more than grammar is a 'branch' of 
music.

[snippage]

>Many non-mathematicians think of mathematics as just arithmetic. It's 
>much more.

See above about 'knowable things' and 'number-stuff'.

[snippage]

>> Why is this now his province and not mine?
>
>Well, he *was* the first person to call himself a Computer Scientist. 

The French have a hyphenated phrase for this: soi-disant.  I *was* the 
first person in this newsgroup to call himself the King of England.

>He's been studying these issues for much longer than most of us.

In the words of that profound philosopher, Archie Bunker, 'Whoop dee doo'.  
Leaving aside the bromide about twenty years' worth of experience versus 
one year's worth twenty times over... let me get to the bromide of 
'Arguments based on authority are those of the weakest sort'... which we 
know is so because Augustine said that Boetius said so.

Mr Reid, please forgive me... you might have something rather profound to 
say about Djikstra's views but you seem to be hiding it very well.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-26T21:03:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B93FDC89013491FF10E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <DDB1600CF8008320.A59EBC97492D6A6F.FC43E6956D137C6B@lp.airnews.net> <3CF5CC2A.FF040E5C@worldnet.att.net> <01HW.B93F587701158E4210E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com> <afd04c$17p$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
On Wed, 26 Jun 2002 14:11:24 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote
> 'Logic *is* a branch of mathematics'? (emphasis original)  This is... 
> something different, to say the least; [...]
> Mr Reid, please forgive me... you might have something rather profound to 
> say about Djikstra's views but you seem to be hiding it very well.

DD, you may have something to say about logic and mathematics, but it 
appears that you haven't visited a college math curriculum in the past 
150 years -- that being about the time that mathematicians have been 
keen on developing logic. Before that, logic was more often considered 
part of philosophy, but at the same time philosophy was a broader field 
than today, including it various times much of what we now call 
science.

Your trivium/quadrivium dichotomy has about as much meaning as whether 
the course is listed on an odd or an even page in the course catalog. 
Yes, freshman logic is often taught by philosophy professors. The good 
ones know their math quite well, that part of it.

It's been over 30 years since I studied the history of mathematics, but 
the link between logic and math is one of the most important 
developments in that history, and has stuck well in my memory.

Cheers,

Edward
```

```
Mr Reid, you *do* realise how absurd this sounds... so more than 150 years

>Before that, logic was more often considered 
>part of philosophy, but at the same time philosophy was a broader field

Mr Reid, it is obvious that you either neglected to read what I posted 
about the trivium and the quadrivium or that - since it proves your 
baseless assertions wrong - you are choosing to ignore it.

>
>Your trivium/quadrivium dichotomy has about as much meaning as whether 
>the course is listed on an odd or an even page in the course catalog. 

As Wittgenstein tells us - and he lived within the past 150 years! - 
'meaning is the result of interpretation.'  I have demonstrated a 
precedent for logic being separated from mathematical subjects in such a 
way as to put it into another order entire... you have made assertions and 
a few insults.

>Yes, freshman logic is often taught by philosophy professors. The good 
>ones know their math quite well, that part of it.

Mr Reid, the 'good' philosophy professors also know the inscription which 
was reputed to over the door of Plato's Academy... do you?

>the link between logic and math is one of the most important 
>developments in that history, and has stuck well in my memory.

Mr Reid, nobody said there were no 'link(s) between logic and math', I 
stated that according to the ranking of the subjects in the trivium and 
the quadrivium that  - unlike your assertion - logic is *not* a 'branch of 
mathematics'.

Might you be able to counter this with something which resembles 'fact'... 
or shall your opinions be as dismissable as all other mere opinions?

DD
```

```
Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net> wrote in message
news:01HW.B9409D610161DA4310E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com...
> On Thu, 27 Jun 2002 9:43:01 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote
> > Please be so kind as to document it or
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> expand your view, there's no point in my expending the effort to help.

-----------
Copyright � 1996 P. F. Collier, L. P.  All rights reserved.

Symbolic logic, or mathematical logic, is the science of correct
inference, especially as developed by use of mathematical methods
and with the help of special symbols that enables one to avoid much
of the ambiguity of ordinary language.

This field of study arose in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries
as an outgrowth from both traditional mathematics and the ancient
logic of Aristotle. It joined these hitherto diverse subjects into
a single subject, or at least provided them with a common foundation.

Symbolic logic seeks to attain all the rigor and clarity of the most
exact mathematics, but without the restrictions that limit ordinary
mathematics to dealing only with numbers and numerical relationships.
-----------

It seems clear to me that "logic" is not a branch of mathematics,
as was claimed.

I can not decide whether "symbolic logic" is a branch of matematics,
a branch of logic, or a separate field.

> I'm sure everyone still reading this thread -- both of them -- is
> getting a bit tired of both of us.

I will not guess about the sentiment of the other person reading
this thread, but I am not yet tired.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-06-27T12:43:43-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<afffbv$if4$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <aff4p5$b8v$1@panix1.panix.com> <01HW.B9409D610161DA4310E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com> <uhmd3ke4cf6e68@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <uhmd3ke4cf6e68@corp.supernews.com>,
Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
>Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net> wrote in message
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>a branch of logic, or a separate field.

Hmmmmm... this gets kinda dicey, as 'ordinary language' is being used to 
discuss that which 'enables one to avoid much of the ambiguity of ordinary 
language'... but hey, if it were easy then everyone would be doing it!

According to Collier (op cit), symbolic logic 'joined these hitherto 
diverse subjects (mathematics and logic) into a single subject, or at 
least provided them with a common foundation'.

On the one hand... steel is diverse from glass (using 'diverse' in the 
sense of 'composed of distinct or unlike elements'); when the two are 
combined into a window it is not common to speak of the resulting subject 
as 'window, which is a branch of steel' or 'window, which is a branch of 
glass'.

On the other hand... the subject which is the matured result of the 
combining of diverse haploid gametes into a diploid zygote is often spoken 
of as being 'a branch of the family tree'.

But hey, this is only the result of my limited view of logic.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-27T14:29:46-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uhmm4ftih9po5b@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <aff4p5$b8v$1@panix1.panix.com> <01HW.B9409D610161DA4310E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com> <uhmd3ke4cf6e68@corp.supernews.com> <afffbv$if4$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:afffbv$if4$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <uhmd3ke4cf6e68@corp.supernews.com>,
> Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
> >
[snip]
> >
> >I can not decide whether "symbolic logic" is a branch of matematics,
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> But hey, this is only the result of my limited view of logic.

The use of 'branch' seems central to understanding the relationships.

By analogy to a river and its tributaries (branches), one might argue
that both mathematics and logic are 'branches' of symbolic logic!
However, this is absurd.

Therefore, I accept that symbolic logic is a separate field and
not a branch of either mathematics or logic.

If it is true that computer programming is a 'branch' of symbolic
logic (as implied by Mr. Reid), then it is not true that computer
programming is a branch of [applied] mathematics, as Dijksta
claimed. This supports Mr. Trembley's statement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

```
In article <uhmm4ftih9po5b@corp.supernews.com>,
Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:afffbv$if4$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>However, this is absurd.

But that's what makes it fun!... errrrr... I mean, this is obviously the 
result of a limited view of logic, bound by such antiquated concepts as 
'The cause must precede the effect' and 'The earlier must antedate the 
latter'.

What comes next (or previous)... adherance to that dreary old Aristotelean 
dictium that something cannot both be and not-be the same thin in regards 
to the same aspect at the same time?

>
>Therefore, I accept that symbolic logic is a separate field and
>not a branch of either mathematics or logic.

Insofar as the joining of two disciplines (mathematics and logic) can 
create a discipline which is something 'other' than a branch of said 
disciplines... well, what I have studied as language, logic and 
mathematics agrees with the accepting of this possibility.

>
>If it is true that computer programming is a 'branch' of symbolic
>logic (as implied by Mr. Reid), then it is not true that computer
>programming is a branch of [applied] mathematics, as Dijksta
>claimed. This supports Mr. Trembley's statement.

But... can't you *see*, man? Dijkstra *said* it was thus!  If one starts 
ignoring the pronouncements of The Ancients in so cavalier a manner what 
comes next... a geometry, perhaps, where parallel lines meet?  A view of 
the Universe which does not involve Absolute Space and Time?  A race where 
Achilles beats the tortoise?

This is absurdity and ugliness... parallel lines do not meet, because 
postulated his geometry thus... and Absolute Space and Time were mandated 
by Newton himself... and Zeno demonstrated that Achilles never gets 
there...

... and Dijkstra, being the first soi-disant Computer Engineer, must be 
duly ensconced in this League of the Greats...

... and I am the King of England.  God save the Me!

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-06-28T02:14:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c21e49$6fd6f700$fe95f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <uhmd3ke4cf6e68@corp.supernews.com> <afffbv$if4$1@panix1.panix.com> <uhmm4ftih9po5b@corp.supernews.com> <affn9r$qgp$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
It is not just logic that relates programming to mathematics.  From The Art
of Computer Programming, Vol 1. Fundamental Algorithms, page 1 to 9, some
selected items:

the notion of algorithm is basic to all computer programming

5 properties of algorithms
  finiteness,   definiteness,   input,   output, effectiveness

finally on page 8 he gives a formal mathematical definition of algorithm

As to whether or note programming is " a branch" of mathematics I leave
that up to others, as I find unless the terms are defined and agreed upon,
the arguments go on with each one seeing mostly their own perspective and
very little of the other persons.  Nonetheless their is a strong
relantionship.

docdwarf@panix.com wrote in article <affn9r$qgp$1@panix1.panix.com>...
> In article <uhmm4ftih9po5b@corp.supernews.com>,
> Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
```

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:affn9r$qgp$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <uhmm4ftih9po5b@corp.supernews.com>,
> Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
> >
[snip]
> >
> >If it is true that computer programming is a 'branch' of symbolic
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> comes next... [...]

Advances in computer programming?

"By means of mathematics we purchase a great ease of
manipulation at the cost of a certain loss of complexity of
content. If we ever forget this cost, and it is easy for it to
fall to the back of our minds, then the very ease with which
we manipulate symbols may be our undoing. All I am saying
is that mathematics in any of its applied fields is a wonderful
servant but a very bad master; it is so good a servant that
there is a tendency for it to become an unjust steward and
usurp the master's place."

Kenneth Boulding, as quoted in Gerald M. Weinberg, "An
Introduction to General Systems Thinking," John Wiley & Sons,
1975, page 105.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-06-27T15:01:33-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VMKS8.5154$%N4.1551187@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <aff4p5$b8v$1@panix1.panix.com> <01HW.B9409D610161DA4310E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com> <uhmd3ke4cf6e68@corp.supernews.com> <afffbv$if4$1@panix1.panix.com> <uhmm4ftih9po5b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message

> If it is true that computer programming is a 'branch' of symbolic
> logic (as implied by Mr. Reid), then it is not true that computer
> programming is a branch of [applied] mathematics, as Dijksta
> claimed. This supports Mr. Trembley's statement.

Back at the begining of this, someone asked if cabinet making or house
building would be considered part of applied mathmatics.  I asked my roomy,
who frames houses for living, since his first degree is in music, and his
second in philosophy.  He replied that any carpenter knows three dimensional
geometry was developed by an boss trying to build houses so they would not
fall down.

I think he is correct.  The applied math came first, and the theoretical
math later.  Early mathmatics was obviously a branch of carpentry, just as
most modern logic is a branch of programming. You guys have just let all the
artsie fartsie academics con you.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-28T06:15:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D1BFF1D.C8698057@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <aff4p5$b8v$1@panix1.panix.com> <01HW.B9409D610161DA4310E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com> <uhmd3ke4cf6e68@corp.supernews.com> <afffbv$if4$1@panix1.panix.com> <uhmm4ftih9po5b@corp.supernews.com> <VMKS8.5154$%N4.1551187@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> building would be considered part of applied mathmatics.  

I think I was that miscreant.

> I asked my roomy,
> who frames houses for living, since his first degree is in music, and his
> second in philosophy.  He replied that any carpenter knows three dimensional
> geometry was developed by an boss trying to build houses so they would not
> fall down.

But we could just as easily say that building a house is purely an
engineering task.  Certainly there is arithmetic involved, but it's a
problem of stresses and loads.  It requires a knowledge of the quality
of materials and construction techniques, their strengths and
weaknesses.  Even without using arithmetic, builders would have
learned by trial and error that certain construction methods resulted
in a more durable structure than others. 

> 
> I think he is correct.  The applied math came first, and the theoretical
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald

Well, I certainly don't want to start a flame war against academics
just because I think Dijkstra is wrong about COBOL.  By the way, I
tend to agree with you that COBOL is more likely a branch of
philology.

I hear a lot of talk about software engineering, and I think we're
kidding ourselves.  How often does a building or a bridge fall down of
its own weight?  Ever see a wing fall off a jetliner while it's parked
on the runway?  If we examine the reliability of software to the
engineering structures of modern bridges, skyscrapers, and airplanes,
we don't look very good by comparison.

How often do you see a skycraper construction project exceed its
budget by 300%, and then be abandoned with nothing to show for it? 
But it's fairly common with software projects.  If software
"engineering" is what we're striving for, we have a very long way to
go.  It's a different problem from the choice of programming
language.  

I'm suspicious of theoretical approaches.  We should look at
successful projects and try to figure out what really works and what
doesn't.  COBOL might not be perfect, but it's a pretty good tool for
a certain set of problems.  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-28T07:59:50-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uhojletks9b82e@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <aff4p5$b8v$1@panix1.panix.com> <01HW.B9409D610161DA4310E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com> <uhmd3ke4cf6e68@corp.supernews.com> <afffbv$if4$1@panix1.panix.com> <uhmm4ftih9po5b@corp.supernews.com> <VMKS8.5154$%N4.1551187@news20.bellglobal.com> <3D1BFF1D.C8698057@worldnet.att.net>`

```

Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:3D1BFF1D.C8698057@worldnet.att.net...
[snip]
>
> I hear a lot of talk about software engineering, and I think we're
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> we don't look very good by comparison.

"When compared to other engineering disciplines, software
engineering does not come out looking good. But this should
not be surprising, because in at least two respects, software
is different from the physical objects, materials, and systems
with which traditional engineers work.

"First, in physical systems smooth changes in inputs usually
produce smooth changes in outputs. That is, most physical
systems are continuous. This allows the behavior of the
system to be determined by testing only certain inputs, and
using extrapolation and interpolation to determine the
behaviors for untested inputs.

"Software systems are, by their very nature, discontinuous. A
small change in input may change the outcomes at several
decision points within the software, causing very different
execution paths and major changes in output behavior. As a
result, using extrapolation and interpolation to estimate
output behaviors for untested inputs is risky at best, and
exceedingly dangerous at worst.

"Software differs from physical systems in another way: its
complexity. Much of the functionality of modern systems is
provided by software; therefore, much of the complexity of
these systems is expressed in the software, also. The greater
the complexity, the more likely design flaws - flaws in the
intellectual construction of the system that cause it to do the
wrong thing under some conditions - are to occur. Design
flaws are the only way that software can go wrong; software
does not wear out like physical components. Thus, to ensure
that a software system does what it is intended to do, design
flaws must be handled in some way."[1]

> How often do you see a skycraper construction project exceed its
> budget by 300%, and then be abandoned with nothing to show for it?
> But it's fairly common with software projects.  If software
> "engineering" is what we're striving for, we have a very long way to
> go. [...]

Failure is more frequent in large projects, than small.

"Software is notorious for being late in delivery and
unpredictable and unreliable in operation. According to a
1994 article by Wayt Gibbs, 'Studies have shown that for
every six new large-scale software systems that are put into
operation, two others are cancelled. The average software
development project overshoots its schedule by half; larger
projects generally do worse. And three quarters of all large
systems are operating failures that either do not function as
intended or are not used at all.'[2]"[1]

> I'm suspicious of theoretical approaches.  We should look at
> successful projects and try to figure out what really works and what
> doesn't. [...]

The term "Software Engineering" was first used in 1968.
For more than 30 years, software engineers have been looking
at successful(?) projects trying "to figure out what really works
and what doesn't." For most of that 30 years, I have been reading
their opinions about what worked here and what worked there.
I think Weinberg[3] provided the answer in 1971. While he did not
say it directly, it may be inferred from his book, that If the right
people come together -- at the right time -- on the right project,
it will be successful. Furthermore, any mismatch (with people)
will allow the creation of a less than successful system.

What the theoretical approaches attempt, is to allow creation
of successful systems in the presence of these mismatches.
This is more important in large systems because the larger
number of people increases the probability of mismatches.
People are not as interchangable as project managers would
like!
----------
[1] C. Michael Holloway, "WHY ENGINEERS SHOULD
CONSIDER FORMAL METHODS,"
< http://techreports.larc.nasa.gov/ltrs/PDF/1997/mtg/NASA-97-16dasc-cmh.pdf
>,
1997.
[2] W. Wayt Gibbs, "Software's Chronic Crisis," Scientific
American, pages 86-95, September 1994.
[3] Gerald M. Weinberg, "The Psychology of Computer
Programming," Van Nostrand Reinhold, 1971.
```

```
This is sorta kinda almost what I was taught, e'er-so-long ago; I do not 
have any documentation other than that of my admittedly porous memory's 
ability to recall what my instructor said but he told the class something 
along the lines of that for large systems - 'large' being defined as 
60,000 lines of code or more - 70% were never put into production.

(The next COBOL-semester's instructor told the class that 'IBM had done a 
study' - which, of course, gave what followed the aura of Divine Writ - 
which showed that when a new concept was introduced in a classroom 20% of 
the students grasped the concept and could relate it to other material, 
20% of the students would *not* grasp the concept and relate it...

... and 60% of the students were thinking about sex.

For the rest of the semester, of course, every time I wanted clarification 
or repetition or the like I would raise my hand and say 'Excuse me, I'm 
sorry, I was thinking about sex... might I ask about... ')

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-28T12:21:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uhp2u646mdcqf7@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <VMKS8.5154$%N4.1551187@news20.bellglobal.com> <3D1BFF1D.C8698057@worldnet.att.net> <uhojletks9b82e@corp.supernews.com> <afhm0k$3d4$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:afhm0k$3d4$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <uhojletks9b82e@corp.supernews.com>,
> Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> ><
http://techreports.larc.nasa.gov/ltrs/PDF/1997/mtg/NASA-97-16dasc-cmh.pdf
> >>,
> >1997.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 60,000 lines of code or more - 70% were never put into production.

The Gibbs article has a graph relating U.S. Average
Cancellation Probability versus project size in function points.
The graph shows a 50%+ probability at more than 10,240
function points. The article "Research Issues in the Renovation
of Legacy Systems" from members of the University of
Amsterdam, Programming Research Group, states that
each function point requires about 107 lines of COBOL.
This suggests that projects using COBOL would, on average,
need more than 1,000,000 lines of code to reach a 50%
probability for cancellation. Relating the 60,000 lines to
560 function points shows a 20% cancellation rate. This
seems a remarkable improvement from "e'er-so-long ago."

I have no up-to-date source that relates 'large' to lines of
code. The article "Very Large Projects" from Datamation,
January 15, 1986, states: "Very large projects are not
quantitatively defined. They are qualitatively defined within
an IS environment." The Gibbs article talks mostly about
very large projects despite having used 'large' as a point
of reference.
```

```
from failures.
```

- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <aff4p5$b8v$1@panix1.panix.com> <01HW.B9409D610161DA4310E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com> <uhmd3ke4cf6e68@corp.supernews.com> <afffbv$if4$1@panix1.panix.com> <uhmm4ftih9po5b@corp.supernews.com> <VMKS8.5154$%N4.1551187@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
On Thu, 27 Jun 2002 15:01:33 -0400, "Donald Tees"
<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> enlightened us:

>"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Au contraire Monsieur Tees.  This is almost a chicken or egg situation
but I would think theory came before applied mathematics.  Generally
applied math is no more than the proof of a theory.  Somewhere,
someone speculated (theorized) that 1 + 1 = 2.  Until numbering
systems and proof that 1 + 1 did indeed = 2, it was only a theory most
likely stated in the words as:  when a group is put with another group
the new group is the sum of the seperate groups...or something like
that!

I believe that logic is a separate entity from mathematics but in many
cases you need logic to prove a theory.  An algorithm is a set of
logical steps.

And as for your statement that mathematics was a branch of carpentry,
I don't think those cave builders ever needed a carpenter.  A stone
mason maybe, but never a carpenter.

Regards,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>

```
feed and two a long way before they developed numbers, and were quite able

theory, and lack of any arithmetic.  etc.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-06-28T16:50:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<afi44b$hr7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <aff4p5$b8v$1@panix1.panix.com> <01HW.B9409D610161DA4310E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com> <uhmd3ke4cf6e68@corp.supernews.com> <afffbv$if4$1@panix1.panix.com> <uhmm4ftih9po5b@corp.supernews.com> <VMKS8.5154$%N4.1551187@news20.bellglobal.com> <CC4B72B25C3F9867.A3F85A42452C6E76.860FDA2141705A1C@lp.airnews.net>`

```

On 28-Jun-2002, SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com> wrote:

> Au contraire Monsieur Tees.  This is almost a chicken or egg situation
> but I would think theory came before applied mathematics.  Generally
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> that!

I very much doubt if the theory of addition occurred before the fact. 
People just counted two things separately and counted two things together. 
After a while people noticed that they don't need to count them twice, but
whenever two groups of one were combined, the new count was 2.  As things
got more complex, theories were needed to supply rules for what was not
obvious.  The rules extended what could be done with numbers.

On a side note - a couple of years ago an anthropologist was touring the
Mediterranean. He noticed that lots of temples to gods used to be temples to
goddesses.  After looking into it he determined that the switch occurred
about when the region containing a temple became literate.   His theory was
that before literacy, women's superior social skills allowed them to find
consensus for ruling issues.  After literacy, these skills weren't as
important as we could have rules based society.  Skills that men predominate
in, such as warfare became more dominant and men became rulers.
```

- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <aff4p5$b8v$1@panix1.panix.com> <01HW.B9409D610161DA4310E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com> <uhmd3ke4cf6e68@corp.supernews.com> <afffbv$if4$1@panix1.panix.com> <uhmm4ftih9po5b@corp.supernews.com> <VMKS8.5154$%N4.1551187@news20.bellglobal.com> <CC4B72B25C3F9867.A3F85A42452C6E76.860FDA2141705A1C@lp.airnews.net>`

```


SkippyPB wrote:

> On Thu, 27 Jun 2002 15:01:33 -0400, "Donald Tees"
> <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> enlightened us:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Oxford Concise supports this - logic can be summed up as 'reasoning', the ability
to 'figure out' - no mention made of mathematics.

>
> And as for your statement that mathematics was a branch of carpentry,
> I don't think those cave builders ever needed a carpenter.  A stone
> mason maybe, but never a carpenter.
>

Oh yes they did. Probably called him a 'wood mason' back when Raquel Welch just
wore a thong, (what a BOOTIFUL thought - see Doc, you're not the only one who
thinks about sex <G>).

Our forebears, having learned they were safer living in caves, even if the
particular cave had the title Chez Bruin, realized they had to keep out the
previous occupants - or finish up as lunch or supper. Just like the Masai they
probably started by using prickly bushes, then teams probably jumped up and down
on a branch of a tree until it snapped - these could be used as a more sturdy
defence. Their skills with stone masonry probably came later, having spent
decades just chipping away at flint to give them the elementary stone mason's
tools. (My own thoughts - hey I'm no paleontologist !).

I endorse Don's 'artsie fartsie' reference. Too many self proclaimed gurus out
there turning the obvious into theoretical crap with appropriate buzz words or
're-minted' words to support their arguments.

And if programming was solely related to mathematics - count me out !. A little
tale - way back when I was an apprentice in the RAF, I was short listed as a
prospective candidate to go the RAF College at Cranwell, (equivalent to West
Point). My commanding officer said to me, "I understand your math is poor. What
is 2.5% of ï¿½1 ?". If he hadn't given me the answer, (which I have never forgotten
<G>), I'd still be sat staring at him across his desk, covered in cobwebs. You go
figure what 2.5% is - a ï¿½  then had 240 pence.

Very specifically COBOL is a language just like English or German and a
communication tool. It may well be backed up by mathematical thinking at the
earliest stages, (although if you dumb it down, ones and noughts are no more
complex than the sequencing for Morse code), but to requote Michael., 'It is the
artist not the paintbrush".

Being no Einstein, I can still apply logic or reasoning to a problem - but you
wont want to recruit me to dream up some new Sort/Merge techniques - give that
one to the mathematical boffins !.

PS: Did go for selection, but didn't make it to Cranwell. I guess they figured if
you didn't know what 2.5% was, that you wouldn't make a very good 'driver' for an
airplane/aeroplane <G>.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <VMKS8.5154$%N4.1551187@news20.bellglobal.com> <CC4B72B25C3F9867.A3F85A42452C6E76.860FDA2141705A1C@lp.airnews.net> <3D1CEAB2.D7F1E956@shaw.ca>`

```
In article <3D1CEAB2.D7F1E956@shaw.ca>, James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
>
>SkippyPB wrote:

[snippage]

>> I believe that logic is a separate entity from mathematics but in many
>> cases you need logic to prove a theory.  An algorithm is a set of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>to 'figure out' - no mention made of mathematics.

Gah... if you want to get so radical (in the sense of 'going to the root') 
then you can skip merrily past my references to the trivium and the 
quadrivium and home in on the Ancient Greek.

'Logos' is a *very* complicated word... depending on the context it can be 
translated as 'word', 'account' (as in 'to give an account of'), 'reason' 
(as in 'cause for existence'), 'speech' (both the act-of-speaking and 
those things given after a testimonial dinner), 'discourse', 'ratio' 
(Euclid refers to the 'logos' between two lines)... and all that kind of 
stuff.

'Mathematics', as mentioned before, is from 'manthano', 'to know', hence 
'manthanomai', the 'knowable things'.  As for 'what is it that can be 
known'... this is a whole 'yuther question; it should be rather plain that 
the language shows little relation between the words.

>
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>thinks about sex <G>).

Mr Gavan, 'thinking about it' is something that most folks can do... now, 
actually *doing* something about it... !

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** spamdump@nospam.noway.nohow (Ed Stevens)
- **Date:** 2002-06-27T13:14:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d1b0d13.4610389@ausnews.austin.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3CF47911.189318CE@worldnet.att.net> <DDB1600CF8008320.A59EBC97492D6A6F.FC43E6956D137C6B@lp.airnews.net> <3CF5CC2A.FF040E5C@worldnet.att.net> <01HW.B93F587701158E4210E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com> <3D1AC228.D344D050@worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Thu, 27 Jun 2002 07:43:34 GMT, Arnold Trembley
<arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

<snip quote of previous posts>

>I think you have it exactly backwards.  Every tool I am aware of, from
>a stone axe to a COBOL compiler, is the product of human thought.  The
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It's not that the tool creates the user, but once the user has the tool, he sees
problems and solutions based on the capabilites of the tool.  The old "if your
only tool is a hammer, then all your problems look like nails" thing.

Or, in our own business, the tool used to be an 80-column punch card.  Even
after we learned to put records on magnetic media,  how long did it take us to
figure out that we were not bound by the 80-character limitation?  How many of
our naming standards are influenced by the limits/capablities of TSO/SPF?  How
many program specs are written by a person who already sees (conceptualizes) the
solution in a specific language?  Yes, the tools are the product of human
thought, but the tools also very much influence human thought.

My 2-cents worth.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-27T09:26:25-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B9408AC1015D7AA610E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3CF47911.189318CE@worldnet.att.net> <DDB1600CF8008320.A59EBC97492D6A6F.FC43E6956D137C6B@lp.airnews.net> <3CF5CC2A.FF040E5C@worldnet.att.net> <01HW.B93F587701158E4210E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com> <3D1AC228.D344D050@worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Thu, 27 Jun 2002 3:43:34 -0400, Arnold Trembley wrote
> I think you have it exactly backwards.  Every tool I am aware of, from
> a stone axe to a COBOL compiler, is the product of human thought.  The
> tool couldn't exist without the person who created it.  I'm surprised
> that anyone can believe the tool creates the user.

You imply that humans have complete control over the effects of our 
actions. You give us far, far too much credit for competence. The state 
of the world as a whole is a strong indication that we cannot control 
the effects of what we create -- indeed that we don't even know at the 
point of creation what those effects will be.

Stone axes weren't invented to kill people, but they have been used 
thus nonetheless. And when people learned that they could kill other 
people with stone axes, they started thinking about killing other 
people on a scale not previously imagined. The tool affected their 
thinking in a profound and devious way -- and a way that the creator of 
the stone axe never imagined or intended.

> It's the artist, not the paintbrush.

Most artists would tell you that they have a love/hate relationship 
with their tools. The tools allow them to create but also restrict the 
ways in which they think about creating, and thus direct their 
creations. Many artists fight constantly against the very tools that 
they use.

Creation is not a one-way street. The creation and the creator 
interact. This is the very nature of creation.

Edward Reid
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-27T11:18:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uhmatinlmnqc1b@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3CF47911.189318CE@worldnet.att.net> <DDB1600CF8008320.A59EBC97492D6A6F.FC43E6956D137C6B@lp.airnews.net> <3CF5CC2A.FF040E5C@worldnet.att.net> <01HW.B93F587701158E4210E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com> <3D1AC228.D344D050@worldnet.att.net>`

```

Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:3D1AC228.D344D050@worldnet.att.net...
> Edward Reid wrote:
> >
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> that anyone can believe the tool creates the user.

It is not the tool but rather the use of the tool that influences
our thinking. Wiliam A. Wulf expressed it differently,
"There is an implicit Whorfian hypothesis that the nature
of language shapes the ways in which we think about
problems. Thus, although we may not be able to measure
it directly, most experts believe that the user of one of the
more modern, structured languages is better equipped
to think about complex problems than the user of one
of the older languages (e.g., Fortran)."[1]

Also,
"The antistandardization argument holds that language
evolution is essential to incorporating new concepts into
the field, and further that the most important impact of
language is not easily observed because it is in the
mental set of its users."[1]

COBOL 74 provided a "vocabulary" and means for
structuring solutions. COBOL 85 extended both
"vocabulary" and structure. The same applies to
COBOL 2002. It is this "vocabulary" and structure
that shapes the ways in which we think about solutions
to problems when using these particular tools.

One who knows only COBOL 74, is less likely to use
"structured programming," "modular programming," or
recursive data structures than one who knows the later
COBOL standards, or who knows other languages.
Thus, one's use of a tool (COBOL 74) influences one's
thinking habits. The absence, from a language, of
constructs, for expressing some concepts, limits our
ability to think about solutions when using the language.

Therefore, in general,
"The tools we use have a profound ... influence on our
thinking habits, and, therefore, on our thinking abilities."

But, it also seems true that evolutionary improvements
in a language have a positive influence on our thinking
habits and on our thinking abilities.

[1] William A. Wulf, "Trends in the Design and
     Implementation of Programming Languages,"
    Selected Reprints in Software, IEEE Computer
    Society, 1982, 21
```

```
the Data Processing Industry that make us think.

Why, then, do these kind of discussions always seem to degrade into personal
attacks?
Why can't we just state our opinions, our position behind those opinions,
and reply to others thoughts without attacking each other?

I've never really understood this kind of behaviour in Newsgroups.  This
would have been a very different conversation if it had been held face to
face at a party.

Children . . . Behave!

Buddy Ray
```
