```
The next time my phone rings at 2:00 a.m. I'll have them phone you :).  I
```

```
at
> 4:59pm.  We mainframers are petitioning for a larger refridgerator, a

> and some cots in the back room.

I expect I make peanuts compared to most of the programers in here, but I do
have a twelve foot pool table, an upright grand piano, two guitars and a
fiddle in my office.  And the time to play and listen to them all.  I have
nice long (16 foot) desk designed to hold six PCs, and enough floor space
left to teach my grand-daugher to ride a two wheeler.

I do get a fair bit extra time since I can walk to my office from my
apartment (it is through one doorway, actually). I have to declare $300 a
month for the apartment, but it is nice to have a tiny bedroom off my office
to sleep in.  Saves commuting and having to own an expensive place to live,
and enables me to live on a miniscule salary.  Good bussiness too.  I only
have to bill out four days a month to pay all the bills.

That leaves me about twenty working days a month to work on my own code.
Stuff that I decided to write because I want to see it work, and sometimes
just because I like to write code.  It helps me think, when I have to
actually produce the code.  Prevents sloppy thinking.  Besides, I like to
program.  Thats why I got into the business.

I think all most programmers need to get a better grasp of reality, as well
as better tax accountants.

Donald
```

```
<grin> I have been reading this thread for more than week now, with great amusement. :)

I can only think of a couple things to do -

(1) Subvert the Java/C/Ada/Pascal/Perl/C++ etc programmers. Of course, to do that, we would have
to learn what those languages do better than mainframe COBOL, and why they are not commonly used on the Mainframe.

(2) Come up with "new and exciting" things to do with Mainframe COBOL.  Yes, I know the arguments
about 'real work' vs. playing around. I even agree with it to a degree. But this is the real world, if you cannot
get people excited about what you are doing, you are sooner or later going to be doing something else.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-02T05:43:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TfiK8.177298$Q42.7991729@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<ad97dt$h2d$1@panix1.panix.com> <20020601101458.14029.00001738@mb-ma.aol.com> <%q5K8.175965$Q42.7875673@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CF8F57B.51D87D5@shaw.ca>`

```
It really isn't anything to be sad about.  With more than a billion lines of running code out there,
COBOL is not going to stop being used for quite a while.

But we COBOL programmer types (and mainframe types, and CICS types, and so forth) really
do give the impression that we know it all, and have the best possible environment. That attitude
serves as nothing but a challenge to the people who really have an agenda out there.

For example, it is certainly possible to write a business system, such as an accounting system,
using Java and a good database, such as DB/2. It is not even that difficult to do it. In a lot of ways,
it is easier to do it in Java than in COBOL. And yes, it would be fast, reliable, and even efficient.
(Not as efficient as COBOL, in the same ways that COBOL is not as efficient as assembler, but
still plenty fast for the user's, and easy to maintain for the programmers.)

IBM at least, has recognized this and is trying to do something about it without cutting their own throats. Note the very high
interest at IBM for Linux.

So again, while it may be a little sad that the 'old world' is sooner or later going to go away, there
really isn't anything to be sad about. A bunch of new possibilities are opening up and who knows
where they will lead. I would not lay money against COBOL, or some new variant of it, being
right there in the thick of it. :)

"JamesJ. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:3CF8F57B.51D87D5@shaw.ca...

…[55 more quoted lines elided]…
```


###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-03T23:37:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y4TK8.66648$9z5.4325413@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<ad97dt$h2d$1@panix1.panix.com> <20020601101458.14029.00001738@mb-ma.aol.com> <%q5K8.175965$Q42.7875673@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CF8F57B.51D87D5@shaw.ca> <TfiK8.177298$Q42.7991729@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3CFBC57A.5070701@optonline.net>`

```
What does a kid who knows he is overmatched do? He blusters. Same with some of those types you
have ran into. They have the talk, but they can't even crawl, much less walk. ;)

And believe me, there is nothing worse than someone who thinks they know all there is to know
about an industry from a single platform point of view. There are a few of those types out there on
the mainframe side. They are quite as incapable of doing anything as those C/C++ guys.

The guys who owns the company I work for has it right though;

You are what you are because that is what you think you are.
If you don't like what you are, change what you think you are,
and you *will* change what you are.

It sounds pretty trite, but it is a powerful truth.

-Paul
```

```
Richard Plinston wrote:

> Liam Devlin wrote:
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Okay, Richard, you're a lot older than I:-)

I worked for IBM 1965-70 in a new business office, so I installed many 
punched card systems, e.g., 402, 026/029, 083 sorter, 085 collator, 
etc., in my first year there (before I went off to computer training). 
Wired a LOT of boards.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-06-01T15:58:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020601115805.22552.00001825@mb-mg.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20020601101458.14029.00001738@mb-ma.aol.com>`

```
>From: scomstock@aol.com  (S Comstock)
>Date: 2002-06-01 10:14 Eastern Daylight Time

>Hey, Doc, here's an idea. What if every time we do maintenance on a program
>that puts out a report or display, we add the line: "This data brought to you
>by a COBOL program." ? Wonder what the reactions would be.

I think you are joking, but it's really not such a bad idea.  I think I
could slip the word "COBOL" into a printout without attracting too
much attention.  I think I'll try it.  Right in front of the program name
would be a good place.  Instead of 
"Program P02027 March 31, 2002, etc." it will say
"COBOL Program P02027 March 31, 2002, etc." 
Even if the system gets converted, the end-users will have gotten
used to seeing it, and will insist that the word be included on
their new printouts. 

Incidentally, I've been thinking over my earlier remark about 2:00 a.m.  
It was rubbish, and I retract it.  
It is indeed the COBOL mainframers who get more late night calls.
I think that's because there are more mission-critical programs running
mainframe COBOL.  System failures tend to be a result of recent
changes, and there is some possibility of getting things up and
running within a reasonable time frame (measured in hours).  The
failures of c/s o/o efforts tend to be attempts to launch entire
systems; the costs tend to be measured in millions of dollars and
the time frames are measured in months or years, and there is
no point in calling someone at midnight for that.  

What I was trying to say was that the c/s programmers are often
granted the luxury of working directly on production data or 
production programs that are misbehaving without going through 
so much red tape.  

Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL "people"

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-06-02T17:12:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c20a58$30632940$e3ccf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Please see my comments embedded below.

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in article
<acuje5$s4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>...
> Given some private conversations, I would be interested in some input
from
> any of "you" who are actually doing "day-to-day" coding in COBOL on IBM
> mainframe environments.  I am *particularly* interested in those who are
> employees rather than "contractors" or "consultants" - but any input that
I
> can get will be gratefully received. (Please pass this on, if there are
> others you know that you think might provide me with some "real-world"
> input.)
> 

I have been a mainframe COBOL programmer since 1973, primarily as an
employee for the U.S. government (25 years), once as a consultant to the
government, and once as an employee in the private sector.  In the past I
have done a lot of work in IBM mainframe assembler, VSAM and several dbms,
but the last 10 years have been primarily COBOL, CICS, and Datacomm/DB
dbms.  I have a MS in computer science from Johns Hopkins University.  I
consider myself "technically oriented" with more interest in computer
languages and algorithms than in mostly boring applications (with some
exceptions).

> It seems to me that few IBM mainframe COBOL programmers are planning on
> attending the upcoming (end of June) "COBOL Expo 2002".  I have some
guesses
> of my own on why this might be, but I am interested in input from others.
 I
> am also interested in any "relevant" input on any of these topics:
> 

While I am are of this event I really have not investigated it to determine
if what is being offered would be worth the time and expense.  If I were to
attend I would have to take personal leave and pay all of the expenses
myself.  While I could afford to do this I am uncertain that it would be as
cost effective as other ways of learning. My agency does not make technical
training of employees a priority.  Most of the programmers here are
consultants and it is felt that they should provide for their own training.
 Taking courses at local colleges and/or the Department of Agriculture
Graduate School gives me more targeted learning for my buck.  Also I am a
book reader and have learned a lot that way.  Books are very cost
effective.


> 1) It seems to me that VERY few IBM mainframe COBOL programmers
> "participate" in the comp.lang.cobol Usenet newsgroup or the IBM-MAIN
> list-server.  Why do you think this is?  I can think of a few
possibilities
> (NOT mutually exclusive), but I don't know how realistic they are:
>   - IBM mainframe COBOL programmers want to put in a "day's work" and
then
> stop thinking about COBOL
>  - IBM mainframe COBOL programmers are "old-timers" who are just
"waiting"
> for the day they can retire and aren't interested in other forums for
> discussing "recent" or "new" techniques and issues
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>  - The estimates of how many programmers there really are still doing
COBOL
> coding on IBM mainframes is really highly exaggerated.
>  - others?
> 

Many people end up in jobs that they do not love.  I think this is
particularly true of the computer programming field as advancement can be
fairly rapid.  Before you know it you can find yourself making more money
than is possible in other fields.  Those fields may have more interest to
you but it is hard to give up the money.  Most programmers that I have
known have not had programming as a primary interest.  They put in their
day and then devote their time to other things that they like better. 

For myself, even though programming is a primary interest, most of what I
do at work is not that challenging.  One reason I got a MS degree was
because I had to seek outside my work for new things to learn and challenge
me.  Also mainframe COBOL as a language has not changed that much.  I am
not interested in "future upcoming standards coming real soon now" that may
never make it into a compiler that I have access to.

I do most of my internet and email access form home.  Programming also. One
reason is that the company firewall monitors everything and if your access
is not strictly job related you could end up in trouble.  I perfer to avoid
the whole issue by doing most stuff at home. Goverment computers are not
for personal use.  Unless there is a direct relation to your job, you are
taking a chance in programming something interesting that might be of use
someday/sometime.  Plus cutbacks in government have greatly reduced spare
time for such activities.

> 2) From what I hear, the COBOL (and LE) projects at SHARE aren't
> particularly large/active (but certainly more alive than the now defunct
> GUIDE projects).  What *would* interest IBM mainframe COBOL programmers
in
> the way of "presentations," discussions, forums, etc?
> 

No company that I have ever worked for ever sent anyone to Share/Guide
except for systems programmers.  

> 3) How much of the time of IBM mainframe COBOL programmers are spent with
> "mainframe-only/primarily" applications and how much is spent with
> applications that (now) normally have PC-ish "front-ends"?
>

I frequently download/upload data between the maniframe and the PC for my
own purposes and ocassionally for users.  Moslty I work with databases that
contain many millons of records and with transactions that are so heavily
utilized that even a mainframe is pushed to handle peak loads.  This make
PC use a pretty minor component.  I use it for quick printing of short
documents, for some documentation and for realtively small files.  My main
two uses of it are for 3270 terminal emulation and for email.
 
> 4) What do you think can/could be done to "improve" the image (both
> self-image and image from outside) of those doing IBM mainframe COBOL
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

People tend to think that what they know is the best stuff to know.  It
would help if more colleges taught COBOL.  It would help if more
interesting books were written showing how COBOL can be used to do many of
the same things that other languages are use for.  The best chance we have
is that a shortage of COBOL programmers will create a crisis making COBOL
the "hot language of the times".  I know many other computer languages but
COBOL is still the best for traditional business applications. Even with
the advent of the internet and client/server programming I think COBOL will
still have a place on maifraimes on the server side, and with proper
extensions to other parts as well.

> *OBVIOUSLY* some/most of this applies to other mainframe COBOL
programmers,
> but I am particularly interested in those in IBM environments.
> 
> Feel free to respond to me publicly via CLC or IBM-MAIN - or via private
> email.  This is *not* an official (much less paid for) survey - but is
aimed
> at my trying to get in touch with those "on the front line" today. 
Anything
> posted publicly - will obviously be available to others; things sent to
me
> privately will NOT be generally distributed - unless explicitly allowed
by
> the sender.
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Recalling source code. — 8 msgs

### Recalling source code.

- **From:** nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas)
- **Date:** 2002-06-01T00:03:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c02fd744.0205312303.5abd205f@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
          Once by mistake I put the load in the same PDS where my
source is and
finally my source code get screwed. Is there any method of recalling
the source code?

Regards,
Shyam
```

#### ↳ Re: Recalling source code.

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-06-01T09:20:54-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
shyam nivas <nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com> wrote:
>Hi,
>          Once by mistake I put the load in the same PDS where my
>source is and
>finally my source code get screwed. Is there any method of recalling
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Recalling source code.

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-06-01T18:28:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020601142819.28330.00001764@mb-mh.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<adahnm$s4j$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Hi Shy,

I've come across this a few times, but I'll be damned if I can recall the
solution. It's very simple, too!

Have you tried recompiling and linking another pgm using the approprate
libraries?
Give it a shot; I think that's all that's required.

When you made the error the DSCB (or the Catalog entry) of the source lib was
changed to contain the DS attributes of the Link library (RECFM U and a large,
27K+ LRECL).

I'm not sure why a recompile/relink returns them to their original state, but
give it a try.
Sorry I couldn't be more definite.
  
 Jack.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recalling source code.

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamDD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-02T05:45:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF9B0EE.20105@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<adahnm$s4j$1@panix1.panix.com> <20020601142819.28330.00001764@mb-mh.aol.com>`

```
Jnjsle wrote:

> Hi Shy,
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sorry I couldn't be more definite.


You may have an Undelete function in MVS / OS/390 / z/OS. It was on the 
Utility panel, option 3, the last place I worked. If you have it, it'll 
give you a list of every backup, i.e., every Save, of every member 
altered since the last compress, so you'll have to browse thru the list 
to find the most recent copy of your pgm. Good luck.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recalling source code.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-06-02T15:42:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020602114236.06614.00001771@mb-ca.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3CF9B0EE.20105@optonline.net>`

```
OK Shy,

Here's another approach. Since the data content of your source lib has remained
unchanged in spite of your misstep, try copying the library using IEBGENER, or
SYNCSORT or DFSORT or whatever.

Be sure to explicitly define the DCBs for the sending and receiving DDs as:

DCB=(DSORG=PO,RECFM=FB,LRECL=80,BLKSIZE=n) 
where, n = the blocksize of your source lib before the "accident" occured.

I'm sure you'll find the library "restored".

Jack
```

#### ↳ Re: Recalling source code.

- **From:** "Rick Hood" <RickHood@insightbb.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-01T19:01:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ES8K8.79490$ux5.100538@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0205312303.5abd205f@posting.google.com>`

```
Shyam:

You didn't mention what environment you're using, so I will assume that it
is MVS/TSO.  Actually, you're dealing with two separate issues here.  First,
you need to assess the extent of the damage to your source PDS.  Second, you
need to restore your original source.

Source libraries are usually RECFM=FB and LRECL=80 whereas load libraries
are usually RECFM=U and LRECL=0.  If the linkage editor changed the DCB
information on your source PDS or messed it up in any other way, then you
will probably have to restore the entire PDS.  However, if the DCB
information is intact and you can access other members of the PDS you may
only have to restore the individual member.

Assuming that your site uses SMS to manage disk space and that you didn't
create the source member today, you could check to see if there is a backup
copy of the dataset available.  From a TSO ready prompt or ISPF option 6,
enter the following command replacing the sample dataset name with yours of
course:

        HLIST 'userid.source.cobol' BCDS

This command checks the Backup Control Data Set (BCDS) to see if there are
any backup copies of the specified dataset available.  I haven't done this
for a while, so I'm not sure the syntax is exactly right, but you can enter
"HELP HLIST" to verify the syntax.  If the HLIST command shows that there is
a backup available, enter the following command to restore it to a temporary
dataset name:

        HRECOVER DATASET('userid.source.cobol')
                              NEWNAME('userid.source.cobol.temp')
                              GEN(??)

Again, I'm not sure the syntax is exactly right, so you might want to check
it via "HELP HRECOVER".  Check the output of the HLIST command to get the
value for the generation (GEN) number.  After the dataset is restored
successfully, you can either copy the individual member from the temporary
dataset to your original dataset and delete the temporary dataset, or delete
your original dataset and rename the temporary dataset.  Now you should be
back in business!

If you are unable to resolve the situation yourself, you can always ask for
assistance from your technical support team.  That's what they are there
for.

Good luck,
Rick


"shyam nivas" <nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com> wrote in message
news:c02fd744.0205312303.5abd205f@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>           Once by mistake I put the load in the same PDS where my
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Shyam
```

#### ↳ Re: Recalling source code.

- **From:** "Kjeld Hansen" <paabol@12mail.dk>
- **Date:** 2002-06-01T23:15:57+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cf9398d$0$9117$edfadb0f@dspool01.news.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0205312303.5abd205f@posting.google.com>`

```
You can use a utility like PDSFAST or a SAS procedure (can't remember the
name of the proc) to recover overwritten PDS-members, as long as the dataset
hasn't been compressed, and assuming the linkage editor didn't alter the
dataset organisation..

Good luck..

Kjeld Paab�l Hansen


"shyam nivas" <nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com> skrev i en meddelelse
news:c02fd744.0205312303.5abd205f@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>           Once by mistake I put the load in the same PDS where my
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Shyam
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Recalling source code.

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-06-02T04:08:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020602000856.14149.00001972@mb-cj.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3cf9398d$0$9117$edfadb0f@dspool01.news.tele.dk>`

```
Hi Shy,

As I stated before, I've seen this happen on more than one occasion. There is
nothing permanently wrong with the contents of the PDS. The contents are NOT
permanently compromised. The dataset has just been "reblocked".

When you look at a member via ISPF Edit, you'll probably only see the first
card of the program. Maybe some other code, if the pgm is big. This results
from the data being "reblocked" as ~27k Undefined records. If you PF11 across
the record you'll see the rest of the pgm code in the record.

Try what I've suggested. If it doesn't correct the problem, talk to your
sysprog. He's probably encountered it before.

Jack
```

```
Under Windows 98 you have the option or not of running autoexec.bat. 
Also, if your autoexec.bat was complex, then the Install may have not
correctly changed
the path.  Make sure (using MSCONFIG) that you are running the
autoexec.bat - then look at it and see if the path was updated for the
compiler.  I'll be happy to fix up your autoexec.bat file if you email
it to me at this address:

C O B O L 2 4 @ S O F T W A R E S I M P L E . C O M 

(Collapse the spaces)

Do not reply to this news group posting address - email sent there
goes straight to bitland.
```

## Thread: What's new *and* interesting for IBM mainframe COBOL programmers - in the next COBOL Standard — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### What's new *and* interesting for IBM mainframe COBOL programmers - in the next COBOL Standard

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-01T13:29:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<adb3qg$kah$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Given last week's note and the replies that I received, I thought that I
would pass along to comp.lang.cobol and IBM-MAIN a LONG note that I just
sent to some of my IBM and SHARE contacts.

  ***

As I think you know, I have been doing a "totally informal" survey via
IBM-MAIN and comp.lang.cobol concerning status and "interests" of IBM
mainframe COBOL programmers. Although I am still gathering
information/input, I have become thoroughly convinced (and NOT surprised)
that most IBM mainframe COBOL programmers think that the current ('85/'89
Standard) facilities of IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM (ignoring - for the
moment - the enhancements in Enterprise COBOL) really DO meet all their
current needs and desires (as far as "syntax") goes.

Although most of the "press" on the next Standard has centered on OO, I
thought that there really ARE a *bunch* of enhancements that SHOULD interest
mainframe programmers. (Validate and Common Exception Handling come to mind
instantly.) I don't know if this is worth developing as a SHARE
presentation - a "newsletter" (if you ever do one again) article or
something else.

As a starting place for thinking about this, the following are the items
that strike me as "most interesting" to IBM mainframe COBOL programmers
(from my limited perspective) - as they are described in the "Substantive
changes not affecting existing programs" Annex of the FDIS. (IBM already
supports some items in this list; I am *not* including those here)

(Please let me know if you think this is worth pursuing)

NOTE WELL:
 This is a medium-long list, which in and of itself, is pretty interesting.

NOTE 2:
 Nothing in this note is intended to "imply" that there has been any public
indication that IBM will *ever* implement any, much less all, of these
enhancements. Furthermore, some items in this list are in the "processor
dependent" list, so that even if IBM were to provide a "fully conforming"
implementation of the next COBOL Standard, they need not implement all these
items.

NOTE 3:
 Not all IBM mainframe COBOL programmers would agree that ALL these items
are "good enhancements" - but I think that (if implemented by IBM) they are
all "interesting".

***

4) ARITHMETIC clause. The STANDARD phrase specifies that certain arithmetic
will be performed in a well-defined manner and may yield results that are
portable. When standard arithmetic is in effect there is no restriction on
composite of operands.

6) AT END phrase. The AT END phrase of the READ statement does not have to
be specified if there is no applicable USE statement.

7) BINARY and floating point data. Two new representations of numeric data
type are introduced, a binary representation which holds data in a
machine-specific way and is not restricted to decimal ranges of values, and
a floating-point representation. The floating-point type exists both in a
numeric form, with a machine-specific representation, and in a
numeric-edited form.

8) Bit/boolean support. The capability of defining bit strings and setting
or testing boolean values is added. Data of class and category boolean may
be represented as bits, alphanumeric characters, or national characters by
specifying usage bit, display, or national, respectively. Boolean data items
are specified by picture character strings containing the symbol '1';
boolean literals are identified by a separator B", B', BX", or BX'. Boolean
operations B-XOR, B-AND, B-OR, and B-NOT are provided for use in boolean
expressions.

15) CALL statement. Arithmetic expressions and literals may be used as
arguments in a new format of the CALL statement.

21) Coded character sets for national character data. Predefined
alphabet-names UCS-4, UTF-8, and UTF-16 are added to the SPECIAL-NAMES
paragraph for referencing the Universal Multiple-Octet Coded Character Set
(UCS). Additional alphabet-names for national coded character sets can be
defined by the implementor or by the programmer in the SPECIAL-NAMES
paragraph.

22) CODE-SET clause. A code set defined with literals in the SPECIAL-NAMES
paragraph can be specified in the CODE-SET clause for use in conversion on
input and output of data.

23) CODE-SET clause. A CODE-SET clause may specify an alphabet defining a
single-octet (alphanumeric) coded character set, such as ISO/IEC 646, and
multiple-octet (national) coded character set, such as ISO/IEC 10646-1.

24) Collating sequences for national character data. A predefined alphabet,
UCS-4, is added to the
SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph for use in referencing a collating sequence
associated with ISO/IEC 10646-1 and ISO/IEC 10646-2. Additional
alphabet-names for national collating sequences can be defined by the
implementor or by the programmer in the SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph. For each
use of a collating sequence, language is provided for specifying either an
alphanumeric collating sequence or a national collating sequence, or both.

29) Compiler directives. Compiler directives instruct the compiler to take
specific actions during compilation. Compiler directives are provided:
 - to control the source listing
 - to request flagging of syntax that might be incompatible between the
previous COBOL standard and the current standard
 - to specify page ejection
 - to cause propagation of an exception condition to an activating runtime
element
 - to specify whether the reference format of the source text or library
text that follows is fixed-form or free-form
 - to turn checking for certain exception conditions on or off
 - to conditionally treat certain text lines as comments
 - to define values that may be used in constant entries in the data
division
 - to allow for the reporting of leap seconds
 - for specifying options that are defined by the implementor
 - to request the flagging of syntax that might require native arithmetic
when standard arithmetic is in effect.

31) Concatenation expression. A concatenation expression operates on two
literals of the same class to concatenate their values.

32) Conditional compilation. Directives are provided to enable specific
lines of source to be included or omitted depending on the value of literals
referenced in those directives.

33) Conditional phrases. The conditional phrases in the ACCEPT, ADD, CALL,
COMPUTE, DELETE, DISPLAY, DIVIDE, MULTIPLY, READ, RECEIVE, REWRITE, START,
STRING, SUBTRACT, UNSTRING, and WRITE
statements may be specified in any order.

34) Constants. The user may define constants in the data division with
constant entries.

41) COPY and REPLACE statement partial word replacement. LEADING and
TRAILING phrases of the REPLACE statement and the REPLACING phrase of the
COPY statement allow replacement of partial words in source text and library
text. This is useful for prefixing and postfixing names.

42) Cultural adaptability and multilingual support. Support is provided for
using local conventions that depend on language and culture (cultural
conventions). A "locale" contains the specification of cultural conventions.
Features supported are collating sequences, date and time formats, monetary
and number formats, and character case mappings.

43) Cultural convention switching. The capability of switching the set of
cultural conventions, known as locales, in effect at runtime is provided by
the SET statement.

44) Currency sign extensions. The CURRENCY SIGN clause has been extended to
allow for national characters and for multiple distinct currency signs,
which may have any length.

47) Dynamic storage allocation. ALLOCATE and FREE statements are provided
for obtaining storage
dynamically. This storage is addressed by data-pointers.

48) EVALUATE statement, partial expressions. A partial expression may now be
used as a selection object in an EVALUATE statement. This partial
expression, when combined with its corresponding selection subject, forms a
complete conditional expression.

49) EXIT statement. The ability to immediately exit an inline PERFORM
statement, a paragraph, or a section has been added.

51) EXTERNAL AS literal. The option to specify a literal in the EXTERNAL
clause was added for external names that are not valid COBOL words or need
to be case-sensitive.

52) File sharing. File sharing provides a cooperative environment that
allows the user to permit or deny access by concurrent file connectors to a
physical file.

54) Fixed-form reference format area A and B removed. Areas A and B of the
previous COBOL standard reference format have been combined into one area
called the program-text area. Restrictions of area A and area B have been
removed. The previous COBOL standard reference format is fully upward
compatible.

55) Fixed-point numeric items. The maximum number of digits that may be
specified in a numeric literal or in a picture character-string that
describes a numeric or numeric-edited data item is increased from 18 to 31.

56) FLAG-85 directive. A directive, FLAG-85, has been added that causes
compiler flagging of language elements that may be incompatible between the
previous COBOL standard and this International Standard.

57) FORMAT clause. For sequentially-organized files, the FORMAT clause
provides the capability to present data so that people may read it. This is
called external media format; it includes any special encoding needed by the
operating environment to successfully print or display data, as might be
needed for multiple-octet data, for example.

58) Free-form reference format. Free-form reference format provides
varying-length source lines and permits source text and library text to be
written with a minimum of restrictions on the placement of source code on a
line. A compiler directive permits selection and intermixing of free-form or
fixed-form reference format, with fixed-form as the default.

59) FUNCTION keyword. The keyword FUNCTION may be omitted from a function
reference when the
referenced function-name or the ALL phrase is specified in the REPOSITORY
paragraph.

60) Function use expanded. The restrictions that numeric and integer
functions could be used only in arithmetic expressions was removed as well
as the restriction that no integer function could be used where an unsigned
integer was required. Numeric and integer functions may now be used anywhere
a numeric sender is allowed and an integer form of the ABS function may now
be used where an unsigned integer is required.

64) HIGHEST-ALGEBRAIC and LOWEST-ALGEBRAIC functions. The HIGHEST-ALGEBRAIC
and
LOWEST-ALGEBRAIC functions provide the ability to manipulate numeric data
items in a manner similar to the means that HIGH-VALUES and LOW-VALUES
permit with alphanumeric data items, but without the risks of the data
incompatibilities associated with those figurative constants.

65) Identification division header. The identification division header is
now optional.

66) Implementor-defined successful I-O status codes. A range of I-O status
codes is allocated for use by the implementor to indicate conditions
associated with successful completion of an input-output statement.

68) Inline comment. A comment may be written on a line following any
character-strings of the source text or library text that are written on
that line. An inline comment is introduced by the two contiguous characters
'*>'.

71) Initialization of tables. The VALUE clause may be used to initialize
selected elements of a table to specific values.

72) INITIALIZE statement, FILLER phrase. FILLER data items may be
initialized with the INITIALIZE statement.

73) INITIALIZE statement, VALUE phrase. A VALUE phrase may be specified in
the INITIALIZE statement to cause initialization of elementary data items to
the literal specified in the VALUE clause of the associated data description
entry or to NULL for items of category data-pointer, object-reference, or
program-pointer.

74) INITIALIZE statement, variable-length tables. A variable-length table
may be initialized with the INITIALIZE statement.

78) Intrinsic functions. New intrinsic functions are:
 ABS
 BOOLEAN-OF-INTEGER
 BYTE-LENGTH
 CHAR-NATIONAL
 DATE-TO-YYYYMMDD
 DAY-TO-YYYYDDD
 DISPLAY-OF
 E
 EXCEPTION-FILE
 EXCEPTION-FILE-N
 EXCEPTION-LOCATION
 EXCEPTION-LOCATION-N
 EXCEPTION-STATEMENT
 EXCEPTION-STATUS
 EXP
 EXP10
 FRACTION-PART
 HIGHEST-ALGEBRAIC
 INTEGER-OF-BOOLEAN
 LOCALE-COMPARE
 LOCALE-DATE
 LOCALE-TIME
 LOWEST-ALGEBRAIC
 NATIONAL-OF
 NUMVAL-F
 PI
 SIGN
 STANDARD-COMPARE
 TEST-DATE-YYYYMMDD
 TEST-DAY-YYYYDDD
 TEST-NUMVAL
 TEST-NUMVAL-C
 TEST-NUMVAL-F
 YEAR-TO-YYYY

83) OCCURS clause. Repetition vertically or horizontally and a STEP phrase
are added for report writer.

91) PERFORM statement. The AFTER phrase is allowed in an inline PERFORM.

94) PICTURE clause. The maximum number of characters that may be specified
in a picture character-string is increased from 30 to 50.

95) PICTURE clause. The content of a character position described with the
picture symbol 'A' is not required to be a letter except in the format stage
of a VALIDATE statement.

96) PICTURE clause. The PICTURE clause may be omitted when the VALUE clause
in a data description or report group description entry is specified with an
alphanumeric, boolean or national literal.

97) PICTURE clause. The currency symbol may be specified at the end of a
PICTURE character-string, optionally followed by one of the symbols '+',
'-', 'CR', or 'DB'.

106) Program prototypes. The interface and characteristics of a program are
defined in a program-prototype. This permits passing parameters by value,
omission of parameters, type checking and coercion of arguments, and the use
of calling conventions other than those used by default for COBOL.

109) READ PREVIOUS. The READ statement has been enhanced to allow reading
the record prior to that pointed to by the file position indicator.

110) RECORD KEY and ALTERNATE RECORD KEY. Keys for indexed files may be made
up from more than one
component.

111) Record locking. Record locking allows control of record access for
shared files. A record lock is used to prevent access to a record from other
file connectors.

114) REPLACE statement nesting. A REPLACE statement may be specified and
terminated without canceling the effect of a previous REPLACE statement.

115) Report writer. Previously, the report writer was a separate module and
its implementation was optional. The report writer facility is integrated
into the specification and it shall be implemented by a conforming
implementation. In addition, the following changes were made and are
documented elsewhere in the list of substantive changes not affecting
existing programs:
 - CODE IS identifier added
 - COLUMN clause has several additions
 - COLUMN, LINE, SOURCE, and VALUE clauses are allowed to have more than one
operand
 - Control data-name can be omitted
 - FILLER allowed in report section
 - National characters are allowed in reports
 - OCCURS allows repetition vertically or horizontally and a STEP phrase
 - Optional words OF, FOR, and ON in some clauses
 - OR PAGE phrase of CONTROL HEADING phrase
 - PAGE FOOTING allowed to have only relative LINE clauses
 - PAGE LIMIT includes COLUMN phrase
 - PLUS or MINUS can be the symbol '+' or '-', respectively
 - PRESENT WHEN clause added
 - SIGN clause does not require SEPARATE phrase in report section
 - SOURCE allows arithmetic expressions and functions and a ROUNDED phrase
 - SUM clause has many changes
 - USE BEFORE REPORTING changed
 - VARYING clause may be used with an OCCURS clause or a multiple LINE or
multiple COLUMN clause
 - WITH RESET phrase added to NEXT PAGE

117) SAME AS clause. The SAME AS clause indicates that the description of a
data item is identical to that of another item.

119) SELECT clause. A file may be dynamically assigned by specifying a
data-name in the SELECT clause.

120) SELECT WHEN clause. The SELECT WHEN clause of a record description
permits selection of one of several record description entries in the file
section during input-output operations. The selected record description
entry is used with a CODE-SET clause or a FORMAT clause to process
individual data items in a record.

125) SORT statement. A SORT statement may be used to sort a table. This sort
may be done using the fields specified in the KEY phrase defining the table,
by using the entire table element as the key, or by using specified key data
items.

129) START FIRST, LAST, and LESS THAN. The START statement has been enhanced
to allow starting to a position before the key and to point to the first or
last record in the file.

130) START and sequential files. The START statement has been enhanced to
allow a sequential file if the FIRST or LAST phrase is specified.

131) STOP WITH STATUS. The WITH STATUS phrase was added to the STOP
statement to allow a run unit to return a value to the operating system.

132) STRING statement. The DELIMITED phrase is optional in the STRING
statement. DELIMITED BY SIZE is assumed.

133) Subscripting with arithmetic expressions. An arithmetic expression may
now be used as a subscript, not just the forms data-name + integer and
data-name - integer.

137) TYPE, and TYPEDEF clauses. The TYPEDEF clause identifies a type
declaration which creates a user-defined type. The TYPE clause is used to
apply this user-defined type to the description of a data item. No storage
is allocated for the type declaration.

138) Underscore (_) character. The basic special characters of the COBOL
character repertoire have been expanded to include the underscore (_)
character, which can be used in the formation of COBOL words.

139) UNSTRING statement. The sending operand may be reference modified.

142) User-defined functions. The ability was added to write functions that
are activated in a manner similar to intrinsic functions. The word FUNCTION
is not specified as part of this invocation.

143) Validate facility. The new statement VALIDATE was added, giving the
ability to perform comprehensive data validation. The new data division
clauses CLASS, DEFAULT, DESTINATION, INVALID, PRESENT WHEN, VALIDATE-STATUS,
and VARYING were also added, with the PRESENT WHEN and VARYING clauses
having a similar function to those in the report section. These clauses,
together with the VALID, INVALID and WHEN phrases of the level-88 VALUE
clause, are ignored when they appear in a data description that is the
operand of any statement other than VALIDATE.

144) VALUE clause, WHEN SET TO FALSE phrase in data division. The WHEN SET
TO FALSE phrase allows
specification of a FALSE condition value. This value is moved to the
associated conditional variable when the SET TO FALSE statement is executed
for the associated condition-name.

148) Writing literals to a file. A record can be written from a literal
using the WRITE, RELEASE, or REWRITE statement. Using the FILE phrase of
WRITE, alphanumeric, national, and boolean literals and the figurative
constant SPACE can be written. When the FILE phrase is not used, any literal
can be written.
```

#### ↳ Re: What's new *and* interesting for IBM mainframe COBOL programmers - in the next COBOL Standard

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-02T14:14:26-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CFA7CA2.ABE29ECA@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<adb3qg$kah$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Given last week's note and the replies that I received, I thought that I
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> all "interesting".

I find many of these enhancements to be very nice.  But using the IBM
COBOL for VSE/ESA compiler I worry that I won't see any of them
implemented within the next ten years.  I hope I am wrong.

Just want to mention a few I am especially fond of...

 
> ***
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> expressions.

Like this a lot.  Especially useful for the TCP/IP SELECT statement.
 

> 22) CODE-SET clause. A code set defined with literals in the SPECIAL-NAMES
> paragraph can be specified in the CODE-SET clause for use in conversion on
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> multiple-octet (national) coded character set, such as ISO/IEC 10646-1.

Not sure I understand this exactly, but if it can be used for
ASCII/EBCDIC conversions I'm all for it.
 
> 29) Compiler directives. Compiler directives instruct the compiler to take
> specific actions during compilation. Compiler directives are provided:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> when standard arithmetic is in effect.

Like this one a lot.  Looks to me like the C PRAGMA statement.  I would
like being able to 'comment' out certain code during testing without
actually putting the '*' in column 7.  Of course having this in
production code might lead to a bit of confusion, but...  Would also be
nice to be able to turn off certain warning statements.
 
> 31) Concatenation expression. A concatenation expression operates on two
> literals of the same class to concatenate their values.

Yes!
 
> 32) Conditional compilation. Directives are provided to enable specific
> lines of source to be included or omitted depending on the value of literals
> referenced in those directives.

Again nice, though perhaps not so good in production code.
 
> 34) Constants. The user may define constants in the data division with
> constant entries.

Finally!
 

> 47) Dynamic storage allocation. ALLOCATE and FREE statements are provided
> for obtaining storage
> dynamically. This storage is addressed by data-pointers.

*Really* looking forward to this.  I know many will say it's dangerous,
but...
 
> 49) EXIT statement. The ability to immediately exit an inline PERFORM
> statement, a paragraph, or a section has been added.

My number 1 COBOL desire!!!!!  Should eliminate any need for SECTIONs,
PERFORM...THRU's and GO TO's.

> 71) Initialization of tables. The VALUE clause may be used to initialize
> selected elements of a table to specific values.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> may be initialized with the INITIALIZE statement.

All four of these would have been useful to me at times within the last
two years.

> 106) Program prototypes. The interface and characteristics of a program are
> defined in a program-prototype. This permits passing parameters by value,
> omission of parameters, type checking and coercion of arguments, and the use
> of calling conventions other than those used by default for COBOL.

Sounds good, especially for inter-language support (C et al).
 
> 117) SAME AS clause. The SAME AS clause indicates that the description of a
> data item is identical to that of another item.

Sounds nice.
 
> 132) STRING statement. The DELIMITED phrase is optional in the STRING
> statement. DELIMITED BY SIZE is assumed.

Sounds good.

One thing I think would be terrible useful in the STRING statement is a
TRAILING keyword.  In other words:

STRING FIRST-NAME MIDDLE-NAME LAST-NAME DELIMITED BY TRAILING SPACES
INTO FML-NAME.

I know it's too late now, but it seems to me a simply and obvious
improvement and one that would be useful to just about everyone.
 
> 137) TYPE, and TYPEDEF clauses. The TYPEDEF clause identifies a type
> declaration which creates a user-defined type. The TYPE clause is used to
> apply this user-defined type to the description of a data item. No storage
> is allocated for the type declaration.

Sounds nice, though it seems somewhat similar to the SAME AS clause. 
Not sure if having both would be confusing, but...
 
 
> 142) User-defined functions. The ability was added to write functions that
> are activated in a manner similar to intrinsic functions. The word FUNCTION
> is not specified as part of this invocation.

Sounds nice.  I still wish perfomed paragraphs could receive parameters
and return values (instead having to use 'sub-programs'), but I guess
that's too much to hope for.
 
> 143) Validate facility. The new statement VALIDATE was added, giving the
> ability to perform comprehensive data validation. The new data division
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> operand of any statement other than VALIDATE.

Looks to be incredibly useful.
 
> 144) VALUE clause, WHEN SET TO FALSE phrase in data division. The WHEN SET
> TO FALSE phrase allows
> specification of a FALSE condition value. This value is moved to the
> associated conditional variable when the SET TO FALSE statement is executed
> for the associated condition-name.

Have wanted this for a long time.  Should eliminate still statements
such as "SET NOT-DONE TO TRUE".  Yikes!
 

Thanks for posting all of this.  Now if only they would be implemented
in to the compiler I use...
```

```
You can unstring directly into the numeric field (discussing elsewhere) or
use function numval:

COMPUTE NUM-FIELD = FUNCTION NUMVAL(ALPHA-FIELD).
```

<a id="t-86417c7cc2"></a>
## Thread: Announce: Ada Distilled - Update — 1 msgs

### Announce: Ada Distilled - Update

- **From:** Richard Riehle <richard@adaworks.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-01T17:25:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CF965F1.156C0E3F@adaworks.com>`

```
I realize this is an COBOL forum and apologize in
advance to anyone who is offended by reference to
any other language.   For those who might be interested
I am offering the following information.

There is an updated version of the little booklet, Ada Distilled in
PDF format available for free download at:

            http://www.adaic.org
            http://www.adapower.com

It is my intention to continue to refine and update this
little booklet on Ada for as long as there is continued
interest.   My thanks to the many users who have sent
me suggestions and appreciation over the past year.  I am
trying to keep Ada Distilled short and simple so it can be used by
any programmer experienced in some other language
as a quick start into the Ada language.

Richard Riehle
richard@adaworks.com
```

---

<a id="t-e4fd01dd0e"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu 6.0; Using german special-chars in alphabetic-test — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu 6.0; Using german special-chars in alphabetic-test

- **From:** "Spectra" <Spectra.Soft@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 2002-06-02T12:18:05+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<adcrd1$30i$01$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Hi!

Is it possible do define an alphabet which includes german special
characters like "ï¿½" "ï¿½" and others so that the condition "IF .... IS
ALPHABETIC " becomes true even if these characters are included in the
string being examined?

Are special compiler-options required?

Do You have an example?

Thanks a lot in advance!
Hans Jakschik
```

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>

```
The table may be all numeric display today, but what if in future
changes/enhancements, it becomes a combination of packed decimal, numeric
display, and alphanumeric?  The advantage of using INITIALIZE now will cause
NO PROCEDURE DIVISION statement to change then.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-06-13T01:04:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020612210449.20182.00000446@mb-ma.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<dkrK8.947$yB3.37026045@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```
>From: "Terry Heinze" terryheinze@prodigy.net 
>Date: 2002-06-02 12:02 Eastern Daylight Time

I cautiously broached the idea of using WITH TEST AFTER to
solve the problem created by PERFORM UNTIL loop-counter > nn,
at the point where the loop-counter reaches but does not surpass
its physical limits. 
The vehement abuse and scorn heaped upon the idea made me
hasten to insist that the idea was all Stringer's and none of my
own.  
If there are any present who happen to hate WITH TEST AFTER, 
why do you hate it?  It looks pretty cool to me. 




Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-13T06:20:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ae9v68$5ut$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<dkrK8.947$yB3.37026045@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <20020612210449.20182.00000446@mb-ma.aol.com>`

```
My view of  "With Test After" (commonly referred to as "DO Until" in
non-COBOL environments) is:

A) It is a useful tool - but one that isn't needed particularly often

B) It should be used "knowledgeably" i.e. ONLY when you require that you
"perform" your loop at least once - whether OR NOT the "control" condition
is true before the loop

C) (follow-on to B) if you end up "required" to put the same test inside
your loop and on your PERFORM statement when using WITH TEST AFTER, the
chances are that you have used it when you shouldn't have.

 ***

As far (as I recall) the original "question" - it will NOT solve problems of
trying to compare a 2 digit variable with a 3 digit literal.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** stringer@es.co.nz (stringer)
- **Date:** 2002-06-16T22:01:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<dkrK8.947$yB3.37026045@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <20020612210449.20182.00000446@mb-ma.aol.com> <ae9v68$5ut$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As the poster of the original solution, perhaps i should comment that i agree 
with Mr Klein (dare I call him Bill?)

But (Please excuse the English!) it did seem an elegant solution to a problem 
faced by Mr Klatte who seems hamstrung by an IT boss who would rather save 
$0.02 worth of memory than have logical bullet-proof code.

Correct me if I'm wrong, but "with test after" was added to the language at 
some time. Perhaps this explains the reluctance of some to take up its use. I 
have noticed in this newsgroup that htere are some who still use CoBOL 74, and 
some who simply prefer to use its constructs despite more modern ones being 
available.

I won't get into the argument about spaghetti-code vs procedural vs object 
oriented, save to naote that a) almost everyone agrees that first is plain bad 
and to be avoided and b) that htere are horses for courses. While OO may be 
best for many things, such a database backed programmes, there are still some 
things better coded in procedural. And no, I won't respond to any replies about 
this, as not being a full time paid programer I haven't the time or experience 
to get into such a debate in detail!

To Bill Jimmy, Thane and others too numerous to mention, I enjoy your posts. 
Keep up the good work. I have learnt much just from reading this newsgroup, and 
now feel confident enough to occasionally contribute. I now have thousands of
lines of code behind me in an ever expanding suite of programmes. Perhaps some 
day I will be in a position to make some money out of it!!

D J H Stringer
In article <ae9v68$5ut$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>, wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com 
says...
>
>My view of  "With Test After" (commonly referred to as "DO Until" in
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-06-17T03:59:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D0D5EFD.F3D9EAF5@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<dkrK8.947$yB3.37026045@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <20020612210449.20182.00000446@mb-ma.aol.com> <ae9v68$5ut$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <aej1rt$jn2$1@lust.ihug.co.nz>`

```


stringer wrote:

> As the poster of the original solution, perhaps i should comment that i agree
> with Mr Klein (dare I call him Bill?)
>

Why not ? Having met the man he is a very nice person with no pomp or circumstance
<G>. "My boy Bill........",  from Carousel .

> But (Please excuse the English!) it did seem an elegant solution to a problem
> faced by Mr Klatte who seems hamstrung by an IT boss who would rather save
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> day I will be in a position to make some money out of it!!

Not slavishly following Pete, I don't use the PERFORM TEST AFTER - and like him
haven't as yet found a need to. Given the right occasion, I would use it.

Perhaps COBOL is too flexible to a fault - it just provides so many options to do
the same thing, and each individual settles into a particular style. I'd be curious
to know how many people use DECLARATIVES. I find it just as easy to do the
following :-

    open input Data-file
    if file-status <> "00"
        set FileError to true
    end-if

where the last three lines would be covered automatically by DECLARATIVES.

The plethora of coding options gets worse when you look at Exception Handling in
the draft for COBOL 2002. Declaratives, EC (Exception Conditions), a list of 50
plus, and EXIT RAISING etc.. The mind boggles as to how much of this will be put to
good use, along with Report Writer and Screen Section which wont be supported by
IBM.

The COBOL Standard is a bit like a set of City bylaws; once introduced, you have
one hell of a job getting rid of superfluous syntax. Announce you are going to
remove feature X and a bunch of people will come on like screaming dervishes saying
"BUT I *always* use that....!".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** stringer@es.co.nz (stringer)
- **Date:** 2002-06-17T04:35:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aejou4$b7f$1@lust.ihug.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<dkrK8.947$yB3.37026045@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <20020612210449.20182.00000446@mb-ma.aol.com> <ae9v68$5ut$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <aej1rt$jn2$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <3D0D5EFD.F3D9EAF5@shaw.ca>`

```
Jimmy,

Like you, I code without declaratives.

I also tend to code without the 'test after' alternative, although there are 
several places where I could have used it, and may just rewrite my code that 
way where appropriate.

And finally, I thought dervishes whirled, not screamed? Perhaps a better 
reference might have been to Syrians coming down like the wolf from the 
fold..(I won't guarantee the accuracy of the quote).



David Stringer
```

```
> Jimmy,
>
```

```
Declaratives are extremely useful under some circumstances. Particularly in
mainframe environments where there may be quite complex cleanup requirements
if hardware fails.

> I also tend to code without the 'test after' alternative, although there
are
> several places where I could have used it, and may just rewrite my code
that
> way where appropriate.

Why would you rewrite working code, just so you can use a previously unused
construct?

> And finally, I thought dervishes whirled, not screamed? Perhaps a better
> reference might have been to Syrians coming down like the wolf from the
> fold..(I won't guarantee the accuracy of the quote).
>
Dervishes whirl. The Assyrian came down like a wolf ON the fold (the fold
being the place where sheep were kept). I believe the quote is from the
Bible, but I haven't checked for Chapter and Verse. Having said that, it has
a ring of Byron or Tennyson (might be Ozymandias) to it, so it might be that
Jimmy is right about his Victorian romantics.  The thing that screams
is an Irish lost spirit called a Banshee. Jimmy, having Irish blood, should
have known that; Stringer, being a Kiwi, should demonstrate a better
standard of education...<G>

Pete. (This NG always guarantees a smile...<G>)
```

```
stringer wrote:

> In article <3d0d9d8e_3@Usenet.com>, dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz says...
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I consider my hand smacked!!

Consider your head smacked ! Do they have a refresher for English 101 in NZ
<G>

Got it :-

    Destruction of SENNACHERIB  - Lord Byron (1788-1824)

1      The Assyrian came down like the wolf on the fold,
2     And his cohorts were gleaming in purple and gold;
3     And the sheen of their spears was like stars on the sea,
4     When the blue wave rolls nightly on deep Galilee.

5       Like the leaves of the forest when Summer is green,
6     That host with their banners at sunset were seen:
7     Like the leaves of the forest when Autumn hath blown,
8     That host on the morrow lay withered and strown.

9       For the Angel of Death spread his wings on the blast,
10   And breathed in the face of the foe as he passed;
11   And the eyes of the sleepers waxed deadly and chill,
12   And their hearts but once heaved, and for ever grew still!

13     And there lay the steed with his nostril all wide,
14   But through it there rolled not the breath of his pride;
15   And the foam of his gasping lay white on the turf,
16   And cold as the spray of the rock-beating surf.

17     And there lay the rider distorted and pale,
18   With the dew on his brow, and the rust on his mail:
19   And the tents were all silent, the banners alone,
20   The lances unlifted, the trumpet unblown.

21     And the widows of Ashur are loud in their wail,
22   And the idols are broke in the temple of Baal;
23   And the might of the Gentile, unsmote by the sword,
24   Hath melted like snow in the glance of the Lord!

Pete was right about it's Israelite connotation, but not the Bible. Don't ask
me why, but in the back of my head the word "SENNACHERIB" was ticking away,
with no meaning.

>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Into the valley of death rode the five hundred

Your math is wrong ! What sort of "funny" history book were you reading,
there's only some 3,000 years between the two events, the latter being the
Crimean War  :-

ALFRED LORD TENNYSON (1809-1883)

                               CHARGE OF THE LIGHT BRIGADE

I.

1     Half a league, half a league,
2         Half a league onward,
3     All in the valley of Death
4         Rode the six hundred.
5     `Forward, the Light Brigade!
6     Charge for the guns!' he said:
7     Into the valley of Death
8         Rode the six hundred.

II.

9     `Forward, the Light Brigade!'
10   Was there a man dismay'd?
11   Not tho' the soldier knew
12       Some one had blunder'd:
13   Their's not to make reply,
14   Their's not to reason why,
15   Their's but to do and die:
16   Into the valley of Death
17       Rode the six hundred.

III

18   Cannon to right of them,
19   Cannon to left of them,
20   Cannon in front of them
21       Volley'd and thunder'd;
22   Storm'd at with shot and shell,
23   Boldly they rode and well,
24   Into the jaws of Death,
25   Into the mouth of Hell
26       Rode the six hundred.

IV

27   Flash'd all their sabres bare,
28   Flash'd as they turn'd in air
29   Sabring the gunners there,
30   Charging an army, while
31       All the world wonder'd:
32   Plunged in the battery-smoke
33   Right thro' the line they broke;
34   Cossack and Russian
35   Reel'd from the sabre-stroke
36       Shatter'd and sunder'd.
37   Then they rode back, but not
38       Not the six hundred.

V

39   Cannon to right of them,
40   Cannon to left of them,
41   Cannon behind them
42       Volley'd and thunder'd;
43   Storm'd at with shot and shell,
44   While horse and hero fell,
45   They that had fought so well
46   Came thro' the jaws of Death,
47   Back from the mouth of Hell,
48   All that was left of them,
49       Left of six hundred.

VI

50   When can their glory fade?
51   O the wild charge they made!
52       All the world wonder'd.
53   Honour the charge they made!
54   Honour the Light Brigade,
55       Noble six hundred!

PS: There's a site where you can listen to Tennyson reciting the first lines
of the poem, recorded on one of those oldie cylinders.

Try this link, if you want to follow up - it's free and would save you paying
for English 101 <G>

http://www.library.utoronto.ca/utel/rp/index1stlines.html

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-17T00:28:34+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d0d1c8f_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<dkrK8.947$yB3.37026045@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <20020612210449.20182.00000446@mb-ma.aol.com>`

```
Although I don't "hate" WITH TEST AFTER (it is kind of silly to get
emotional about computer code...) I don't use it and probably never will.

Why not?

Because during more than three decades of writing COBOL, I have become used
to the fact that WITH TEST BEFORE is the way Perform works and I don't have
to think about it. (This does NOT mean I am stuck in COBOL 74 or COBOL 59 or
any other version. Quite the contrary. I have been coding Object Oriented
COBOL for over 3 years now and DO use additions to the language which add
value to it... This one simply doesn't.

I have never encountered a case where it would be necessary to use it. That
doesn't mean there aren't any, just that I personally never came across any.
I probably organise my code so as to obviate the need for it, without even
thinking about it.

I would have no problem maintaining code that DID use it, but my own view is
that it is inelegant and unnecessary. (Inelegant because it means writing
three more words; unnecessary because it is possible to organise things so
it isn't needed.)

It DOESN'T look "pretty cool" to me; it simply looks ugly...<G>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-17T20:41:06+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D0E3B52.3655444@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<dkrK8.947$yB3.37026045@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <20020612210449.20182.00000446@mb-ma.aol.com> <3d0d1c8f_7@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> 
> Although I don't "hate" WITH TEST AFTER 
> ...
> It DOESN'T look "pretty cool" to me; it simply looks ugly...<G>

In general what looks 'nice' is what one recognises and what looks
'ugly' is what one doesn't recognise.

If a particular feature hasn't been used then one's reaction to it is
'confusion and frustration' at having to work out what it is doing (even
if this is only momentary), whereas the intent of code in one's usual
style can be recognised at a glance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: until greater than

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** stringer@es.co.nz (stringer)
- **Date:** 2002-06-17T21:34:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aelkkl$15f$2@lust.ihug.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<dkrK8.947$yB3.37026045@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <20020612210449.20182.00000446@mb-ma.aol.com> <3d0d1c8f_7@Usenet.com> <3D0E3B52.3655444@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Quite right. Coding styles all differ.

Pete Dashwood wrote:Why would you rewrite working code, just so you can use a 
previously unused construct?

He too missed thep point. I wasn't wanting to use a (new?) construct just for 
the sake of it, but because it was easier to read. I have several places where 
a portion of code in a perform loop is repeated just before the perform loop. 

The reason is so that that code is run at least once before the test. This I 
consider inelegant compared to the extra three words contained in the test.

I suspect that the compiled machine code may well be more efficient too, though 
I'll not waste my time checking that out. Any students here with the time to 
waste on a test programme??



In article <3D0E3B52.3655444@Azonic.co.nz>, riplin@Azonic.co.nz says...
>
>Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>style can be recognised at a glance.
```

```
> Quite right. Coding styles all differ.
>

for

I

I suggest reviewing the structure. If you still want to use WITH TEST
BEFORE, fine. It's your code and I truly  believe people should use whatever
they are comfortable with.


> I suspect that the compiled machine code may well be more efficient too,
though
> I'll not waste my time checking that out. Any students here with the time
to
> waste on a test programme??
>
>
Would it matter to you if it was ?

Or is that simply another argument to justify doing it?

Whatever you do is OK. But it is important you should know the real reasons
you code things a certain way and not what you think are the reasons <G>.

Pete.
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-06-03T12:07:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1023106038@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: DOS utility that controls Caps Lock & Num Lock keys — 6 msgs

### DOS utility that controls Caps Lock & Num Lock keys

- **From:** "Bookie" <bobsmith@netnet.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-03T12:52:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cfbab4a$0$2961$39cecf19@nnrp1.twtelecom.net>`

```
I'm looking for a DOS utility that controls the status of the "Caps Lock"
and "Num Lock" keys.   Does anyone know of one??
```

#### ↳ Re: DOS utility that controls Caps Lock & Num Lock keys

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-03T11:14:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CFBB217.1690@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<3cfbab4a$0$2961$39cecf19@nnrp1.twtelecom.net>`

```
Bookie wrote:
> 
> I'm looking for a DOS utility that controls the status of the "Caps Lock"
> and "Num Lock" keys.   Does anyone know of one??

There's an old PC Magazine utility called "toggle.com" which does that.  You should be 
able to find it on their web site, even though its VERY old (about 1988).
```

#### ↳ Re: DOS utility that controls Caps Lock & Num Lock keys

- **From:** Albert Richheimer <richheimer@bluewin.ch>

```
----the above empty line is important!---------------------


Put all lines between the cut lines into a file, e.g. ww. Then enter:

debug<ww


For help simply type "numlock h" or "capslock h".
```

#### ↳ Re: DOS utility that controls Caps Lock & Num Lock keys

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-04T08:11:32+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CFC6824.D15E3CC5@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3cfbab4a$0$2961$39cecf19@nnrp1.twtelecom.net>`

```
Bookie wrote:
> 
> I'm looking for a DOS utility that controls the status of the "Caps Lock"
> and "Num Lock" keys.   Does anyone know of one??

It is very simple to change the key locks from DOS by peeking and poking
0x0000:0417 on an IBM-PC.  Do not do this on other types of machine.

If your _actual_ requirement is to have the fields keyed in upper case
then your compiler may have options on the accept mechanisms.  eg with
MF and MS Cobol use CALL x"AF" with 'bit-pair' function 85 to have all
input set to upper case or not.
```

<a id="t-0df3c1039b"></a>
## Thread: Fuji-Cobol - Table and List -Controls — 7 msgs

### Fuji-Cobol - Table and List -Controls

- **From:** Hajo Schepker <schepker@web.de>
- **Date:** 2002-06-03T23:18:36+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<adgtgc.go.1@ID-20196.news.dfncis.de>`

```
Hi,
does anybody know, why Fuji limits the items in the table-control to
2000 rows?

Can a list-control display more than 2000 rows?
If not, what else can I take to display a database with more than 2000
entries?
-thanks-
```

#### ↳ Re: Fuji-Cobol - Table and List -Controls

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-04T21:46:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kzaL8.55738$4i.5995186@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<adgtgc.go.1@ID-20196.news.dfncis.de>`

```

"Hajo Schepker" <schepker@web.de> wrote in message
news:adgtgc.go.1@ID-20196.news.dfncis.de...
> Hi,
> does anybody know, why Fuji limits the items in the table-control to
> 2000 rows?

Because of Y2K, tables with rows greater than 2000 wrapped and overwrote
earlier entries in the table. (It's a joke!)

>
> Can a list-control display more than 2000 rows?
> If not, what else can I take to display a database with more than 2000
> entries?
> -thanks-

Yes.

Test it yourself:

PERFORM VARYING MYNUM FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL MYNUM > 5000
       INVOKE CmList1 "AddString" USING MYNUM
     END-PERFORM
```

#### ↳ Re: Fuji-Cobol - Table and List -Controls

- **References:** `<adgtgc.go.1@ID-20196.news.dfncis.de>`

## Thread: Page printer adjustments — 2 this month / 4 total

_Thread started [2002-05](2002-05.md#t-fa9f209222)._

#### ↳ Re: Page printer adjustments

- **From:** Jason Adams <jsa@kona.homeip.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-04T00:21:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ufoqkc5334n122@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<8a02b0a1.0205290516.684fa0c1@posting.google.com>`

```
graham wrote:

> 
> I've looked through some Epson ESC P and HP PCL specifications but
> without success.

Keep looking in that hpcl manual.
You can position things easliy to withn 1/144th of an inch with hpcl.
Its a pain, but doable with excape sequences. You can even set up
your own forms flash with hpcl.  I've had to do that a time or two.

Side note:  (Flame bait)  ;-)
Ever notice how people thing your nutz to resort to micromanaging
your print by generating hpcl sequences but thing nothing of writing
html by hand?

Whats up with that?

Jason
```

#### ↳ Re: Page printer adjustments

- **From:** philhickling@compuserve.com (Phil Hickling)
- **Date:** 2002-06-06T01:57:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6a371be2.0206060057.3c8e7e0a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8a02b0a1.0205290516.684fa0c1@posting.google.com>`

```
Yes, one way would be to use escape, or control sequences.  For
example:
*p520x690Y @ADDR1---------------------------------@
*p520x740Y @ADDR2---------------------------------@
will allow you to print 2 lines of an address at an exact position.

I use this technique for printing giro payment slips, with different
fonts, boxes, circles, optical character recognition fonts, etc.  It
is fairly simple once you have learnt the techniques.  I will post on
my website a more detailed example - within the next day or so:  see
www.willowtreecomputing.com

Another method might be to do the printing via Microsoft Word, giving
you very good control where everything goes.
```

<a id="t-7d52e86785"></a>
## Thread: TinyCOBOL release 0.58 - Newsgroups — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Open-source COBOL (GnuCOBOL, OpenCOBOL, TinyCOBOL)`](topics.md#open-source) · [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### TinyCOBOL release 0.58 - Newsgroups

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-04T06:46:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CFC9AA0.E9E375BA@arvotek.net>`

```
Announce: TinyCOBOL release 0.58

The TinyCOBOL compiler version 0.58 is available at the TinyCOBOL home
page (1) and iBiblio (2).

This release contains mainly bugs fixes, and some enhancements.  
It includes updates to the main compiler, run-time, the regression and
NIST test suites.

Support for inline comments, DEBUGGING lines, declaratives
(preliminary), intrinsic functions (preliminary), INSPECT CONVERTING,
concatenation (preliminary), have been added to the compiler.

The compiler front end and resource options have been enhanced to to
define the default compiler action (create executable or library). The
compiler can also create an static/shared libraries, from COBOL sources,
directly from the command line.

And finally, embedded SQL can be used with TC, by using a vendor
supplied SQL pre-processor and libraries.


Please report any bugs and/or problems to the TinyCOBOL mailing list. 


1) TinyCOBOL Home Page
   http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/
   http://www.tinycobol.org/

2) iBiblio (formerly Metalab)
   http://www.ibiblio.org/pub/Linux/devel/lang/cobol/
```

```
string used @37@ to terminate the string.

news:abbdop$iq4$1@newsfeed.norlight.net...

> ?N?
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: AS400/ CONTRACT/ HAWAII — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`AS/400, iSeries, RPG`](topics.md#as400)_

### Re: AS400/ CONTRACT/ HAWAII

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-04T11:53:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<60E685B9E8D96909.0D3AF6E7BC44E0F4.15FBEDDD30437BA6@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<adgup9$b24$0@192.153.35.30>`

```
On 3 Jun 2002 23:40:25 GMT, "Tom Gugger" <omnimed@norden1.com>
enlightened us:

>From: "Tom Gugger" tgugger@msn.com
>Subject: AS400/ COBOL/ CONTRACT/ HAWAII
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Everyone has different needs and such and can live on different
amounts of salary but to try and live something other than a really
frugal life style in Hawaii on 55K for a year is very hard.  Consider:
If you live in Ohio, the cost of living in Hawaii is 44.5% higher --
thus if you made 55K in Ohio, you would have to make almost 80K to
maintain your life style.

However, if you live in New York city, the cost of living in Honolulu
is 26.2 % lower.  And if you're only making 55K and living and working
in NYC, you're probably not doing so well so you should jump on this.

And as a final comparison, if you lived and worked in San Francisco
(and again on 55K you're probably not doing so well), the cost of
living in Honolulu is 21.8% lower, so go for it.

What I'm trying to point out to all of you potential applicants, is
don't be swayed by the thought of sunny beaches, warm days and bikini
clad tourists.  You still got to eat and drink and pay rent etc. etc.


>                                                       Qualifications
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

No one ever says "It's only a game," when their team is winning.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

```
Tom Gugger <omnimed@norden1.com> wrote:
>
>Subject: ENDEVOR CONSULTANT/ IBM MAINFRAME/ MA

When posting to comp.lang.cobol please include a rate, or range of rates, 
associated with the position(s) offered; to do otherwise leads many to 
conclude that you are either trolling for resumes or running a blind ad to 
determine rates.

DD
```

```
METHOD CODE in COBOL DLL method-id. "meth3". local-storage Section.

PI-INC-DATE PIC X(8). 05 PI-INC-DATE-R REDEFINES PI-INC-DATE. 10
SERV-DATE-CY PIC X(4). 10 SERV-DATE-MM PIC XX. 10 SERV-DATE-DD PIC XX.
01 WS-PI-PROT-INC PIC 9(05). 01 WS-PI-FAMILY-SIZE PIC X(02).

       01 WS-FEE-DIVISR-REC. 05 WS-FD-FEE-SCH PIC X(01). 05
       WS-FD-DIVISOR PIC 9(05). 05 WS-FD-SERV-DATE PIC X(08). 05
       WS-FD-ERR-IND PIC X. 05 WS-SERV-CODE PIC X(03).

       '/'. 05 SQL-DATE-DD PIC XX. 05 FILLER PIC X VALUE '/'. 05
       SQL-DATE-CY PIC X(4). 01 SQL-REQSERV-DATE PIC X(10). 01
```
