       01 WS-OUT-REC pic x(28). EXEC SQL INCLUDE SQLCA END-EXEC.

       ++INCLUDE WSSQLCA 01 WS-PARA-NUM pic x(4).

       linkage section. 01 PI-FD-REC. 05 PI-FD-FAMILY-SIZE PIC 9(02).
       05 PI-FD-FEE-SCH PIC X(01). 05 PI-FD-SERV-CODE PIC 9(03). 05
       PI-FD-SERV-DATE PIC 9(08). 05 PI-FD-PROT-INC PIC 9(05). 05
       PI-FD-DIVISOR PIC 9(05). 05 PI-FD-FEE-IND PIC X(01). 05
       PI-ERR-IND PIC X(01). 05 FD-ERR-IND PIC X(01). 05 SV-ERR-IND
       PIC X(01).

       01 PI-FD-REC-OUT. 05 passed-value pic x(28).

       procedure division using by reference PI-FD-REC returning
       PI-FD-REC-OUT.

           EXEC SQL CONNECT TO "CCPEDITS" AS CONN1 USER "LARRY.AAAAA"
           END-EXEC PERFORM 1000-SERV-IND-PARA THRU 1000-EXIT. MOVE
           PI-FD-REC TO PASSED-VALUE.

       1000-SERV-IND-PARA. MOVE '1000' TO WS-PARAGRAPH-NBR WS-PARA-NUM.
       MOVE PI-FD-SERV-CODE TO WS-SERV-CODE. EXEC SQL SELECT
       SERV_FEE_IND INTO :WS-PI-FD-FEE-IND

           FROM SERV_TBL_5 WHERE SERV_SERV_CD = :WS-SERV-CODE

           END-EXEC IF SQLCODE > -1 AND < 2 MOVE WS-PI-FD-FEE-IND TO
           PI-FD-FEE-IND IF PI-FD-FEE-IND = 'Y' PERFORM
           2000-PROTECT-INCOME-PARA THRU 2000-EXIT PERFORM
           3000-FEE-DIVSR-PARA THRU 3000-EXIT ELSE GO TO 1000-EXIT ELSE
           IF SQLCODE = 100 MOVE 'N' TO SV-ERR-IND.

                               1000-EXIT.
                                 EXIT.

       2000-PROTECT-INCOME-PARA. MOVE '2000' TO WS-PARAGRAPH-NBR
       WS-PARA-NUM. MOVE PI-FD-SERV-DATE TO PI-DATE. MOVE SERV-DATE-CY
       TO SQL-DATE-CY. MOVE SERV-DATE-MM TO SQL-DATE-MM. MOVE
       SERV-DATE-DD TO SQL-DATE-DD. MOVE SQL-SERV-DATE TO
       SQL-REQSERV-DATE. MOVE PI-FD-FAMILY-SIZE TO WS-PI-FAMILY-SIZE.
       EXEC SQL SELECT PROT_INC_LEVEL INTO :WS-PI-PROT-INC

               FROM PROT_INC_LEVELS_TBL_36

                 WHERE FAMILY_SIZE = :WS-PI-FAMILY-SIZE
               AND PI_LEVEL_START_DT <= :SQL-REQSERV-DATE
                AND PI_LEVEL_END_DT >= :SQL-REQSERV-DATE

            END-EXEC

            IF SQLCODE > -1 AND < 2 MOVE WS-PI-PROT-INC TO
            PI-FD-PROT-INC ELSE IF SQLCODE = 100 MOVE 'N' TO PI-ERR-IND.

       2000-EXIT. EXIT. 3000-FEE-DIVSR-PARA. MOVE '3000' TO
       WS-PARAGRAPH-NBR WS-PARA-NUM. MOVE PI-FD-SERV-DATE TO PI-DATE.
       MOVE SERV-DATE-CY TO SQL-DATE-CY. MOVE SERV-DATE-MM TO
       SQL-DATE-MM. MOVE SERV-DATE-DD TO SQL-DATE-DD. MOVE SQL-SERV-DATE
       TO SQL-REQSERV-DATE. MOVE PI-FD-FEE-SCH TO WS-FD-FEE-SCH. EXEC
       SQL SELECT DIVISOR

              INTO :WS-FD-DIVISOR

           FROM FEE_DIVISOR_TBL_38

            WHERE FEE_SCHEDULE = :WS-FD-FEE-SCH AND FD_LEVEL_START_DT <=
            :SQL-REQSERV-DATE AND FD_LEVEL_END_DT >= :SQL-REQSERV-DATE

            END-EXEC

            IF SQLCODE > -1 AND < 2 MOVE WS-FD-DIVISOR TO
            PI-FD-DIVISOR ELSE IF SQLCODE = 100 MOVE 'N' TO

```
Hi Grinder, I have a another example which is shown below. it works fine
here. But I have created similiar classes , but I get the same error.
438 Object does not support this method or property. Any suggestions

VB code Private Sub Command1_Click() Dim g As Double Dim p As Single Dim
n As Double Dim o As Object Set o = CreateObject("idoa7") g = Text1.Text
p = Text2.Text n = o.calnet7(g, p) Text3.Text = n End Sub


Net Express Code cobol dll

      $set ooctrl(+p)

      *>----------------------------------------------------------- *>
      Class description
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
      class-id. idoa7 inherits from olebase.

       object section. class-control. idoa7 is class "idoa7" *> OCWIZARD
       - start list of classes olebase is class "olebase" oleSafeArray
       is class "olesafea" oleVariant is class "olevar" *> OCWIZARD -
       end list of classes *>---USER-CODE. Add any additional class
       names below.

           .

      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
      working-storage section. *> Definition of global data
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------

      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
      class-object. *> Definition of class data and methods
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
      object-storage section.

      *> OCWIZARD - start standard class methods
      *>----------------------------------------------------------- *>
      Return details about the class. *> If you have a type library,
      theClassId and theInterfaceId *> here MUST match. *> theProgId
      must match the registry entry for this class *> (a zero length
      string implies using the class file name) *> theClassId must match
      the CLSID stored in the registry. *> theVersion is currently
      ignored (default 1 used).
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
      method-id. queryClassInfo. linkage section. 01 theProgId pic
      x(256). 01 theClassId pic x(39). 01 theInterfceId pic x(39). 01
      theVersion pic x(4) comp-5. 01 theDescription pic x(256). 01
      theThreadModel pic x(20). procedure division using by reference
      theProgId by reference theClassId by reference theInterfceId by
      reference theVersion by reference theDescription by reference
      theThreadModel. move z"{FC65FB59-68CB-11D6-B317-9B901B06D928}" to
      theClassId move z"{FC65FB5A-68CB-11D6-B317-9B901B06D928}" to
      theInterfceId move z"Description for class idoa7" to
      theDescription move z"Apartment" to theThreadModel exit method.
      end method queryClassInfo.

      *> OCWIZARD - end standard class methods

       end class-object.

      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
      object. *> Definition of instance data and methods
      *>-----------------------------------------------------------
      object-storage section.

      *> OCWIZARD - start standard instance methods *> OCWIZARD - end
      standard instance methods

      *>---------------------------------------------------------------
      method-id. "calnet7". local-storage Section. 01 d11 comp-2.
      *>---USER-CODE. Add any local storage items needed below. linkage
      Section. 01 gp1 comp-2. 01 tx1 pic s9(4) comp-5. 01 np1 comp-2.

       procedure division using by value gp1 by value tx1 returning np1.
       compute d11 = (gp1 * tx1) /100 compute np1 = gp1 - d11


      *>---USER-CODE. Add method implementation below.

       exit method. end method "calnet7". *>----------------------------
       ------------------------------------


       end object.

       end class idoa7.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help on Net Express COBOL dll and calling the DLL from VB program

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-07T21:03:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<adron402onr@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<be69045e.0205280944.76acdd8e@posting.google.com> <3cff001c$3@usenetgateway.com>`

```

"aravind741" <member@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:3cff001c$3@usenetgateway.com...
> Hi Grinder, I have a another example which is shown below. it works fine
> here. But I have created similiar classes , but I get the same error.
> 438 Object does not support this method or property. Any suggestions

Look at your ActiveX library with a COM object browser -- either the one in
VB or another tool of your choice.  This will show you if the interface is
being properly exposed.

You mentioned that the library is being early bound into a VB app.  Is the
method you are trying to use, found by the VB interface as you're typing it
in?  Does the syntax you're using match that offered by VB?
```

<a id="t-06240c04b0"></a>
## Thread: Cobol 390 2.2 and Cobol Z/Os 3.1 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Cobol 390 2.2 and Cobol Z/Os 3.1

- **From:** eric.hamtiaux@fortisag.be (Eric hamtiaux)
- **Date:** 2002-06-06T00:50:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6830b58.0206052350.4a27bd8e@posting.google.com>`

```
For your information an IBM Apar is opened , by me ,  for a Cobol problem.
This apar is not accessible at this moment but it contains :

APAR= PQ61957  SER=                            IN INCORROUT
EXEC INCORRECT RESULT CMPL ARITH(EXTEND),OPT AND COMPLEX COMPUTE
WITH EXPONENTIATION EXECUTED BY PERFORM...THRU...UNTIL STATEMENT

ERROR DESCRIPTION:
A COBOL program gets incorrect results using the compiler
options ARITH(EXTEND),OPT(STD!FULL) in that a complex COMPUTE
involving exponentiation gives the answer of 000000000.0000
when the paragraph containing the COMPUTE is executed
with a PERFORM...THRU...UNTIL statement.
*
 The code involved is the following:
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01 RESULTC         PIC 9(9)V9(4).
       01  FIELD48040-0500      PIC 9(9)V9(4) COMP-3.
      *---   SETTING TO = 48040.0500
       01  FIELD1            PIC 99V9(5) COMP-3.
      *---   SETTING TO = 01.00000
       01  DATA0                    PIC 99V9(5) COMP-3.
      *---   SETTING TO = 00.00000
       01  EXPON0                   PIC 9(2)V9(5) COMP-3.
*---   SETTING TO = 00.00000
 01 COUNT-IT                     PIC 9(3) COMP.
*---  SETTING TO = 000
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
     MOVE 0 TO  DATA0.
     MOVE 0 TO EXPON0.
     MOVE 0 TO COUNT-IT.
     MOVE 1 TO  FIELD1.
     MOVE 48040,0500 TO  FIELD48040-0500.
     PERFORM CALCR THRU CALCR-EXIT UNTIL COUNT-IT = 3.
       GOBACK.
 CALCR.
     IF COUNT-IT = 3
            GO TO CALCR-EXIT.
     ADD 1 TO COUNT-IT.
     COMPUTE RESULTC =
                  ( FIELD48040-0500 *  FIELD1
               * ((1 +  DATA0) ** EXPON0)
               * ((1 +  DATA0) **  (1 / 2))
               * ((1 +  DATA0) ** (COUNT-IT / 360))).
            DISPLAY RESULTC.
       CALCR-EXIT.   EXIT.
****
NOTE:
The same results occur with the COBOL/OS390 2.2 compiler
and the Enterprise COBOL/zOS 3.1 compiler.
LOCAL FIX:
If any one of these changes is made the result is
correct as 000048040.00500  :
--Use ARITH(COMPAT) with either OPT or NOOPT.
--Use ARITH(EXTEND) with NOOPT.
--Perform the COMPUTE paragraph without the UNTIL clause
  or code it inline.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol 390 2.2 and Cobol Z/Os 3.1

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-06T11:14:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ado1pc$4tb$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b6830b58.0206052350.4a27bd8e@posting.google.com>`

```
For those who have missed it, IBM does *not* encourage the use of
ARITH(EXTEND) in the first place.  So it isn't surprising (to me) that this
BUG wasn't found sooner.
```

---

<a id="t-1f8d665820"></a>
## Thread: NT question — 2 this month / 7 total

_Thread started [2002-05](2002-05.md#t-1f8d665820)._

#### ↳ Re: NT question

- **From:** philhickling@compuserve.com (Phil Hickling)
- **Date:** 2002-06-06T02:16:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6a371be2.0206060116.6ef2faa1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3cf3a1fb.7177872@news.earthlink.net>`

```
Just a thought - it might be as easy to write your own scheduler, if
it is to control your own system jobs.  I wrote one in a day:  a
simple screen giving the operator a list of the 'end of day' jobs,
with the option to tick or not, and to enter some parameters.  The
COBOL program then works through in sequence, calling each job,
waiting for return, continuing on to the next job, etc.  The jobs
could be made to wait for successful completion of the previous job,
or run concurrently, or wait for a given time, or ...

That way you would have complete control, and wouldn't need to learn
any new skills?

Regards,
Phil Hickling
www.willowtreecomputing.co.uk
```

#### ↳ Re: NT question

- **From:** charles.godwin@ca.com (Charles Godwin)
- **Date:** 2002-06-07T02:15:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8035ce7.0206070115.3b99f112@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3cf3a1fb.7177872@news.earthlink.net>`

```
none@none.com (freewheelin) wrote in message news:<3cf3a1fb.7177872@news.earthlink.net>...
> Hello folks,  I thought this would be a good place to ask this
> question.   
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> JF
If all you're using is NT class machines (NT, 2000 and XP) then you
have better choices. The command processor (cmd.exe) is far superior
to the Win 9x version (command.com). There is a lot of stuff, like
complex for loops using files as input, if... else.. processing,
partial string manipulations, prompt for values etc.

However, if you want to build an industrial strength set of scripts
for job control consider Windows Scripting Host (part of 2000 and up
and available free for NT). This allows you to build console scripts
using Jscript, VBScript and optionally, perl (free) and rexx (I don't
know about free). This gives you amazing control and power.
```

Under "Unwarranted Sarcasm" should do.

I wrote a batch Material Release subsystem, and an online clone,
in COBOL, containing a 1000 entry, 325-bytes per entry, table
used to store an assembly demand record and all of the related
subassembly and component demand records of the assembly.

The batch module didn't initialize it a million times per run.
It was more like 100,000.

Regardless, Mr. Coker's test was valid and realistic, IMO.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "gwarner" <gwarner@Bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-06T23:05:06-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<idVL8.46942$_p6.6749344@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <MPG.174a1037b58063398968e@news.charter.net> <abr3vu$3rg$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
I know this thread is rather old but I saw this technique used in dos/vs.
Don't know if it works in newer dialects.
01  State-Table.
      05 st-zero-entry.
           10  st-invalid    pic x(15) value 'invalid state'.
      05 state-table-literals.
           10  st-alabama pic x(15) value 'alabama'.
           10  st-alaska    pic x(15) value 'alaska'.
           10 etc..
      05  filler redefines state-table-literals
           10 State-table-entry occurs 53 pic x(15)

now,
    Move State-table-entry(0) to dtl1-state-name
results in dtl1-state-name having 'invalid state'



Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:abr3vu$3rg$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 13-May-2002, Charles Haatvedt Jr. <clastname@nospam.com> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> But I also have had occasion to work with 80000-89999.  But for this we
need
> to do more bounds checking than IF NUMERIC and your code above.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-07T11:17:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gD0M8.406$mL2.27@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <MPG.174a1037b58063398968e@news.charter.net> <abr3vu$3rg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <idVL8.46942$_p6.6749344@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```
It may or may not 'work' but it is certainly a violation of the rules.

If the 15 bytes preceding STATE-TABLE are owned by your program (and
depending on the brand of compiler and the layout of your WS, they may or
may not), you won't get a memory exception;  but this is not the same as
this being correct code. What if those fifteen bytes are a WS-something or a
printline and happen to contain 'alabama'?

MCM

index"

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

possible
> > > values. It does require an extra "SET" statement when accessing the
> > > table. I use this technique quite frequently in combination with
> > > "indirection" tables in Cobol...  it is very fast...
> >
> > That works for the 00000-99999 tables.  I tend to use subscripts and
have
> > done the same thing.  I have also checked for 00000 and translated it to
> > 100000 - I am not sure which is faster.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-06-07T11:52:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0206071052.58ec5f40@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <MPG.174a1037b58063398968e@news.charter.net> <abr3vu$3rg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <idVL8.46942$_p6.6749344@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```
I tried doing this on MVS COBOL in 1981, but could not get it to clean
compile, though it would pass the compiler's syntax only checking
facility, most annoying!  Like you, I reserved a data area at the
beginning of the 01 level group in front of the table definition to
avoid overwriting other parts of working-storage.

However, such coding does not conform to any of the COBOL standards. 
If you think it worthwhile, consider making a proposal for a change to
the standard, though it will miss the current revision.  Other people
have also suggested such a change and also included the possibility of
explicitly specifying the valid range of subscripts that would be
applicable to a table, so that instead of going from 1-99 for a table
of 99 elements, one could have a range from 10-109, or 0-98, etc.

Robert

"gwarner" <gwarner@Bellsouth.net> wrote in message news:<idVL8.46942$_p6.6749344@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2002-06-08T09:47:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0206080847.62650cc3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <MPG.174a1037b58063398968e@news.charter.net> <abr3vu$3rg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <idVL8.46942$_p6.6749344@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <fcd09c56.0206071052.58ec5f40@posting.google.com>`

```
robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones) wrote in message news:<fcd09c56.0206071052.58ec5f40@posting.google.com>...
> I tried doing this on MVS COBOL in 1981, but could not get it to clean
> compile, though it would pass the compiler's syntax only checking
> facility, most annoying!  Like you, I reserved a data area at the
> beginning of the 01 level group in front of the table definition to
> avoid overwriting other parts of working-storage.

I tried this with varying success using several old MS-DOS compilers
and one recent compiler (cob2c). One of these compilers emulated
several mainframe products. What amazed me was that it was possible to
read beyond the bonundaries of an array by using a subscript that was
too high, particularly if the subscript was a variable instead of a
constant. One compiler did no bounds checking at all (cob2c) nor could
I find a compiler switch to turn it on. Others let me read before and
after an array, however the data was consistently mis-aligned unless
the out of bounds areas were part of the same group item as the array.

I would have expected to find bounds checking rigorously enforced in a
language designed to handle business applications, since robustness
and reliability are essential there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Optimization Techniques.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-08T16:01:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<adtrbj$6f4$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<c78087de.0205020801.2c91b200@posting.google.com> <vQ_C8.61036$gd5.25189801@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <7f64e2ff.0205130750.5edc8a78@posting.google.com> <abortd$oqk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <MPG.174a1037b58063398968e@news.charter.net> <abr3vu$3rg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <idVL8.46942$_p6.6749344@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <fcd09c56.0206071052.58ec5f40@posting.google.com> <7f64e2ff.0206080847.62650cc3@posting.google.com>`

```
The draft of the next COBOL Standard *does* include a "standard" way of
checking boundary violations (both tables and reference modification).
Unlike most (all?) existing "out-of-range" checking, the next Standard
*requires* that one (run-time) validate *ALL* levels of subscripts for a
multi-level table rather than just the "composite" of subscripts being
inside the table.  This is an OPTION and the default is to *not* check for
this (for compatibility with the existing Standard which didn't tell what
happened when you subscripted out of range)
```

to simulate a recursive routine such as quick sort. If time wasn't

<a id="t-84fb9aedb1"></a>
## Thread: Over half — 27 msgs

### Over half

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-06-07T11:18:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020607071858.25074.00000130@mb-fg.aol.com>`

```
The newsgroup comp.risks carried a story saying 
"Over half of software bugs are currently not found until downstream
in the development process leading to significant economic costs." 

I don't understand this.  I would have thought that "a software bug" 
is a problem that is left undiscovered, by definition.  If "upstream"
includes normal coding and testing, then thousands of problems
are encountered and solved during development.  Only a tiny 
handful of problems are unwittingly turned over into production; 
when they reveal themselves in the field, they are properly
labelled "bugs."  
The quotation sounds a little like saying "over half of an island
is surrounded by water." 

Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

#### ↳ Re: Over half

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-07T15:36:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4q4M8.1014$X6.45@nwrddc04.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<20020607071858.25074.00000130@mb-fg.aol.com>`

```
When I was general manager for a VAR firm, we had a rule of thumb we used
regarding the cost of repairs:

If it costs one dollar to fix something in design, it will cost ten dollars
after it's coded and one hundred dollars if it's in the field.

Maybe these "downstream development costs" are the "after it's coded"
numbers.

FWIW, even though the 'rule' above was adopted in the early 1980's, I'll
stick with it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Over half

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-07T14:12:01-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ug1tgo9iv7sh34@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<20020607071858.25074.00000130@mb-fg.aol.com>`

```
Every document has a target audience. Words, understandable
by that audience, are used to inform or pursuade.

The authors of the referenced document, "The Economic Impacts
of Inadequate Infrastructure for Software Testing",
< http://www.nist.gov/director/prog-ofc/report02-3.pdf >,
apparently, use the word "bug" to indicate defects, in general.
The word "defect" is also used. The target audience appears
to be decision makers who might have an economic interest
in software testing.

While the word "bug" has an interesting historical connection
to COBOL, there are many who argue that words, such as,
defect, mistake, or error should be used in place of "bug".

With allowance for the target audience and vocabulary, the
quoted statement, from < news:comp.risks > appears to be
well-supported in the referenced document.

Ross Klatte <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20020607071858.25074.00000130@mb-fg.aol.com...
> The newsgroup comp.risks carried a story saying
> "Over half of software bugs are currently not found until downstream
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Over half

- **From:** "egildone1" <egildone1@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-07T22:16:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ghaM8.36485$092.1126982@news1.east.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<20020607071858.25074.00000130@mb-fg.aol.com> <ug1tgo9iv7sh34@corp.supernews.com> <ck9M8.87650$4i.8833949@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
I heard a long time ago that the use of "bug started with Grace Hopper when
she was a novice programmer on an ancient mainframe that had relays in it
and she found a bug that was squashed between the contacts of the relay
causing the contacts not to close and causing her program not to work
properly.  The name bug for software defect stuck from that point on.

Ed

"egildone1" <egildone1@cox.net> wrote in message

Close. But she wasn't the programmer and the program wasn't in COBOL and she
didn't find the bug and the bug was a moth. The offending critter was
presented to her (in her role as data-processing supervisor) as perhaps the
lamest excuse since "the dog ate my homework" for an overnight run not
completing on time.

In article <OkcM8.156898$%o.15038278@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>,
JerryMouse <nospam@invalid.com> wrote:
>
>"egildone1" <egildone1@cox.net> wrote in message
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>completing on time.

Is the Smithsonian National Museum of American History considered a 
reliable source?

http://americanhistory.si.edu/csr/comphist/objects/bug.htm

--begin quoted text:

American engineers have been calling small flaws in machines "bugs" for 
over a century.  Thomas Edison talked about bugs in electrical circuits in 
the 1870s.

--end quoted text

... and this is supported by a slightly less... reputable site (I try not 
to invoke the ever-popular 'documentation based on some guy's web-page') 
at:

http://jove.eng.yale.edu/pipermail/eas-info/2001/000244.html

--begin quoted text:

"The bug, that perverse and elusive malfunctioning of hardware
and  later of software, was born in the nineteenth century. It was
already  accepted shop slang as early as 1878, when Thomas Edison
described his  style of invention in a letter to a European
representative: 'The first  step is an intuition and it comes with a
burst, then difficulties arise --  this thing gives out and then that
-- "Bugs" -- as such little faults and  difficulties are called --
show themselves, and months of intense watching,  study and labor are
requisite before commercial success -- or failure -- is  certainly
reached.'
      "Edison implies that this use of 'bug' had not begun in his 
laboratory but was already standard jargon."

--end quoted text

DD

Why not quote the rest?

"...When the first computers were built during the early 1940s, people
working on them found bugs in both the hardware of the machines and in  the
programs that ran them.
"In 1947, engineers working on the Mark II computer at Harvard University
found a moth stuck in one of the components.  They taped the insect in their
logbook and labeled it "first actual case of bug being found."  The words
"bug" and "debug" soon became a standard part of the language of computer
programmers."

- **References:** `<20020607071858.25074.00000130@mb-fg.aol.com> <OkcM8.156898$%o.15038278@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <adroqf$c41$1@panix1.panix.com> <BioM8.164925$%o.15530272@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <adu6lm$l90$1@panix1.panix.com>`

<docdwarf@panix.com>
> >>
> >> American engineers have been calling small flaws in machines "bugs" for
> >> over a century.  Thomas Edison talked about bugs in electrical circuits
in
> >> the 1870s.

> >"...When the first computers were built during the early 1940s, people
> >working on them found bugs in both the hardware of the machines and in
the
> >programs that ran them.
> >"In 1947, engineers working on the Mark II computer at Harvard University
> >found a moth stuck in one of the components.  They taped the insect in
their
> >logbook and labeled it "first actual case of bug being found."  The words
> >"bug" and "debug" soon became a standard part of the language of computer
> >programmers."
>

In my community, we have another definition of BUG. There's a periodic
contest using one's "Back Up Gun." That is, a shooting match where the
weapon used is the shooter's backup weapon and not the primary,
fancy-schmancy, pistol normally carried.

These backup guns are generally smallish, .22, .25, and .32 caliber,
derringers, fold-up revolvers, and small automatics. The BUGS hold from 2 to
6 cartridges and make really funny noises on the range when compared to the
hyper-sonic barely-controlled explosions that's standard fare.

Interestingly, some shooters CAN hit a (stationary) moth at 7 meters. Even
with a BUG.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Over half

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-06-13T01:54:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020612215419.20182.00000457@mb-ma.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<ug1tgo9iv7sh34@corp.supernews.com>`

```
>From: "Rick Smith" ricksmith@mfi.net 
>Date: 2002-06-07 14:12 Eastern Daylight Time

>The authors of the referenced document, "The Economic Impacts
>of Inadequate Infrastructure for Software Testing",
>  http://www.nist.gov/director/prog-ofc/report02-3.pdf 
>apparently, use the word "bug" to indicate defects, in general.
...
>With allowance for the target audience and vocabulary, the
>quoted statement, from < news:comp.risks > appears to be
>well-supported in the referenced document.
>

Since there is no hockey game tonight, I have read the cited
document.  It appears to me that your response is full of
sound and fury, but does not signify very much. 

The authors, in my opinion, are dealing exclusively with 
off-the-shelf software.  Their definition of *bug* refers exclusively 
to design flaws, not "defects in general."      

My point, trivial as it was, is that defects in general are for 
the most part encountered / detected and corrected during the 
very earliest stages of development, not after the software has
been released to the public.   
On my first compile, I might very easily hit 500 syntax errors.  
Each of these constitutes a "defect in general."  By the time I
submit a program for integration testing, it is very likely that 
several thousand "defects in general" have been identified
and corrected.  The article and your endorsement thereof
do not pay the slightest attention to that fact.  

I heartily agree that, after a program has passed primivitive
testing and unit testing and integration (system) testing, and 
no error has been detected, and if it then goes into production
and promptly blows up, then the cost of correcting that particular
production error can be very high.  So what?  Neither analysts 
nor programmers make mistakes on purpose.  If they did, it
would be sabotage, not a mistake.  Programmers and 
designers tend to place their confidence in their own infallibility.  
Managers tend to place their confidence in forms that have been 
properly filled out.  Nevertheless, a program that goes into 
production is usually bug-free, as far as it goes.  Some very
useful programs, such as Windows or the Internet, go into 
production with known defects.  Should the Internet have 
been withheld because of its obvious built-in security weaknesses? 
Of course not.  

So some bozos have published an erudite paper which concludes
that "errors are more cheaply corrected sooner rather than later"?  
And another bozo has affirmed the validity of their conclusions?  
These authors, and you, Rick Smith, are just demonstrating
their 20-20 hindsight--a quality endowed to everybody, but in
particular to those lacking in the capacity for rational thought. 


Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Over half

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-13T02:35:44-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uggf115u1uf81@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ug1tgo9iv7sh34@corp.supernews.com> <20020612215419.20182.00000457@mb-ma.aol.com>`

```

Ross Klatte <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20020612215419.20182.00000457@mb-ma.aol.com...
> >From: "Rick Smith" ricksmith@mfi.net
> >Date: 2002-06-07 14:12 Eastern Daylight Time
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> off-the-shelf software.

I am not certain why the distinction, "off-the-shelf", is important.

From page 1-1,
"Software is an intrinsic part of business in the late 20th century.
Virtually every business in the U.S. in every sector depends on it to
aid in the development, production, marketing, and support of its
products and services. This software may be written either by
developers who offer the shrink-wrapped product for sale or
developed by organizations for custom use."

"... developed by organizations for custom use.", is not
off-the-shelf.

Also see section 5.2 on page 5-9, "CUSTOM VERSUS
COMMERCIAL SOFTWARE PRODUCTS"

>  Their definition of *bug* refers exclusively
> to design flaws, not "defects in general."

See page 5-1 - 5-2,
"... bugs (also referred to as defects or errors) ..."

See table 5-3, page 5-3, for "Where Errors are Introduced",
"Requirements Gathering and Analysis/Architectural Design,
Coding/Unit Test,
Integration and Component/RAISE System Test"

The authors do not use "bug" exclusively for design flaws.

> My point, trivial as it was, is that defects in general are for
> the most part encountered / detected and corrected during the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> do not pay the slightest attention to that fact.

The report focused on testing, and, as such, ignored such
early development defects, and explained why.

From the footnote on Page 5-2,

"1 We make the distinction between inadequate software testing and
inadequate programming skills or techniques. For example, Carnegie
Mellon Software Engineering Institute has developed the Personal
Software Process (PSP) and the Team Software Process (TSP)
that are designed to reduce the number of errors in the program when
it is first compiled. In general, the PSP and TSP involve individual
programmers tracking their errors to improve their programming
skills and team members thoroughly reviewing code to identify errors
prior to compiling and run time testing. For this study, we define these
programming activities as up stream and not part of the software
testing process. Thus, the number of errors generated as part of initial
software coding does not change in the counterfactual scenario. It is
the process of identifying and correcting these "exogenous" errors
that changes."

> I heartily agree that, after a program has passed primivitive
> testing and unit testing and integration (system) testing, and
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Of course not.

This problem is covered, in part, in section 3.1.3, page 3-3,
"Lack of a Rigorous Method for Determining When a Product
Is Good Enough to Release".

> So some bozos have published an erudite paper which concludes
> that "errors are more cheaply corrected sooner rather than later"?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> particular to those lacking in the capacity for rational thought.

Their conclusion, from section 8, was "... that the national cost
estimate of an inadequate infrastructure for software testing
is $59.5 billion. The potential cost reduction from feasible
infrastructure improvements is $22.2 billion. This represents
about 0.6 to 0.2 percent of the U.S.'s $10 trillion dollar gross
domestic product (GDP). Software developers accounted
for about 40 percent of impacts, and software users accounted
for the remaining 60 percent."

It is because "errors are more cheaply corrected sooner rather
than later" that allows the conclusion that improvements in the
infrastructure for software testing saves money. Additionally,
Boehm has shown the economic costs associated with
other areas of software development and Humphrey has shown
how to reduce the number of errors that need correcting later.

Finally,

From Page 6-10,
"Table 6-2. Distribution of Bugs Found Based on Introduction Point
The diagonal elements in bold represent the occurrences where
software errors are found in the same development stage where
they are introduced. Occurrences to the right of the bold diagonal
indicate errors found 'downstream' in the product development
process." [See the document for the table.]

This totals to 60.7% or "Over half of software bugs are currently
not found until downstream in the development process leading
to significant economic costs."

You began your comments in your initial post with "I don't
understand this." [Referring to the quote in the above paragraph.]
You then proceeded to supply your own definition for "bug" to
dispute the statement. After reading the report, you continue to
use "your" definitions to comment on the report. Stop it! For you
to "understand this", you must use "their" definitions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Over half

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-13T20:55:00+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D08F894.170DDB31@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ug1tgo9iv7sh34@corp.supernews.com> <20020612215419.20182.00000457@mb-ma.aol.com> <uggf115u1uf81@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> 
> This totals to 60.7% or "Over half of software bugs are currently
> not found until downstream in the development process leading
> to significant economic costs."

Yeah, but I bet that it doesn't give any _realistic_ estimates of 'the
significant economic costs' of finding and eliminating these bugs in the
development and testing process.

> It is because "errors are more cheaply corrected sooner rather
> than later" that allows the conclusion that improvements in the
> infrastructure for software testing saves money.

That is simple statistical incompetence.

It may well be true that the errors found earlier were cheaper to find
and correct, but it does not therefore follow that trying harder or
longer to find more errors will result in a similar bug/cost ratio being
maintained.

If there are 10 bugs that are equally hard to find (but different) in a
piece of code then finding the first one is ten times easier than
finding the tenth one (because initially any of the ten will do). 
However, each bug has a different difficulty of finding.  It is likely
that the first found is in the 'easy' categorey, this may make the 10th
50 times or 100 times or 1000 times harder to find.

And then after spending such time on the 10th, how much should be spent
looking for the 11th ?

There isn't an 11th ?  How do we know ?  Should we spend infinite time
continuing to look or should we have stopped after the 10th ? or the 9th
? or the 8th ?  After all we have no actual idea about how many bugs
there really are, there may not be a 9th or an 8th.

A more realistic conclusion would be that, in any competent shop, bug
searching stops when the cost of finding the next one in pre-deployment
is likely to exceed the cost of finding it in post-deployment (and make
sure the backups are done).



Similar statistical incompetence has led to some managers deciding that
they should convert from Cobol to C.  A Datamation article showed that
Cobol development was something like 40% new work and 60% maintenance
while C shops had 90% new work and 10% maintenance.

Thus, concluded the incompetent manager, converting to C will save over
half of our staff costs or will double our productivity.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Over half

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-06-13T13:03:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aea4sp$fm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ug1tgo9iv7sh34@corp.supernews.com> <20020612215419.20182.00000457@mb-ma.aol.com> <uggf115u1uf81@corp.supernews.com> <3D08F894.170DDB31@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

On 13-Jun-2002, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> Similar statistical incompetence has led to some managers deciding that
> they should convert from Cobol to C.  A Datamation article showed that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> half of our staff costs or will double our productivity.

I like that!    My wife's printer is bad - it probably wouldn't be hard to
fix, but shops require $100 even to LOOK at a printer.  So we are going to
throw it away.   We live in a throwaway society - C code is throwaway.  It
just costs too much to maintain C code.

This wouldn't be bad if we could replace it with off-the-shelf products that
work OK, like whatever printer we buy for my wife.  But if we buy an
off-the-shelf inventory system and need to make extensive modifications -
those modifications are really the same thing as what we call "maintenance"
- changing a product to meet changing requirements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Over half

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-13T13:17:23-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ughkidki1a12ea@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ug1tgo9iv7sh34@corp.supernews.com> <20020612215419.20182.00000457@mb-ma.aol.com> <uggf115u1uf81@corp.supernews.com> <3D08F894.170DDB31@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3D08F894.170DDB31@Azonic.co.nz...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> development and testing process.

The authors never tried to establish such an estimate!

Financial models for software developers are given in section 3.3.
Graphs associated with these models show that increasing
quality (defect removal) leads to exponential increases in costs.

Also note the last paragraph in section 3-3,

"As shown in Figure 3-2b, the quality gap reflects instances where
profit-maximizing software developers do not have the proper
incentives to invest testing resources to achieve the socially optimal
level of software testing. The quality gap illustrates that the greater
the market power of developers, the more costs are shifted toward
users, lowering developers' incentives to invest in quality."

Here, the authors suggest that increased testing reduces
developer profits. Later they show that increased testing reduces
end user costs and conclude that increased testing, overall,
reduces costs to the [US] national economy.

> > It is because "errors are more cheaply corrected sooner rather
> > than later" that allows the conclusion that improvements in the
> > infrastructure for software testing saves money.
>
> That is simple statistical incompetence.

Read the report!

> It may well be true that the errors found earlier were cheaper to find
> and correct, but it does not therefore follow that trying harder or
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> sure the backups are done).

Testing, alone, will not find every defect. It's time to quote from
Humphrey, "A Discipline for Software Engineering", Chapter 6,
in the section, "Code Reviews are More Efficient than Testing".

"While it is true that some defects can be traced quite quickly,
others can be extraordinarily hard to find. In one operating system
example, three experienced engineers worked for three months
to find a subtle system defect that was causing persistent customer
problems. At the time they found this defect, the same code was
being inspected by a different team of five engineers. As an
experiment, this inspection team was not told about the defect.
Within two hours, this team found not only this defect, but also
71 others! Once found, the original defect was trivial to fix."

The report was somewhat biased toward testing as a means
for reducing economic cost. Other than that, I find no problem
with the report.
```

If one programmer in ten days produces a program that has ten bugs,

one day with only one bug, and 40 programmers could do it

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Catching errors early was Re: Over half

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-06-13T09:33:16-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D08910C.933AE5A2@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<gT_N8.37177$gs4.25073@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <20020613084638.15997.00001974@mb-mo.aol.com>`

```
Have you ever found that you can find other people's mistakes easier
than you can catch your own.  Have you ever found holes in other
people's designs or had them find problems with yours?  Is your shop one
of those where the same person does design, code and test (or even just
code and test?  If you are the person doing maintenance, have you ever
been in the position of knowing how to fix a problem but not knowing
enough about the system to test to make sure you didn't cause another
problem?  Have you ever had problems getting a coherent set of test data
so that you could do a test?  

Based on my own answers to these questions, I agree with Michael and I
have never been a manager in the 35+ years I have been in the field.  I
also believe that adequate testing is one of the most under funded and
least respected parts of our field, especially in business programming
where the rules for correctness change (legal changes, business
organization changes, etc.).  Documentation is even less respected and
we do even less to keep that in step with reality.  If we paid proper
attention to review, testing and documentation and made sure that we
assigned people whose talents lie in those areas to those jobs, our
systems would be a lot more reliable.  A good designer and coder may not
be a good tester and their documentation may leave everything to be
desired.  A good documenter might write a terrible program.  

Ross Klatte wrote:
> 
> >From: "Michael Mattias" michael.mattias@gte.net
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Catching errors early was Re: Over half

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-06-13T22:05:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D09179E.2DEC4330@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<gT_N8.37177$gs4.25073@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <20020613084638.15997.00001974@mb-mo.aol.com> <3D08910C.933AE5A2@istar.ca>`

```


"Clark F. Morris, Jr." wrote:

> Have you ever found that you can find other people's mistakes easier
> than you can catch your own.  Have you ever found holes in other
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> desired.  A good documenter might write a terrible program.

I agree with what you write, except the documentation bit - the documentation HAS
TO BE THE PROGRAM SOURCE. Having started out as an analyst, the attempt was to
document the system; inevitably program source changed, all too often a 'quick
fix' to embrace some new business rule, and this was not reflected in the
original documentation. (Introduce all the rules you want, even given a
'stickler' as your manager. Eventually he will move on and there will be a lapse
in documentation standards).

At best, the only part of the original documentation that matters is the overall
program flow design that was drawn up at the conceptual stage. What that document
intended and what is now 'for real' exists only in program source - because
that's how the application is currently functioning - coupled with the current
relevance of any User Manuals - need I add, even User Manuals get out of date !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Catching errors early was Re: Over half

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-06-18T11:23:59-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D0F427F.E5098C2B@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<gT_N8.37177$gs4.25073@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <20020613084638.15997.00001974@mb-mo.aol.com> <3D08910C.933AE5A2@istar.ca> <3D09179E.2DEC4330@shaw.ca>`

```
I don't disagree with your statement about the documentation issue as we
currently do business.  I know that I have normally paid the highest
amount of attention to the program source and found it difficult to keep
the documentation up to date.  However, this attitude is one of the
major problems in our field.  I have come to the conclusion that the
best specification for any upgrade/fixing work is that either the user
specifies a change to the user documentation that would then be
reflected by a change in the system or the change is made because the
system doesn't work the way the documentation says it does. 

Indeed, the question of how to document a system and make certain that
the documentation agrees with the currently functioning product is one
of the most overlooked ones in our field.  Lavish amounts of effort are
put into design documents using tools such as UML and rigidly formatted
word processing documents.  Once these things are approved, the system
is then implemented and starts drifting away from those now largely
static documents.  For internal or single using entity systems, the User
manuals may almost be an afterthought with little or no updating after
initial implementation.  All of this has given our field a bad
reputation.

I believe that this lack of respect for documentation and design
documents as well as the large costs of producing our many times ill
tested systems has led to the revolt of the business community.  As the
hardware has become fast enough to support the inefficiencies of
generalized customizable software, the trend for use of purchased
packages has grown.  However in many ways, these packages and Pete
Dashwood's vision of user generated systems will suffer the same fate as
the single entity systems.  Many of those systems will evolve with
customizing parameters being set with no documentation on the settings
or the reasons for them.  The systems will not be tested after
customization.  The user generated systems will probably have even less
testing than our current systems.  If the user generation process does
not produce documentation and a help system as a by-product the result
will will a black box that works in mysterious ways.  There is an
inherent amount of mystery because systems have to change at the whim of
legislators, management fads and marketing brainstorms.  Many times we
may be making decisions on correctness with no written guidance from the
users or management.  If there is guidance it may be buried over time
within voluminous amounts of memo's.  I believe that OO, use of BAP (the
language for SAP where use of scaling information is by compiler
option), and conversion to new high level language or mix and match
components may actually make an already bad situation worse.  The use of
tools such as repositories if they have the capabilities envisioned in
the SHARE/Guide Language Futures Task Force Report of November 1984 can
help balance this as can effective use of data bases.  

In conclusion, lack of current, usable, maintainable documentation for
both the user and the implementor.  The tools for obtaining this seem to
be primitive or non-existent.  Indeed most of the literature seems to
assume that we will produce good unchanging systems using method X. 
Lack of complete documentation leads to inability to test for
correctness.  Lack of complete documentation leads to modifications that
cause system problems because all of the ramifications of the change
were not easily apparent.  The problem is not a symptom of fortress
COBOL but rather a mindset that far transcends the choice of computer
language and the issue of procedural versus OO.  It is a basic failure
to integrate the necessity of dealing with manageable pieces with making
certain the whole works and a lack of willingness to accurately keep
track of what we are building and have built.   

"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> "Clark F. Morris, Jr." wrote:
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Catching errors early was Re: Over half

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-19T13:44:23+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d107a83_4@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<gT_N8.37177$gs4.25073@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <20020613084638.15997.00001974@mb-mo.aol.com> <3D08910C.933AE5A2@istar.ca> <3D09179E.2DEC4330@shaw.ca> <3D0F427F.E5098C2B@istar.ca>`

```

Clark F. Morris, Jr. <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
news:3D0F427F.E5098C2B@istar.ca...
> I don't disagree with your statement about the documentation issue as we
> currently do business.  I know that I have normally paid the highest
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
All of these arguments only apply as long as SOURCE Code is being
maintained. (And a major reason for the historic success of COBOL has been
that it documents itself when written well.)

If it is OBJECT code that is documented, the documentation will be accurate
because OBJECT code doesn't get Maintained!

That's one reason why the Component approach is so sensible.

Once a component is developed and tested/debugged, its interfaces and
functionality are documented and that is that. It is a piece of encapsulated
functionality that does what it says it does, in the manner that it says it
does, with the data that it says it requires.

So now you want to change it. You must modify the source (if you have it)
and re-release it. If it is re-released without a documentation update (and
in most cases you CAN'T release it without re-wrapping it, which
AUTOMATICALLY regenerates the interface documentation) then it simply won't
work. If you DON'T have the source (and in most cases you won't) then you
must get the vendor to provide added functionality or use a different
component from a different vendor. There can never be a problem of the
documentation not matching what the code does, because the documentation is
automatically accessible with the code.

> Indeed, the question of how to document a system and make certain that
> the documentation agrees with the currently functioning product is one
> of the most overlooked ones in our field.

No it isn't. For the thirty years I have been in this industry writing
COBOL, this has been one of the most re-visited problems in the commercial
computer programming arena. I have seen countless attempts to address it
with standards and software. Some of the software and Methodologies have
been quite ingenious. NONE of it has EVER succeeded! That's why old hand
programmers rely ONLY on the Source code. They've been burned too often with
documentation which failed to be updated, or was never written correctly in
the first place, or was produced by some fancy  package that only documents
what IT thinks is important...

> Lavish amounts of effort are
> put into design documents using tools such as UML and rigidly formatted
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, it has. But it wasn't ONLY this...<G>

> I believe that this lack of respect for documentation and design
> documents as well as the large costs of producing our many times ill
> tested systems has led to the revolt of the business community.

It is certainly a contributing factor. (Some would argue that the business
community (read..."Users") have ALWAYS been revolting, but we won't go
there...<G>). It isn't the documentation (or lack of it) which has killed
in-house IT for the business community, it is the consistent failure to
deliver systems that truly support the business.

Too little; too late.

And the superior intimidating attitude of the technocrats, who regard the
business people as uneducated clods for the most part, hasn't helped either.
Business users going cap-in-hand to IT for systems, then getting systems
that were way below what they needed, and being treated like imbeciles by
smart-arse programmers, is a scenario that is obviously intolerable.
Eventually, it HAD to crack. As Users have become more "computer literate"
(blame IBM for inventing the PC... NOT Bill Gates for being unscrupulous and
in the right place at the right time...<G>) they have decided to do their
"own thing" and simply by-pass the IT fortress. At least if they get it
wrong they can fix it themselves, without having to beg for assistance and
then be told it might get looked at in three months, as long as Management
authorize another zillion dollars on the IT dept. budget...

> As the
> hardware has become fast enough to support the inefficiencies of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the single entity systems.

My vision of user generated systems is becoming more apparent every day.
Packages like SAP, Siebel, and Chordiant are installed once, configured
according to the Business requirement, and then they run. Many companies are
finding that it is MUCH cheaper to do some basic BPR (Business Process
Re-Engineering) so the process fits the Package (Shock! Horror! Heresy!...),
than it is to support an IT department to tailor the Package to the existing
Business, and then maintain it on an ongoing basis. There is nowhere near
the same requirement for technical expertise that there was even 10 years
ago. Yes, there have been some horror stories, but these are becoming fewer
and fewer as the Packages are improved, and the Users are becoming more
comfortable with computers.

> Many of those systems will evolve with
> customizing parameters being set with no documentation on the settings
> or the reasons for them.  The systems will not be tested after
> customization.  The user generated systems will probably have even less
> testing than our current systems.

This is purely supposition, and, in my experience, not supported by the
facts. Yes, if there is bad Management of a Package installation the
scenario you describe could come true... However, these lessons seem to have
been learned and implementations now are subject to proper scrutiny and the
processes, parameters, and test base cases and exceptions are fully
documented before the system goes live. To do otherwise would be stupid,
and, despite what you may think, IT people do not have a monoply on
smartness... Users recognise the need for it to be documented and processed
iteratively, just as we do.


>If the user generation process does
> not produce documentation and a help system as a by-product the result
> will will a black box that works in mysterious ways.

Pointless argument. They DO produce documentation AND on-line Help.

(Anyway, the same is true for Fortress systems).

> There is an
> inherent amount of mystery because systems have to change at the whim of
> legislators, management fads and marketing brainstorms.

Yes, they do. And it is a real good feeling when this change can be
outsourced, or the changed legal requirements are provided as an update by
your vendor...

> Many times we
> may be making decisions on correctness with no written guidance from the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> components may actually make an already bad situation worse.

Why? ABAP is an excellent (and COBOL like) language for configuring SAP. It
seems quite sensible to me to use compiler options for scaling (you only
generate the system once), and, having used "mix and match" components for
the last three years with great success, I see no problems with any of this.
What's your beef?


> The use of
> tools such as repositories if they have the capabilities envisioned in
> the SHARE/Guide Language Futures Task Force Report of November 1984 can
> help balance this as can effective use of data bases.
>

And all of the Packages I mentioned above, use the Relational Model for
their repositories. Often they co-exist with ORACLE but this is not a
requirement.  I have seen Siebel implementations which used SQL Server quite
successfully and Chordiant implementation where the repository was DB2. So
the Packages do not preclude the use of repositories or well designed
databases, on the contrary; they require it. Again, what's the beef?

> In conclusion, lack of current, usable, maintainable documentation for
> both the user and the implementor.  The tools for obtaining this seem to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> were not easily apparent.

ONLY if you have systems based on SOURCE code. (See above)

> The problem is not a symptom of fortress
> COBOL but rather a mindset that far transcends the choice of computer
> language and the issue of procedural versus OO.

It is a symptom of fortress COBOL (that's MY term, by the way, but I'm
flattered that you used it and did so correctly in the context I
intended...<G>) inasmuch as COBOL is totally focussed towards maintaining
SOURCE code. For many years this was right and proper as there was no
alternative. Now there is, and what's more, it is a better alternative.
Hence we see the sad decline of COBOL and its peers (procedural languages in
general, actually).

> It is a basic failure
> to integrate the necessity of dealing with manageable pieces with making
> certain the whole works and a lack of willingness to accurately keep
> track of what we are building and have built.
>
I don't think it is quite as bad as that. However, we could agree that not
enough attention is generally paid to system documentation (of all kinds).

Systems (like component based design and manufacture) where at least the
BASIC documentation is produced AUTOMATICALLY  must solve this problem
better than systems where the human programmer has to author the
documentation. (JAVA can produce documentation from its own source code, and
it is a useful feature which I have used from time to time. Especially with
Java Beans where I tend to use copious comments.)

Pete.

> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > people's designs or had them find problems with yours?  Is your shop
one
> > > of those where the same person does design, code and test (or even
just
> > > code and test?  If you are the person doing maintenance, have you ever
> > > been in the position of knowing how to fix a problem but not knowing
> > > enough about the system to test to make sure you didn't cause another
> > > problem?  Have you ever had problems getting a coherent set of test
data
> > > so that you could do a test?
> > >
> > > Based on my own answers to these questions, I agree with Michael and I
> > > have never been a manager in the 35+ years I have been in the field.
I
> > > also believe that adequate testing is one of the most under funded and
> > > least respected parts of our field, especially in business programming
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > systems would be a lot more reliable.  A good designer and coder may
not
> > > be a good tester and their documentation may leave everything to be
> > > desired.  A good documenter might write a terrible program.
> >
> > I agree with what you write, except the documentation bit - the
documentation HAS
> > TO BE THE PROGRAM SOURCE. Having started out as an analyst, the attempt
was to
> > document the system; inevitably program source changed, all too often a
'quick
> > fix' to embrace some new business rule, and this was not reflected in
the
> > original documentation. (Introduce all the rules you want, even given a

a lapse

> >
> > At best, the only part of the original documentation that matters is the
overall

document

because

date !

## Thread: COBOL to VB converter? — 19 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

#### ↳ Re: COBOL to VB converter?

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-06-07T19:13:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0206071813.5fca6783@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<z8aM8.2169$Kz1.44266@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```
"Albert Paul" <alpaul1@optonline.net> wrote in message news:<z8aM8.2169$Kz1.44266@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>...
> Is there a COBOL to VB converter?

At risk of responding to a troll...

No.  (Well someone here might offer such services, but there's no good
conversion path from COBOL to VB).

The main reason I see people even attempting this is because they
either want GUI or ODBC or both.  These features - GUI and or ODBC are
available today to COBOL programmers - so theres no business case to
be made for conversion.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL to VB converter?

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-07T21:17:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<adrpgs02ptv@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<z8aM8.2169$Kz1.44266@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```

"Albert Paul" <alpaul1@optonline.net> wrote in message
news:z8aM8.2169$Kz1.44266@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net...
> Is there a COBOL to VB converter?

Here's a thread from this group that tangents down this path:

http://groups.google.com/groups?hl=en&lr=&ie=UTF8&oe=UTF8&threadm=YYOb8.8366
1%24d34.5547916%40bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com&rnum=1&prev=/groups%3Fhl%3Den%
26lr%3D%26ie%3DUTF8%26oe%3DUTF8%26selm%3DYYOb8.83661%2524d34.5547916%2540bin
8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com

There are apparently organizations that will do this work, costed per line.

I've had a few experiences moving a code base from one platform to another,
and have found marginal use for mechanical conversions.  The problem lies in
the domain of the source code -- it's just too huge for any meta-compiler to
take all of the factors into account.  As a result, many errors are
introduced, some of which can be subtle.  Ultimately, you end up analyzing
all of the code anyhow.

The most recent mass conversion I assisted on, was moving from one COBOL
screen interface unit to another.  Periodically, we would identify patterns
of translation.  In those cases, I either used a good search and replace
tool (like UltraEdit,) or wrote some small utility that could do the job.

I wish you luck in your endeavor.
```

John:

I agree that Modula-2 is a wonderful language.  I also know that you
are aware of the importance of adding a suffix of "#", "++" or "beans"
to all programming languages in order to bring them up-to-date in this
world of modern programming languages.

Oh....I almost forgot....you can also modernize a programming language
by adding a prefix of "Visual" as well!

I must have had a big bowl of Kellogg's Cynical Flakes today for
breakfast!!

;-)

By the way....I'm really sorry that we didn't get to meet at COBOL
Expo.  I was running around like a madman (as usual) and I know that
you were very busy as well.  I have heard nothing but positive
comments regarding the Expo and have been told that it will be an
annual event.

That's good news for all COBOL programmers.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL to VB converter?

- **From:** "Ira Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-16T13:22:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d0cd51e$1@giga.realtime.net>`
- **References:** `<z8aM8.2169$Kz1.44266@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <F0FM8.24564$86.625287@twister1.libero.it>`

```
"It is not difficult to do"?  Translating between any pair of langauges
is always hard.  The question should really be,
"is there an economic justification large enough to justify the investment"?
This of course turns on just how hard it is to do.

We provide tools to lower the costs of constructing such translators.
And we have a significant head start by having both VB and COBOL
grammars, if those are what are really needed.
See http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/Services/LegacyMigration.html
I'll be clear: building one isn't cheap.
It generally isn't worth for less than 100K SLOC of code.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL to VB converter?

- **From:** "Dan" <Dan@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-11T04:30:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<U1fN8.35329$pw3.396@sccrnsc03>`
- **References:** `<z8aM8.2169$Kz1.44266@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```
Actually,  I wrote a utility a while back that would take a COBOL copybook
and generate a VB .bas file with the copybook as a VB user defined data type
with functions to parse it out from a string and back into a string.  Very
handy for flat file I/O though it only converts copybooks and isn't a true
COBOL to VB generator.   If anyone is interested in it I can make the source
code available.

Dan

OK.  I put it up on my site.  You can download the project at
http://www.auction-sentry.com/VBCopybookSource.zip.  Probably not my best
example of "easy to follow" code I'm afraid.  I wrote it 4 years ago as a
utility for myself when I was converting a COBOL batch system to VB batch
(yes, there are actually companies using VB for batch programming).  It
doesn't have any error handling I'm afraid but it would be easy to add it.

Dan

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to write a Callable file handler in Windows environment - Urgent

- **From:** san@softima.com (san)
- **Date:** 2002-06-07T21:01:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c10d0806.0206072001.a3f69e1@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi All,
   I am new to the area of writing Callable File Handler. I need to
write one for completely windows based application (OS - Windows NT
Workstation) using Visual C++ 6.0 tool. I don't know the exact
syntaxes and the header files included for it. Can anyone help me in
this regard? I am in urgent need to develop this task. If possible
please provide sample code.

Thanks a lot in advance for any help or guidance.

San
```

#### ↳ Re: How to write a Callable file handler in Windows environment - Urgent

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-06-08T07:36:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0206080636.6b6be6fa@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c10d0806.0206072001.a3f69e1@posting.google.com>`

```
There is not enough information here for us to be of much help. 
Here's some questions that might help us to get the information so we
can assist.

Normally when we see "Callable File Handler" it's something like an
"External File Handler" for interfacing with a particular COBOL
vendor's product.  For example, both MicroFocus and Fujitsu have
External file handler specifications so that one may create such a
callable routine and plug it in, in place of or in addition to the
native vendor provided file handler.

Are you talking about writing one of these? 

The other thing we see is a desire to access COBOL data from other
languages via ODBC or via some "callable file handler" invoked from
this other language.  In these cases the handler can be designed in
many different ways.

Which of these are you attempting, or is it something else entirely?



san@softima.com (san) wrote in message news:<c10d0806.0206072001.a3f69e1@posting.google.com>...
> Hi All,
>    I am new to the area of writing Callable File Handler. I need to
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> San
```

- **From:** san@softima.com (san)
- **Date:** 2002-06-12T00:58:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c10d0806.0206112358.246b7e0b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c10d0806.0206072001.a3f69e1@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0206080636.6b6be6fa@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Thane,
   I am sorry for the ambiguity in my statement. I am talking about
the second of the two cases mentioned by you i.e. I need to write a
IDXFORMAT4 File Editor using Visual C console application, which will
be called from a COBOL application. I will be using the External File
Handler (EXTFH) of MicroFocus. Thanks for trying to help me. I hope to
get a reply soon.

Thanks
San.

## Thread: MVS JCL — 26 this month / 27 total

### Re: MVS JCL

- **From:** "David Robinson" <david@nighttime.demon.invalid>
- **Date:** 2002-06-08T11:18:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94psda.eck.ln@pingu.tunstall.ave>`
- **References:** `<iBfM8.1218$bf.328206@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk>`

```
"Bloggs" <bloggs@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:iBfM8.1218$bf.328206@news-binary.blueyonder.co.uk...
> I am reading a book on JCL by Doug Lowe, and have just come across a term
> which I cannot find explained in the book. I have also searched the
Internet
> but to no avail the term is Dunning File. The context is an accounts
> receivable file is to be read against a customer master file and Dunning
> data is extracted to create a Dunning File??? Any help please, would be
> appreciated.
>

My area was General (Nominal) Ledger at the last place I worked, but I'll
have a go. Our Accounts Receivable module could produce Dunning letters to
be send out to customers. IIRC, Dunning letters were sent if a customer
hadn't paid a bill within a certain time, e.g. 30/60/90 days and have a
stronger worded letter for each milestone that passed. I'd assume[1] that a
Dunning file would contain a list of customers that had exceeded particular
payment milestones and that the Dunning file would then be used by another
job to generate Dunning letters.

Hope this helps.
David

[1] Yeah, I know, never assume...
```

Gah... 'I searched the Internet' does not seem to include 'I looked it up 
in the dictionary'; from http://www.m-w.com (input: dunning):

begin quoted text:

Main Entry: 3dun
Function: transitive verb
Inflected Form(s): dunned; dun�ning
Etymology: origin unknown
Date: circa 1626
1 : to make persistent demands upon for payment

--end quoted text

(We used to call these POD letters, pronounced 'Pee Oh Dee'... for 'Pay Or 
Die!')

DD
```

Hillbilly Jazz.  But then I just can't imagine a country or bluegrass
song being played freeform!  Kind of boggles the mind.
