<a id="t-683462a8c6"></a>
## Thread: looking for cobol/cics emulator for didactical purposes — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### looking for cobol/cics emulator for didactical purposes

- **From:** "Michela" <michela.dit@libero.it>
- **Date:** 2002-06-09T21:04:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VpPM8.28438$K4.709008@twister2.libero.it>`

```
hello all,
I'm a junior programmer. As in subject: I need to practice at home. I tried
CA Realia but CICS doesn't work properly. I'm looking for a Cobol/CICS
emulator, free and lasting in time, ever if limited in features, can you
suggest any? thank you
Michela
```

#### ↳ Re: looking for cobol/cics emulator for didactical purposes

- **From:** charles.godwin@ca.com (Charles Godwin)
- **Date:** 2002-06-17T04:52:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8035ce7.0206170352.7fd416e5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<VpPM8.28438$K4.709008@twister2.libero.it>`

```
Michela

I want to respond to the two subjects you bring up in this email.

1) You are looking for free COBOL/CICS development environment. I
don't think you're going to find one. If you are a student, I would
hope your school would provide for internet connectivity to your lab
to allow for practice and training.

2) What do you mean by your statement "I tried CA Realia but CICS
doesn't work properly"? I am the development manager for this product
and find this a rather sweeping condemnation of our product. If it's
true I'd like to fix it quickly. If not, precisely what was it that
didn't work?

Charles Godwin
Computer Associates
Development Manager
Advantage CA-Realia Workbench
Charles.Godwin@ca.com


"Michela" <michela.dit@libero.it> wrote in message news:<VpPM8.28438$K4.709008@twister2.libero.it>...
> hello all,
> I'm a junior programmer. As in subject: I need to practice at home. I tried
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Michela
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: looking for cobol/cics emulator for didactical purposes

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-18T03:53:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D0EAECD.B4C77AE9@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<VpPM8.28438$K4.709008@twister2.libero.it> <c8035ce7.0206170352.7fd416e5@posting.google.com>`

```
Charles Godwin wrote:
> 
> Michela
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Charles.Godwin@ca.com

I evaluated Realia CICS for my company back in 1992, when the current
release of Realia COBOL was 4.2, I believe.  We weren't able to
emulate the way our CICS production application works, because it had
conversational tasks and used the EXEC CICS START command.  But for
more typical CICS applications involving BMS maps,
pseudo-conversational programs and VSAM files it was quite good at
emulating CICS in a single user mode.  I also like the screen painter
for BMS maps.  It was better than what we were using on the mainframe.

I would imagine that the current release of Realia CICS would have
more features.  Back in 1992, I was running it in an OS/2 command line
window.  I think it would just fit in a DOS box under Windows.

My impression at the time was that Realia CICS was a good option for
off-loading CICS development and unit-testing to a PC.  

I really liked the Realia COBOL compiler, and wish I could afford one
for home use.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: looking for cobol/cics emulator for didactical purposes

- **From:** "Michela" <michela.dit@libero.it>
- **Date:** 2002-06-30T20:43:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<64KT8.90494$j65.2188102@twister1.libero.it>`
- **References:** `<VpPM8.28438$K4.709008@twister2.libero.it> <c8035ce7.0206170352.7fd416e5@posting.google.com>`

```
Charles,
Thank you for responding my message.

> 1) You are looking for free COBOL/CICS development environment. I
> don't think you're going to find one.


I agree, and no, I'm not a student so I can't depend on a school.

> 2) What do you mean by your statement "I tried CA Realia but CICS
> doesn't work properly"? I am the development manager for this product
> and find this a rather sweeping condemnation of our product.


I apologize if you feel offended. I didn't mean to condemn Realia, on wich I
learnt COBOL infact. I just tried to explain in a few words the situation.
Just to be clear, this is exactly what happened:

Cobol programs worked properly

The BMS part worked properly too

Sometimes, say once out of ten, CICS transactions weren't recognized. We
checked tables definitions and all other situations we thought could
determine the problem (e.g. we tried another machine, uninstalled programs
that could potentially disturb the processes, checked silly mistypes and so
on).
I am not accusing your product for us not being able to install or use it
properly, and stated that "it didn't work" because the problem was there,
being the cause our incapability or anything else.
I wish I could be more precise about what happpened but the course took
place more than one year ago and since then I worked on host. I'm looking
for CICS because I can not work on it at job.
Thank you
Michela
```

---

<a id="t-44a0776890"></a>
## Thread: help — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### help

- **From:** degoble@gtech (David. E. Goble)
- **Date:** 2002-06-10T10:38:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d047bd3.8644424@news.adelaide.on.net>`

```
Hi all;

Iam trying to add to my program, but I have a problem.

The output is;

          Name                  Break             Personal
     Surname         First     Handicap   handicap  Best  Grade
    Ashenden      Rodney (Rod)   600        178   446    D <----
    Brusnahan     John           100        111   500    C
    Canham        Richard        078        158   522    C
    Gloyne        Jonathon (Jon) 111        118   491    D <----
    Goble         David          600        218   405    D <----
    Harrison      Terry          116        133   484    D
    Miller        Dan            189        210   411    D
    Starr         Malcolm (Jeff) 122        127   478    D
    Waller        Gregory (Greg) 121        143   479    D

the break handicaps marked (<---) should be equal to their handicaps.

Sorry but I think the complete code and data files, would be needed.
So they are below;

*********************
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
      *************************
       PROGRAM-ID. pbhcaps.
       AUTHOR. David. E. Goble.
       INSTALLATION. Gtech_Computing
                     for
                     Kangaroo Island Pistol Club.
       DATE-WRITTEN. 26/11/1999.
       DATE-COMPILED.
       SECURITY. Gtech_Computing, free to distribute.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
      **********************
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
      *----------------------
       SOURCE-COMPUTER. 486DX2-66.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER. 386SX-33.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
      *---------------------
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT f100-matches-file
               ASSIGN TO DISK "constant/Matches.mst"
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
               ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
               FILE STATUS IS w120-file-status.
           SELECT f300-scores-file
               ASSIGN TO DISK "data/scores.dat"
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               RECORD KEY IS f310-indexed-key
               FILE STATUS IS w120-file-status.
           SELECT f400-pbhcap-file
               ASSIGN TO DISK "data/pbhcap.dat"
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               RECORD KEY IS f410-indexed-key
               FILE STATUS IS w120-file-status.
           SELECT f500-calibras-file
               ASSIGN TO DISK "constant/Calibras.mst"
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
               ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
               FILE STATUS IS w120-file-status.
           SELECT f700-initial-file
               ASSIGN TO DISK "initial/initial.dat"
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
               ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
               FILE STATUS IS w120-file-status.
           SELECT f900-report-file
               ASSIGN TO DISK "data/report.rpt"
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
               ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL
               FILE STATUS IS w120-file-status.
       DATA DIVISION.
      ***************
       FILE SECTION.
      *-------------
       FD  f100-matches-file
           LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
           RECORD CONTAINS 67 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS f100-matches-rec.
       01 f100-matches-rec.
           05 f110-matches-match               Pic x(29).
           05 f120-grades-gradem               Pic 9(03).
           05 f130-grades-gradea               Pic 9(03).
           05 f140-grades-gradeb               Pic 9(03).
           05 f150-grades-gradec               Pic 9(03).
           05 f160-match-cal177                Pic 9.
           05 f160-match-cal22s                Pic 9.
           05 f160-match-cal22l                Pic 9.
           05 f160-match-calcf                 Pic 9.
           05 f160-match-cal32                 Pic 9.
           05 f160-match-cal38                 Pic 9.
           05 f160-match-cal9mm                Pic 9.
           05 f160-match-cal357                Pic 9.
           05 f160-match-cal44                 Pic 9.
           05 f160-match-cal45                 Pic 9.
           05 f170-match-stages                Pic 9.
           05 f171-mstage1-max                 Pic 9(03).
           05 f172-mstage2-max                 Pic 9(03).
           05 f173-mstage3-max                 Pic 9(03).
           05 f174-mstage4-max                 Pic 9(03).
           05 f180-matches-max                 Pic 9(03).
       FD  f300-scores-file
           LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
           RECORD CONTAINS 65 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS f300-scores-rec.
       01 f300-scores-rec.
           05 f310-indexed-key.
               10 f311-shooters-name.
                   15 f311b-sname-last         Pic x(15).
                   15 f311a-sname-first        Pic x(15).
               10 f312-match-shot              Pic 9(02).
               10 f313-match-date.
                   15  f313a-year              Pic 9(04).
                   15  f313b-month             Pic 9(02).
                   15  f313c-day               Pic 9(02).
           05 f340-calibra                     Pic x(03).
           05 f350-stage1                      Pic 9(03).
           05 f360-stage2                      Pic 9(03).
           05 f370-stage3                      Pic 9(03).
           05 f380-stage4                      Pic 9(03).
           05 f390-offgun                      Pic 9(03).
           05 f395-hcap-attime                 Pic 9(03).
           05 f397-bhcap-attime                Pic 9(03).
           05 f396-grade-attime                Pic x.
       FD  f400-pbhcap-file
           LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
           RECORD CONTAINS 51 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS f400-pbhcap-rec.
       01 f400-pbhcap-rec.
           05 f410-indexed-key.
               10 f409-match                   Pic 9(02).
               10 f408-mname.
                   15 f408a-lname              Pic x(15).
                   15 f408a-fname              Pic x(15).
           05 f420-score1                      Pic 9(03).
           05 f430-score2                      Pic 9(03).
           05 f440-score3                      Pic 9(03).
           05 f450-pbest                       Pic 9(03).
           05 f460-hcap                        Pic 9(03).
           05 f465-bhcap                       Pic 9(03).
           05 f470-grade                       Pic x.
       FD  f500-calibras-file
           LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
           RECORD CONTAINS 03 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS f500-calibras-rec.
       01 f500-calibras-rec.
           05 f510-calibra                     Pic x(03).
       FD  f700-initial-file
           LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
           RECORD CONTAINS 58 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS f700-initial-rec.
       01 f700-initial-rec.
           05 f710-iname.
               10 f710-first                   Pic x(15).
               10 f710-last                    Pic x(15).
           05 f720-imatch                      Pic 9(02).
           05 f730-cal                         Pic x(03).
           05 f740-date.
               10  f741-day                    Pic 9(02).
               10  f742-month                  Pic 9(02).
               10  f743-year                   Pic 9(04).
           05 f750-stage1                      Pic 9(03).
           05 f760-stage2                      Pic 9(03).
           05 f770-stage3                      Pic 9(03).
           05 f780-stage4                      Pic 9(03).
           05 f790-score                       Pic 9(03).
       FD  f900-report-file
           LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
           RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS f900-report-rec.
       01 f900-report-rec                      Pic x(80).
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      *------------------------
       01 w100-program-switches.
           05 w110-end-of-file                 Pic x.
               88 w111-eof                     value 'y', 'Y'.
           05 w120-file-status                 Pic x(02).
           05 w140-press-a-key                 Pic x.
           05 w160-cancel                      Pic x.
               88 w161-cancel                  value 'y', 'Y'.
       01 w200-date-variables.
           05  w210-system-date.
               10  w211-year                   Pic 9(04).
               10  w212-month                  Pic 9(02).
               10  w213-day                    Pic 9(02).
           05 w220-Date                        Pic x(10).
       01 w300-vars.
           05 w310-lengths.
               10 w311-lenA                    Pic 9(02).
               10 w312-lenB                    Pic 9(02).
               10 w313-center                  Pic 9(02).
           05 w320-temp-hcap                   Pic 9(04).
           05 w325-temp-bhcap                  Pic 9(04).
           05 w330-stage                       Pic 9.
           05 w340-stage-val                   Pic 9(03).
           05 w350-line-count                  Pic 9(02).
           05 w360-old-match                   Pic 9(02).
           05 w370-old-name                    Pic x(30).
           05 w380-aggregate                   Pic 9(04).
       01 w600-actions.
           05 a-to-continue                    Pic x(32)
                               value "Press the return key to
continue".
           05 a-anymore                        Pic x(26)
                                     value "Any more members?
[Yy/Nn]:".
           05 match-selection                  Pic 9(02).
           05 cal-selection                    Pic 9(02).
           05 selector                         Pic x.
               88 sup                           value"U", "u", "<",
",".
               88 sdown                        value "D", "d", ">",
".".
               88 sselect                                value "S",
"s".
               88 squick                                 value "Q",
"q".
               88 sdetailed                              value "D",
"d".
               88 sreport                                value "R",
"r".
               88 ssend                                  value "E",
"e".
               88 sgradings                              value "G",
"g".
               88 sscores                                value "S",
"s".
               88 scancel                                value "C",
"c".
           05 instructions.
               10 i1                           Pic x(80) value
       "To move up press [U/u/,/<], To move down press [D/d/./>], ".
               10 i2                           Pic x(80) value
       "To select current value press [S/s], to cancel press [C/c]".
       01 w700-matches-table.
           05 w705-match-sub                   Pic 9(02).
           05 w710-by-match OCCURS 16 TIMES INDEXED BY w710-match-inx.
               10 w711-match                   Pic x(29).
               10 w711a-stages                 Pic 9(01).
               10 w711d-out-of                 Pic 9(03).
               10 w712-by-grade OCCURS 4 TIMES
                                              INDEXED BY
w912-grade-inx.
                   15 w712b-grade              Pic 9(03).
               10 w713-match-calibra OCCURS 11 TIMES
                                                INDEXED BY
w713-cal-inx.
                   15 w713a-cal                Pic 9.
                   15 w713b-cal-type           Pic x(03).
               10 w714-per-stage OCCURS 4 TIMES
                                                INDEXED BY
w714-per-inx.
                   15 w714a-stage-max          Pic 9(03).
       01 w900-msg.
           05 w990-display-message             Pic x(70).
           05 w980-display-action              Pic x(80).
       01 w1000-gradings.
           05 w1100-heading1.
               10 w1110-date                   Pic x(10).
               10 filler                       Pic x(15) value spaces.
               10 filler                       Pic x(27)
                                    value "Kangaroo Island Pistol
Club".
               10 filler                       Pic x(19) value spaces.
               10 filler                       Pic x(06) value "Page:
".
               10 w1120-page                   Pic 9(03).
           05 w1200-heading2.
               10 filler                       Pic x(33) value spaces.
               10 filler                       Pic x(14)
                                                 value "Match
Gradings".
               10 filler                       Pic x(33) value spaces.
           05 w1400-control1.
               10 w1410-match-name             Pic x(29).
               10 filler                       Pic x(51) value spaces.
           05 w1500-detailheading.
               10 w1510-detailheading1.
                   15 filler                   Pic x(22) value spaces.
                   15 filler                   Pic x(04) value "Name".
                   15 filler                   Pic x(16) value spaces.
                   15 filler                   Pic x(10)
                                                     value "  Break
".
                   15 filler                   Pic x(11) value spaces.
                   15 filler                   Pic x(08)
                                                       value
"Personal".
                   15 filler                   Pic x(20) value spaces.
               10 w1520-detailheading2.
                   15 filler                   Pic x(13) value spaces.
                   15 filler                   Pic x(07)
                                                        value
"Surname".
                   15 filler                   Pic x(09) value spaces.
                   15 filler                   Pic x(05) value
"First".
                   15 filler                   Pic x(09) value spaces.
                   15 filler                   Pic x(08)
                                                       value
"Handicap".
                   15 filler                   Pic x(03) value spaces.
                   15 filler                   Pic x(08)
                                                       value
"handicap".
                   15 filler                   Pic x(02) value spaces.
                   15 filler                   Pic x(04) value "Best".
                   15 filler                   Pic x(02) value spaces.
                   15 filler                   Pic x(05) value
"Grade".
                   15 filler                   Pic x(05) value spaces.
           05 w1600-detail-line.
               10 filler                       Pic x(09) value spaces.
               10 w1620-lname                  Pic x(15).
               10 filler                       Pic x value spaces.
               10 w1610-fname                  Pic x(15).
               10 filler                       Pic x(02) value spaces.
               10 w1630-score1                 Pic x(03) value spaces.
               10 filler                       Pic x value spaces.
               10 w1640-score2                 Pic 9(03).
               10 filler                       Pic x value spaces.
               10 w1650-score3                 Pic x(03) value spaces.
               10 filler                       Pic x(05) value spaces.
               10 w1660-hcap                   Pic 9(03).
               10 filler                       Pic x(03) value spaces.
               10 w1670-pbest                  Pic 9(03).
               10 filler                       Pic x(04) value spaces.
               10 w1680-grade                  Pic x.
               10 filler                       Pic x(06) value spaces.
       SCREEN SECTION.
      *---------------
       01 ss100-Main-screen.
           05 BLANK SCREEN.
           05 LINE  1 COLUMN 01 value "Kangaroo Island Pistol Club".
           05         COLUMN 64 value "DATE: ".
           05         COLUMN 70 Pic x(10) from w220-Date.
       01 ss200-first-entries.
           05 ss210-enter-match.
               10 LINE  3 COLUMN 10 value "Match: ".
               10         COLUMN 17 Pic x(29) FROM

w711-match(match-selection).
               10 LINE 24 COLUMN 65 Pic x TO selector auto.
       01 ss900-msg-screen.
           05 LINE 22 COLUMN 01 value "MESSAGE: ".
           05 LINE 23 COLUMN 10 Pic x(70) FROM w990-display-message.
           05 LINE 24 COLUMN 01 Pic x(80) FROM w980-display-action.
           05 LINE 24 COLUMN 65 Pic x TO w140-press-a-key.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      ********************
       0000000-MAIN-MODULE.
      *====================
           PERFORM 1000000-INITIALISE-MODULE.
           PERFORM 2000000-PROCESS-MODULE UNTIL w111-eof.
           PERFORM 3000000-END-MODULE.
           STOP RUN.
       1000000-INITIALISE-MODULE.
      *==========================
           PERFORM 1300000-SET-DATE.
           PERFORM 1400000-INITIALISE-VARS.
           PERFORM 1500000-READ-INITIAL-FILE.
       1300000-SET-DATE.
      *=================
           MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO w210-system-date.
           STRING  w213-day "/"
                   w212-month "/"
                   w211-year
                   DELIMITED BY SIZE
                   INTO w220-Date
           END-STRING.
       1400000-INITIALISE-VARS.
      *========================
           MOVE "n" TO w110-end-of-file.
          INITIALIZE f300-scores-rec.
           OPEN INPUT  f700-initial-file
               INPUT   f500-calibras-file
               INPUT   f100-matches-file
               I-O     f300-scores-file
               I-O     f400-pbhcap-file
               OUTPUT  f900-report-file.
           PERFORM 141000-LOAD-MATCH-ARRAY
               VARYING w710-match-inx FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL w710-match-inx>15 OR w111-eof.
           MOVE 1 TO match-selection.
           PERFORM 141110-LOAD-CALIBRA-ARRAY
               VARYING w713-cal-inx FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL w713-cal-inx>11 OR w111-eof.
          MOVE "n" TO w110-end-of-file.
       141000-LOAD-MATCH-ARRAY.
      *========================
           READ f100-matches-file
               AT END
                   MOVE "y" TO w110-end-of-file
               NOT AT END
                   PERFORM 141100-LOAD-MATCH
           END-READ.
       141100-LOAD-MATCH.
      *==================
           MOVE f110-matches-match TO w711-match(w710-match-inx).
           MOVE f180-matches-max TO w711d-out-of(w710-match-inx).
           MOVE f120-grades-gradem TO w712b-grade(w710-match-inx, 1).
           MOVE f130-grades-gradea TO w712b-grade(w710-match-inx, 2).
           MOVE f140-grades-gradeb TO w712b-grade(w710-match-inx, 3).
           MOVE f150-grades-gradec TO w712b-grade(w710-match-inx, 4).
           MOVE f160-match-cal177 TO w713a-cal(w710-match-inx, 1).
           MOVE f160-match-cal22s TO w713a-cal(w710-match-inx, 2).
           MOVE f160-match-cal22l TO w713a-cal(w710-match-inx, 3).
           MOVE f160-match-calcf TO w713a-cal(w710-match-inx, 4).
           MOVE f160-match-cal32 TO w713a-cal(w710-match-inx, 5).
           MOVE f160-match-cal38 TO w713a-cal(w710-match-inx, 6).
           MOVE f160-match-cal357 TO w713a-cal(w710-match-inx, 7).
           MOVE f160-match-cal9mm TO w713a-cal(w710-match-inx, 8).
           MOVE f160-match-cal44 TO w713a-cal(w710-match-inx, 9).
           MOVE f160-match-cal45 TO w713a-cal(w710-match-inx, 10).
           MOVE f170-match-stages TO w711a-stages(w710-match-inx).
           MOVE f171-mstage1-max TO w714a-stage-max(w710-match-inx,
1).
           MOVE f172-mstage2-max TO w714a-stage-max(w710-match-inx,
2).
           MOVE f173-mstage3-max TO w714a-stage-max(w710-match-inx,
3).
           MOVE f174-mstage4-max TO w714a-stage-max(w710-match-inx,
4).
           MOVE "PRA" TO w713b-cal-type(w710-match-inx, 11).
           MOVE "n" TO w110-end-of-file.
       141110-LOAD-CALIBRA-ARRAY.
      *==========================
           READ f500-calibras-file
               AT END
                   MOVE "y" TO w110-end-of-file
               NOT AT END
                   PERFORM 141111-LOAD-CALIBRA
                       VARYING w710-match-inx FROM 1 BY 1
                           UNTIL w710-match-inx>15
                   MOVE "PRA" TO
                            w713b-cal-type(w710-match-inx,
w713-cal-inx)
           END-READ.
       141111-LOAD-CALIBRA.
      *====================
           MOVE f510-calibra TO
                           w713b-cal-type(w710-match-inx,
w713-cal-inx).
       1500000-READ-INITIAL-FILE.
      *==========================
           READ f700-initial-file
               AT END
                   MOVE "y" TO w110-end-of-file
           END-READ.
       2000000-PROCESS-MODULE.
      *=======================
           MOVE f741-day TO f313c-day.
           MOVE f742-month TO f313b-month.
           MOVE f743-year TO f313a-year.
           MOVE f710-first TO f311a-sname-first
                              f408a-fname.
           MOVE f710-last TO f311b-sname-last
                             f408a-lname.
           MOVE f720-imatch TO f312-match-shot
                               match-selection
                               f409-match.
           MOVE f730-cal TO f340-calibra.
           MOVE f750-stage1 TO f350-stage1.
           MOVE f760-stage2 TO f360-stage2.
           MOVE f770-stage3 TO f370-stage3.
           MOVE f780-stage4 TO f380-stage4.
           MOVE f790-score TO f390-offgun.
           MOVE "n" TO w110-end-of-file.
           PERFORM 2530000-READ-PBHCAP-FILE
           MOVE f460-hcap TO f395-hcap-attime.
           MOVE f465-bhcap TO f397-bhcap-attime.
           PERFORM 2540000-WRITE-SCORE.
           MOVE "n" TO w110-end-of-file.
           MOVE ZEROS TO f390-offgun.
           PERFORM 1500000-READ-INITIAL-FILE.
       2530000-READ-PBHCAP-FILE.
      *=========================
           READ f400-pbhcap-file
               INVALID KEY
                   IF(w120-file-status EQUALS 23)
                   THEN
                       PERFORM 2531000-CREATE-PBHCAP
                   ELSE
                       MOVE "error reading pbhcap  "
                                                  TO
w980-display-action
                       PERFORM 2888888-FILE-STATUS
                       PERFORM 2900000-ERROR
                   END-IF
           END-READ.
       2531000-CREATE-PBHCAP.
      *======================
           MOVE w711d-out-of(match-selection) TO f460-hcap f465-bhcap.
           MOVE "n" TO w110-end-of-file.
           PERFORM 2533000-VALID-CALIBRA
               VARYING cal-selection FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL cal-selection>10 OR w111-eof.
           IF(w111-eof)
           THEN
               MOVE ZEROS TO f450-pbest
                             f440-score3
                             f430-score2
                             f420-score1
               MOVE "D" TO f470-grade
               PERFORM 2531100-WRITE-PBHCAP
           ELSE
               MOVE "PRA" TO f340-calibra
           END-IF.
           MOVE "n" TO w110-end-of-file.
       2531100-WRITE-PBHCAP.
      *=====================
           WRITE f400-pbhcap-rec
               INVALID KEY
                   MOVE "error writing pbhcap  " TO
w980-display-action
                   PERFORM 2888888-FILE-STATUS
                   PERFORM 2900000-ERROR
           END-WRITE.
       2533000-VALID-CALIBRA.
      *======================
           IF((w713a-cal(match-selection, cal-selection)>ZEROS) AND
               (w713b-cal-type(match-selection, cal-selection) EQUALS
                            f340-calibra))
           THEN
               MOVE "y" TO w110-end-of-file
           END-IF.
       2540000-WRITE-SCORE.
      *====================
           WRITE f300-scores-rec
               INVALID KEY
                   MOVE "error writing score  " TO w980-display-action
                   PERFORM 2888888-FILE-STATUS
                   PERFORM 2900000-ERROR
               NOT INVALID KEY
                   MOVE "y" TO w110-end-of-file
                   PERFORM 2531000-PBHCAP
           END-WRITE.
       2531000-PBHCAP.
      *===============
           PERFORM 2530000-READ-PBHCAP-FILE.
           MOVE "n" TO w110-end-of-file.
           PERFORM 2533000-VALID-CALIBRA
               VARYING cal-selection FROM 1 BY 1
                   UNTIL cal-selection>10 OR w111-eof.
           IF(NOT w111-eof)
           THEN
               MOVE "PRA" TO f340-calibra
           END-IF.
           IF(f340-calibra NOT EQUAL "PRA")
           THEN
               MOVE f470-grade TO f396-grade-attime
               IF(f390-offgun>f450-pbest)
               THEN
                   MOVE f390-offgun TO f450-pbest

                   PERFORM 2531200-SET-GRADE
               END-IF
               PERFORM 2531300-CAL-HCAP
               PERFORM 2531400-REWRITE-PBHCAP-FILE
           END-IF.
       2531200-SET-GRADE.
      *==================
           IF(f390-offgun<w712b-grade(match-selection, 1))
           THEN
               MOVE "A" TO f470-grade
               IF(f390-offgun<w712b-grade(match-selection, 2))
               THEN
                   MOVE "B" TO f470-grade
                   IF(f390-offgun<w712b-grade(match-selection, 3))
                   THEN
                       MOVE "C" TO f470-grade
                       IF
                         (f390-offgun<w712b-grade(match-selection,4))
                       THEN
                           MOVE "D" TO f470-grade
                       END-IF
                   END-IF
               END-IF
           ELSE
               MOVE "M" TO f470-grade
           END-IF.
       2531300-CAL-HCAP.
      *=================
           MOVE f430-score2 TO f420-score1.
           MOVE f440-score3 TO f430-score2.
           IF(f430-score2>ZERO AND f420-score1>ZERO)
           THEN
               PERFORM 2531310-HANDICAP
               PERFORM 2531320-BHANDICAP
           ELSE
               MOVE w711d-out-of(match-selection) TO w320-temp-hcap
                                                     f465-bhcap
           END-IF.
           MOVE f465-bhcap TO f397-bhcap-attime.
           MOVE f390-offgun TO f440-score3.
           MOVE f460-hcap TO f395-hcap-attime.
           IF(w320-temp-hcap<f460-hcap)
           THEN
               MOVE w320-temp-hcap TO f460-hcap
           END-IF.
       2531310-HANDICAP.
      *=================
           ADD f390-offgun
               f420-score1
               f430-score2 GIVING w320-temp-hcap.
           DIVIDE w320-temp-hcap BY 3 GIVING w320-temp-hcap.
           SUBTRACT w320-temp-hcap
               FROM w711d-out-of(match-selection)
           GIVING w320-temp-hcap.
       2531320-BHANDICAP.
      *==================
      * calulate breake handicap...
           IF(f465-bhcap<w711d-out-of(match-selection))
           THEN
               MOVE f465-bhcap TO w325-temp-bhcap
           ELSE
               MOVE w320-temp-hcap TO w325-temp-bhcap
           END-IF.

               MOVE ZEROS TO w380-aggregate
               ADD f390-offgun TO w325-temp-bhcap GIVING
w380-aggregate
               IF(w380-aggregate>w711d-out-of(match-selection))
               THEN
                   SUBTRACT w711d-out-of(match-selection)
                       FROM w380-aggregate
                   SUBTRACT w380-aggregate FROM w325-temp-bhcap
                       GIVING f465-bhcap
               END-IF.
       2531400-REWRITE-PBHCAP-FILE.
      *============================
           REWRITE f400-pbhcap-rec
               INVALID KEY
                   MOVE "error rewriting pbhcap  "
                                                  TO
w980-display-action
                   PERFORM 2888888-FILE-STATUS
                   PERFORM 2900000-ERROR
           END-REWRITE.
       2900000-ERROR.
      *==============
           MOVE FUNCTION LENGTH(a-to-continue) TO w311-lenA.
           MOVE FUNCTION LENGTH(w980-display-action) TO w312-lenB.
           COMPUTE w313-center=(w312-lenB - w311-lenA)/2.
           MOVE a-to-continue TO w980-display-action(w313-center:).
           PERFORM 2999999-DISPLAY-MESG.
       2888888-FILE-STATUS.
      *====================
           EVALUATE w120-file-status
               WHEN 22
                   STRING w980-display-action
                       DELIMITED BY "  "
                          " duplicate key"
                       DELIMITED BY SIZE
                           INTO w990-display-message
                   END-STRING
               WHEN OTHER
                   STRING w980-display-action
                       DELIMITED BY "  "
                          " "
                       DELIMITED BY SIZE
                          w120-file-status
                       DELIMITED BY SIZE
                           INTO w990-display-message
                   END-STRING
           END-EVALUATE.
           MOVE SPACES TO w980-display-action.
       2999999-DISPLAY-MESG.
      *=====================
           DISPLAY ss900-msg-screen.
           ACCEPT ss900-msg-screen.
           MOVE SPACES TO w990-display-message
                          w980-display-action.
       3000000-END-MODULE.
      *===================
           MOVE "n" TO w110-end-of-file.
           MOVE 1 TO match-selection.
           PERFORM UNTIL w111-eof
               DISPLAY ss100-Main-screen
               MOVE "n" TO w160-cancel
               PERFORM 3100000-ENTER-MATCH UNTIL w111-eof
               IF(NOT w161-cancel)
               THEN
                   MOVE "n" TO w110-end-of-file
                   PERFORM 3600000-GRADINGS
               END-IF
               MOVE a-anymore TO w980-display-action
               MOVE SPACES TO w990-Display-message
               PERFORM 2999999-DISPLAY-MESG
               MOVE w140-press-a-key TO w110-end-of-file
               IF(NOT w111-eof)
               THEN
                   MOVE "y" TO w110-end-of-file
               ELSE
                   MOVE "n" TO w110-end-of-file
               END-IF
           END-PERFORM.
           CLOSE   f300-scores-file
                   f400-pbhcap-file
                   f700-initial-file
                   f900-report-file.
       3100000-ENTER-MATCH.
      *====================
           DISPLAY ss210-enter-match.
           MOVE i1 TO w990-display-message.
           MOVE i2 TO w980-display-action.
           DISPLAY ss900-msg-screen.
           MOVE SPACES TO w990-display-message
                          w980-display-action.
           ACCEPT ss210-enter-match.
           EVALUATE TRUE
               WHEN sup
                   PERFORM 2110000-MOVE-MATCH-UP
               WHEN sdown
                   PERFORM 2120000-MOVE-MATCH-DOWN
               WHEN scancel
                   MOVE "y" TO w110-end-of-file
                   MOVE "y" TO w160-cancel
               WHEN sselect
                   MOVE "y" TO w110-end-of-file
                   MOVE "n" TO w160-cancel
           END-EVALUATE.
           MOVE SPACES TO selector.
       2110000-MOVE-MATCH-UP.
      *======================
           IF(match-selection>=15)
           THEN
               MOVE 1 TO match-selection
           ELSE
               ADD 1 TO match-selection
           END-IF.
       2120000-MOVE-MATCH-DOWN.
      *========================
           IF(match-selection<=1)
           THEN
               MOVE 15 TO match-selection
           ELSE
               SUBTRACT 1 FROM match-selection
           END-IF.
       3600000-GRADINGS.
      *=================
           PERFORM 2610000-INITIAL-REPORT
           IF(NOT w111-eof)
           THEN
               PERFORM 2620000-REPORT-HEADINGS
               PERFORM 2630000-REPORT-CONTROL-BREAK
               MOVE "n" TO w110-end-of-file
               PERFORM 2640000-REPORT-DETAILS UNTIL w111-eof
           END-IF.
       2610000-INITIAL-REPORT.
      *=======================
           MOVE ZEROS TO w1120-page.
           MOVE match-selection TO f409-match.
           PERFORM 2612000-MEMBER-KEY-INITIAL-SET.
       2612000-MEMBER-KEY-INITIAL-SET.
      *===============================
           MOVE SPACES TO f408-mname.
           START f400-pbhcap-file
               KEY >= f410-indexed-key
               INVALID KEY
                   MOVE
                   "err starting pbhcap for report"
                                                  TO
w980-display-action
                   PERFORM 2888888-FILE-STATUS
                   PERFORM 2900000-ERROR
                   MOVE "n" TO w110-end-of-file.
           PERFORM 2612100-NEXT-PBHCAP.
       2612100-NEXT-PBHCAP.
      *====================
           READ f400-pbhcap-file NEXT RECORD
               AT END
                   MOVE "y" TO w110-end-of-file
           END-READ.
       2620000-REPORT-HEADINGS.
      *========================
           ADD 1 TO w1120-page.
           MOVE w220-Date TO w1110-date.
           WRITE f900-report-rec FROM w1100-heading1
               AFTER ADVANCING PAGE.
           WRITE f900-report-rec FROM w1200-heading2
               AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
           MOVE 6 TO w350-line-count.
           MOVE f409-match TO w360-old-match.
           MOVE w711-match(w360-old-match) TO w1410-match-name.
       2630000-REPORT-CONTROL-BREAK.
      *=============================
           WRITE f900-report-rec FROM w1400-control1
               AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
           WRITE f900-report-rec FROM w1510-detailheading1
               AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
           WRITE f900-report-rec FROM w1520-detailheading2
               AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINES.
           MOVE SPACES TO w1410-match-name.
           ADD 5 TO w350-line-count.
       2640000-REPORT-DETAILS.
      *=======================
           IF(f409-match NOT EQUAL TO w360-old-match)
           THEN
               MOVE "y" TO w110-end-of-file
           ELSE
               PERFORM 2641000-SET-UP-REPORT
               PERFORM 2642000-PRINT-REPORT
               PERFORM 2612100-NEXT-PBHCAP
           END-IF.
       2641000-SET-UP-REPORT.
      *======================
           MOVE f408a-fname TO w1610-fname.
           MOVE f408a-lname TO w1620-lname.
      *    MOVE f420-score1 TO w1630-score1.
           MOVE f465-bhcap  TO w1640-score2.
      *    MOVE f440-score3 TO w1650-score3.
           MOVE f450-pbest TO w1670-pbest.
           MOVE f460-hcap TO w1660-hcap.
           MOVE f470-grade TO w1680-grade.
       2642000-PRINT-REPORT.
      *=====================
           IF(w350-line-count>70)
           THEN
               PERFORM 2620000-REPORT-HEADINGS
               PERFORM 2630000-REPORT-CONTROL-BREAK
           END-IF.
           WRITE f900-report-rec FROM w1600-detail-line
               AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINES.
           ADD 1 TO w350-line-count.
      **********************************************************
```

<a id="t-0219947080"></a>
## Thread: FAXing — 2 msgs

### FAXing

- **From:** "Charlie" <cgoodman@imi.mb.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-06-10T14:32:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KL2N8.122136$Ka.8606631@news2.calgary.shaw.ca>`

```
perhaps a bit off topic, but we will be using NetExpress to call SDK....



We are looking for a FAX SDK or command line utility that will allow us to
send FAXes and:
- select from one of many cover pages replacing key words
- take reports with imbedded PCL as input
- allow my application software to check progress of FAX events
- be able to work with a variety of modems
- execute under Win2000
We have full control over our application code.

Any ideas and reccomendations???


Charlie Goodman
IT Manager
Integrated Messaging Inc.
```

#### ↳ Re: FAXing

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-12T02:55:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TKyN8.5635$kw5.185765200@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<KL2N8.122136$Ka.8606631@news2.calgary.shaw.ca>`

```
Charlie,

We used to have an application written in Microfocus COBOL 3.4.23 that
interfaced with Winfax Pro 10.  The PC (Windows 95) the application was
running on had the "default" printer set to "Winfax" and we used DDE
commands to send report files out automatically, via the Print command.  It
didn't have any specific PCL commands within the reports, but we let Winfax
Pro do all the work and keep track of the events (i.e. pass, fail, retries,
etc.) and Winfax handled the modem interface and cover pages (we didn't use
one, but we could have) as well.  I'm just providing this information as a
place to start looking, since Symantec probably has a W2K version of Winfax
Pro and I would hope that NetExpress still has DDE/OLE type interfaces with
software such as Winfax Pro.  We've since moved to Fujitsu 6.1 and no longer
use the Mircofocus program.  But no need to create a bunch of new code if
existing DDE (yeah, I know it's old fashioned, but it worked) conversations
might do the job for you.

Hope this helps,

Denny


"Charlie" <cgoodman@imi.mb.ca> wrote in message
news:KL2N8.122136$Ka.8606631@news2.calgary.shaw.ca...
> perhaps a bit off topic, but we will be using NetExpress to call SDK....
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Running RM/Cobol on Linux — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Running RM/Cobol on Linux

- **From:** Sylvain Boudreault <sboudreault@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-06-10T19:36:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns92299FC81683Dsboudreaultsympatico@206.172.150.14>`

```
I have RM/Cobol running on a SCO 5.0.4 box. I want migrate my system on a 
Linux RedHat 7.2 paltform because i'm not comfortable with SCO and there 
are some bugs on the system.

I finaly got the RM/Cobol for SCO running on Linux with the abi kernel 
modules (ver. 2.4.7) provided with RedHat. I can compile programs and the 
runtime is running, but i get a procedure error 204 when I try to running 
my program. The statement in error is a CALL command. The subprogram called 
is in the same directory, and it's a valid object file. (.obj).

My linux kernel is the 2.4.7.


Thank you for help.
```

- **Date:** 2002-06-11T16:07:43+00:00

```
> I have RM/Cobol running on a SCO 5.0.4 box. I want migrate my system on a
> Linux RedHat 7.2 paltform because i'm not comfortable with SCO and there
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> my program. The statement in error is a CALL command. The subprogram
called
> is in the same directory, and it's a valid object file. (.obj).

What version of RM/COBOL?

Are you attempting to CALL a non-COBOL program (indicated by the .obj
extension)?  If so, it would probably be best to take the advice offered by
Frederico, and purchase a runtime for Linux.  You probably will need to
rework the non-COBOL program; the abi compatibility is probably not
complete.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

```
The program I CALL is a COBOL program. I have recompile the programs (the 
main and the subprogram) with no error.

I'm a consultant and before I recommand my client to buy something, I 
want to be sure that everything will work fine. Do it exist any trial 
version of the runtime for Linux that I can use to test the application 
on the new plateform?

Thank You for help!
Sylvain Boudreault
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Running RM/Cobol on Linux

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-12T17:04:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<faLN8.30529$ub1.2708675737@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns92299FC81683Dsboudreaultsympatico@206.172.150.14> <jfpN8.24946$ad.2274485279@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com> <Xns922A7F780C00Esboudreaultsympatico@206.172.150.14>`

```

"Sylvain Boudreault" <sboudreault@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:Xns922A7F780C00Esboudreaultsympatico@206.172.150.14...

> The program I CALL is a COBOL program. I have recompile the programs (the
> main and the subprogram) with no error.

If the CALLed program is a COBOL program, its extension would normally be
.cob, not .obj.  That may be the problem.  You may consult the RM/COBOL
Users Guide for quite extensive documentation on the means by which CALL
locates the CALLed program.

> I'm a consultant and before I recommand my client to buy something, I
> want to be sure that everything will work fine. Do it exist any trial
> version of the runtime for Linux that I can use to test the application
> on the new plateform?

While I am sympathetic with your concern, we don't provide trial versions.
I do think that our sales staff probably can address this concern.

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Running RM/Cobol on Linux

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-12T18:58:27+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<q72fguo2vidk80qc2dqslmlvlctli9midf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns92299FC81683Dsboudreaultsympatico@206.172.150.14> <jfpN8.24946$ad.2274485279@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com> <Xns922A7F780C00Esboudreaultsympatico@206.172.150.14> <faLN8.30529$ub1.2708675737@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com>`

```
On Wed, 12 Jun 2002 17:04:11 GMT, "Tom Morrison"
<t.morrison@liant.com> wrote:

>
>"Sylvain Boudreault" <sboudreault@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>> on the new plateform?
If you use the runtime for Sco under Linux you are bound to find
problems.
If you use the correct runtime, the only problems that I am aware will
be related with printers (and they can be solved by changing some 
configuration files), or with terminfos/termcaps (and once again you 
can change these).

As for recompiling the programs, this is NOT required, as you can move
the objects from one system to the other directly.

Error 204 means that the program was not found, either on the loaded
table of programs, or as a valid file+"valid cobol extension".

Is it possible that in your SCO machine you have a configuration
file using`the keyword
EXTENSION-NAMES OBJECT=obj
If this is the case you need to make sure you have the same on 
the linux runtime configuration.
other option is that you do indeed have this on the configuration,
but as you have recompiled the programs they are now being create
as ".cob".


Regards

FF
```

- **References:** `<oJeF8.3221$eD2.82794@news2.east.cox.net> <7f64e2ff.0205201306.8b71500@posting.google.com> <7f64e2ff.0205212320.149f00ee@posting.google.com> <6GKG8.2860$%s1.364@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <GoOG8.218728$M7.22139758@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <dZOG8.2401$L31.1418@nwrddc03.gnilink.net> <7f64e2ff.0205231745.10131186@posting.google.com> <A4qH8.10$9V.4@nwrddc03.gnilink.net> <ClLI8.32305$R_4.20689@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`

```
> > ... I was able to get the size of the .exe
> > down to 140K for a hello, world program by adding a -s switch to the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> .idata  size x'600'

> > Test1.exe size 566,741 (Decimal) bytes contains six sections
> > .txt  size x'20400'
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

It turns out that the .exe file can be shrunk after using the IDE. The
included version of Cygwin (or other distributions) contains a program
called strip.exe. Invoking this program with a -s switch and the file
name has the same effect as what I described above.
```

```
I downloaded the demo version of this to test. It shows promise but some
basics didn't work. I used a program that was written for Micro-focus.
All "organization is line sequential" statements were flagged.
All comp-x items were flagged.
That's not too bad I guess, you can work around those...  BUT...
It also flagged:
accept data-name from command-line.   (Command-line not defined)
move 12 to return-code.  (return-code not defined)

Command-line?  return-code?  command-line is pretty basic in a PC
environment. Return-code is basic in any environment.

Without that... the program won't work as intended.



-----------------------------------------------------------------------
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Kobol" development system

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-11T22:22:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ae6epm$d6l$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<RXcN8.6$Gi6.2061@nsw.nnrp.telstra.net> <ae5ms9$9n1$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com> <7f64e2ff.0206111842.1542616f@posting.google.com>`

```
> It is really ported from a Unix (Linux) environment to Windows, so in
> this case I guess the PC or DOS specific stuff loses out.
>
> Binary (Comp) items are little endian (Native Intel).

That's no answer.
This product will not have much success when such basic requirements
as passing an execution parm on the command-line and setting a return-code
do not work. By the way, doesn't Unix have command-lines and return-codes?
I seem to remember from my limited exposure that it does.
So what if Binary (Comp) items are Native Intel? That's what Linux runs on
doesn't it? If PC, DOS, Windows stuff loses out, then so will this product in
that environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Kobol" development system

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** pottmi@yahoo.com (Michael Potter)
- **Date:** 2002-06-12T08:28:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2e6e876e.0206120728.6471ecf1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<RXcN8.6$Gi6.2061@nsw.nnrp.telstra.net> <ae5ms9$9n1$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com> <7f64e2ff.0206111842.1542616f@posting.google.com> <ae6epm$d6l$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>`

```
"Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net> wrote in message news:<ae6epm$d6l$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>...

> > It is really ported from a Unix (Linux) environment to Windows, so in

> > this case I guess the PC or DOS specific stuff loses out.

> >

> > Binary (Comp) items are little endian (Native Intel).

> 

> That's no answer.

> This product will not have much success when such basic requirements

> as passing an execution parm on the command-line and setting a return-code

> do not work. By the way, doesn't Unix have command-lines and return-codes?

> I seem to remember from my limited exposure that it does.

> So what if Binary (Comp) items are Native Intel? That's what Linux runs on

> doesn't it? 

if Kobol does not support big endian byte order for comp then
it will be difficult to share data between platforms.

both microfocus and ibm allow comp to represent big endian
while allowing comp-5 (which is in the upcoming standard) to
be machine independent.  this allows you to specify comp
is data that is written to a file (and thereby sharable)
and comp-5 when the data will never be shared and you can
take advantage of the speed increase.


> If PC, DOS, Windows stuff loses out, then so will this product in

> that environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Kobol" development system

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-12T16:59:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ae8gfb$1n4$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<RXcN8.6$Gi6.2061@nsw.nnrp.telstra.net> <ae5ms9$9n1$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com> <7f64e2ff.0206111842.1542616f@posting.google.com> <ae6epm$d6l$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com> <2e6e876e.0206120728.6471ecf1@posting.google.com> <ae874u$1sq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
There may (or may not) be confusion here - in that the draft Standard *does*
provide "true binary" USAGEs (e.g. Binary-Char, Binary-Long, etc) which do
MUCH (but not exactly the same) thing as existing COMP-5 implementations.

There may ALSO be confusion with the X/Open Standard which did (as I recall)
include COMP-5.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Kobol" development system

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2002-06-12T09:33:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0206120833.538acf41@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<RXcN8.6$Gi6.2061@nsw.nnrp.telstra.net> <ae5ms9$9n1$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com> <7f64e2ff.0206111842.1542616f@posting.google.com> <3D0758D6.3B3189E7@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3D0758D6.3B3189E7@Azonic.co.nz>...
> E P Chandler wrote:
> > >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> in Unix well before PC-DOS or PC.

Yes, Unix (C) does have ARGC, ARGV and return, but I would expect them
to be available to the programmer in Unix/Linux terms, not those of
MS-DOS. Also, I was referring to well known Micro-Focus extensions
which allow access to hardware though BIOS calls, something I would
not expect a port from Linux to support.

Since line sequential files are not supported by the compiler, and one
of the early bugs I found involved text-like translation of binary
values which contained 0x0a (line feed), I suspect that little thought
was given to things some of us expect or take for granted in a PC
environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Kobol" development system

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2002-06-12T09:47:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0206120847.628af540@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<RXcN8.6$Gi6.2061@nsw.nnrp.telstra.net> <ae5ms9$9n1$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com> <QtDN8.3804$z56.723119@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message news:<QtDN8.3804$z56.723119@news20.bellglobal.com>...
> "Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com> wrote in message
> news:ae5ms9$9n1$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald

Given the almost total lack of documentation provided with the demo
release, "RTFM" is most definitely *not* warranted here. The one HTML
file supplied with version 1.0.2 discusses some details of using the
development environment, but its only language reference is a table
extracted from source code somewhere which contains keywords and some
related flags.

The best place for documentation, at least about the compiler back
end, is that supplied with Linux or Cygwin or standard documentation
for GNU.

I bet that if the "Kobol" language does not provide it, it would be
possible to patch this ability in by writing external modules in C++
and linking them in, but these are facilities not provided by the
development environment (IDE).
```

```

"David Robinson" <david@nighttime.demon.invalid> wrote in message
news:mjl5ea.786.ln@pingu.tunstall.ave...
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com> wrote in message
> news:oRlN8.195752$Gs.18536561@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Linux is a cult. The U.S., France, China, and, yes, Germany, deal
harshly
> > with cults.
>
> Are you extracting the urine with that comment? By what warped logic do
you
> come to the conclusion that Linux is a cult? How can a kernel be a cult?

A Google search yields over 36,000 entries with the Linux - Cult combo. I
did, regretably use a shorthand. You are correct, Linux itself is not a
cult, no more than Halley-Bopp comet was a cult. It is the followers that
are collectively (perhaps as a shorthand) known as a cult. For example,

"...Torvalds became the dictatorial leader of the Linux cult with all
decisions for the greater community good going through him first, then doled
out at his convenience."
(http://www.worldtribune.com/wta/Archive-2001/mc10_05.html)

"Luther and his followers were excommunicated for their beliefs. I don't
face anything nearly as drastic as that--but I know there are many in the
Linux cult ready to proclaim me a heretic, or worse."
(http://www.zdnet.com/anchordesk/stories/story/0,10738,2773365,00.html)

>
> BTW, do you have permission to use invalid.com as your from address? It's
a
> registered domain. If you want to spamblock, substitute the TLD part of
your
> email address with .invalid as I have done. I can't remember where I read
it
> (probably in a RFC) but I believe that mail transports drop any e-mail
> heading for .invalid domains.

I do not have permission to use "invalid.com" as my "from" address, but
thanks for asking. I did send a brief note to webmaster@invalid.com and told
him I intended to do so unless he had some objection. I haven't heard back,
so I assume all is OK. I don't primarily use "invalid.com" as a spamblocker,
I use it for esthetic reasons: The juxtaposition of the "i" character and
the almost perfect alignment of the "v," coupled with the perfect ratio of
consonants and vowels gives me a sense of serenity and well-being. I don't
expect many to appreciate my finely-honed sense of symmetry, but I'm
comforted by it.


>
> --
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Free-Cobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-06-13T10:53:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020613065311.16025.00001727@mb-mo.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3D07367A.C17BEF45@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
>From: Richard Plinston riplin@Azonic.co.nz 
>Date: 2002-06-12 07:54 Eastern Daylight Time

>There are even 3,500 entries found on 'Cobol cult'.  Will you now claim
>that 'Cobol is a cult' ? or even 'Followers of Cobol are a cult' ?

Searching for COBOL *and* cult gets a lot of hits for glossaries, etc.
A more specific search produces: 

"Jim Jones cult" 393

"Mac cult"       142
     e.g.
  http://www.applelinks.com/macspirit/cult.shtml

"Linux cult"      75 
     e.g.
  http://www.segfault.org/stories/37190641-03a4b480.html

"Hale-Bopp cult"  64

"Microsoft cult"  33
     --many by Linux partisans

"Windows cult"    21
     e.g.
  http://www.computerpranks.com/download/images/computer/gates_ar.jpg

"COBOL cult"       0

So, Linux is more of a cult than Hale-Boppp, but less than Jim Jones.
Only COBOL (touching forehead, chest, and shoulders) is not a cult. 



Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Free-Cobol for Linux

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2002-06-12T16:03:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d076ee0.13381551@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<ae3t6l$1qk$03$1@news.t-online.com> <oRlN8.195752$Gs.18536561@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <mjl5ea.786.ln@pingu.tunstall.ave> <6BuN8.69687$Qg.5725023@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com> wrote:

>
>"David Robinson" <david@nighttime.demon.invalid> wrote in message
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>A Google search yields over 36,000 entries with the Linux - Cult combo. I

Jerry:

A Google search yields over 127,000 entries with the Windows - Cult
combo.....so what have we accomplished by doing this?

We work with a lot of businesses from 5 employees to several hundred
employees each and every week who use Linux as a server platform and
Windows as a desktop platform.  In their opinion, this is an ideal
combination.

It provides them with great reliability and stability at the server
level and still offers the end users exactly what they
want.....Windows on the desktop.

To suggest that Linux users are part of a cult is a bit of a
stretch......kind of like the rumors that there are conspiracies at
Microsoft to control the entire computing and home entertainment
industry.

....actually the Microsoft rumor is even a bit more on the plausible
side.

Anyway....Jerry, are you planning to attend COBOL Expo?  If so, stop
at the Flexus booth to get some free "goodies" and then we'll have a
beer and debate this issue exactly the way it should be
debated.......with an arm wrasslin' contest!!!

;-)





Bob Wolfe

Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Free-Cobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-13T13:55:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3v1O8.223675$Gs.20074946@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<ae3t6l$1qk$03$1@news.t-online.com> <oRlN8.195752$Gs.18536561@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <mjl5ea.786.ln@pingu.tunstall.ave> <6BuN8.69687$Qg.5725023@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3d076ee0.13381551@news.epix.net>`

```

"Bob Wolfe" <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
>
> Jerry:
>
> A Google search yields over 127,000 entries with the Windows - Cult
> combo.....so what have we accomplished by doing this?

Forget Windows. Whether Windows is a cult is irrelevant to whether Linux is
considered a cult. It may be that EVERYTHING is a cult (except, perhaps,
what I do with my radishes).

>
> We work with a lot of businesses from 5 employees to several hundred
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> want.....Windows on the desktop.

Cool! I'm glad for them.

>
> To suggest that Linux users are part of a cult is a bit of a
> stretch......kind of like the rumors that there are conspiracies at
> Microsoft to control the entire computing and home entertainment
> industry.

I'm not suggesting Linux is a cult: I am asserting that Linux users, in the
minds of many, choose an operating system on the basis of emotion rather
than common sense. This perception cannot but have a negative effect on the
potential customers and prospective employees of the Linux shop. We may
complain about COBOL bigotry, but the stereotype exists.

Further, the suggestion that Linux is a cult is not original with me.
Torvalds himself described the Linux community thusly.


>
> ....actually the Microsoft rumor is even a bit more on the plausible
> side.

I agree. On the other hand, few doubt that if Micros~1 acquired the rights
to Linux and, simultaneously, placed Windows in the public domain, most of
the Linux users would switch operating systems forthwith.

I don't have any particular gripe with Linux, per se - my observation has
solely to do with the irrational, manical, visceral hatred of Windows
attributed to the general Linux user. Picking an operating system based on
contempt of another company tells me something about that person's mental
processes. Just like someone whose hobby is throwing cats in the trash
compactor tells me volumes.

>
> Anyway....Jerry, are you planning to attend COBOL Expo?  If so, stop
> at the Flexus booth to get some free "goodies" and then we'll have a
> beer and debate this issue exactly the way it should be
> debated.......with an arm wrasslin' contest!!!

Sorry, no. I'm way behind on radishes and cats.
```

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com> wrote:

>
>"Bob Wolfe" <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>what I do with my radishes).

Why forget Windows?  If one group of OS users is being referred to as
being "cultish" then it is completely logical to look to the users of
other OS platforms in order to compare and contrast.

>> We work with a lot of businesses from 5 employees to several hundred
>> employees each and every week who use Linux as a server platform and
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>complain about COBOL bigotry, but the stereotype exists.

And I am asserting that Windows users, in the minds of many, choose an
operating system on the basis of emotion rather than common sense.

>Further, the suggestion that Linux is a cult is not original with me.
>Torvalds himself described the Linux community thusly.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>the Linux users would switch operating systems forthwith.

I guess I am one of those who doubt this.  Every Linux (or UNIX) user
I know always speaks of reliability, stability and horsepower as their
primary reasons for choosing Linux......not that they choose Linux
because they hate Gates.

Every Linux/UNIX user I know makes the decision for business reasons,
because as a server OS it generally is a better platform.  VERY few (I
can count 'em on one hand, prefer it as a desktop platform.....99.9%
of the Linux/UNIX users I know choose Windows as their desktop
environment.

The world is not black and white and "one size fits all" really is a
myth.  It is good business to use the right tool for the specific task
at hand.  The Linux users I know do exactly that.

A carpenter could build a house with only a hammer.......but it
wouldn't be easy.  The carpenter who uses the hammer for driving nails
and the saw for cutting wood is far more successful.

You may find this difficult to believe, but the Linux/UNIX users I
deal with LOVE Windows on the desktop.....but NOT as a server
platform.  From where I stand, to say that one OS is the best OS for
all purposes leans more toward cult behavior than to say that one OS
is great for this purpose and a different OS is great for a different
purpose.

>I don't have any particular gripe with Linux, per se - my observation has
>solely to do with the irrational, manical, visceral hatred of Windows
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>compactor tells me volumes.

You are stereotyping.  I speak with Linux users every day and the
people I speak with don't hate Windows or Gates or Microsoft.  In fact
they all use it on their desktop installations.

>> Anyway....Jerry, are you planning to attend COBOL Expo?  If so, stop
>> at the Flexus booth to get some free "goodies" and then we'll have a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sorry, no. I'm way behind on radishes and cats.

New recipe?

;-)



Bob Wolfe
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Free-Cobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-14T08:47:55+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D099FAB.5EFBC40F@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ae3t6l$1qk$03$1@news.t-online.com> <oRlN8.195752$Gs.18536561@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <mjl5ea.786.ln@pingu.tunstall.ave> <6BuN8.69687$Qg.5725023@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3d076ee0.13381551@news.epix.net> <3v1O8.223675$Gs.20074946@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> 

> It may be that EVERYTHING is a cult 

The little mouse finally gets a clue.

> I'm not suggesting Linux is a cult:

No ?  And I thought that you wrote "Linux is a cult".

> I am asserting that Linux users, in the minds of many, 
                                   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
> choose an operating system on the basis of emotion rather
> than common sense. 

So it is only in the minds of _others_ that a choice of using Linux must
be on the basis of emotion rather than common sense.  Presumably these
_others_ have made their choice based on 'common sense', therefore if
someone chooses something different it is not 'common sense'.

If I choose to buy a Nissan car it is using 'common sense', if _you_ buy
a different brand then it must be because you didn't use 'common sense'
(which would lead you to Nissan) but bought that on the basis of
emotion.

> Further, the suggestion that Linux is a cult is not original with me.
> Torvalds himself described the Linux community thusly.

No he didn't.  It was described as a 'cult' by its detractors and by
newspaper columnists trying to get their ratings up:

"""Since approximately 1997 Linus probably became the most well known
Finn on the planet.  Now he became a media darling -- a very dangerous
and rather time consuming occupation with not that much time to devote
to kernel :-). And media is pretty cruel as it serves as a powerful
amplifier of "cult f personality" disease. He now has dozens of pages
and hundreds of papers devoted to him, including this chapter ;-). Among
the most active builders of Linus cult I would like to mention Eric
Raymond  who lionize Linus in his famous " The Cathedral and the Bazaar"
paper (here Eric Raymond motives are pretty clear -- he wanted to
benefit from the cult;"""

The 'cult' is the creation of the media (not of Linux users), encouraged
and perpetuated by competitors.

Now he did notice that he was being reported as a 'cult figure' in the
press and introduced himself sarcastically as "I am your god".  He also
stated that the cult image should be dissipated.

He didn't say that the Linux community _was_ a cult, he said the Linux
community was _portrayed_ as a cult.

It seems that either you don't know the difference, or that you prefer
to reinforce your own limited view of 'common sense'.
```

<a id="t-3f91f095e6"></a>
## Thread: object oriented cobal — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### object oriented cobal

- **From:** j_p6@yahoo.com (John)
- **Date:** 2002-06-10T21:10:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92964e8e.0206102010.5ee94c71@posting.google.com>`

```
Just before the Y2K thing came to a conclusion I read, with the
renewed interest in COBAL, that there was an object oriented COBAL
being developed. I have not read any other updates on this project.
The last I read was that it would be listed as COBOL 2000+ standard. I
have not heard nor read anything else concerning this project. Any
info will be greatly appreciated. Thank you.

j_p6@yahoo.com
```

#### ↳ Re: object oriented cobal

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-06-11T04:36:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D057EBC.3FB1E443@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<92964e8e.0206102010.5ee94c71@posting.google.com>`

```


John wrote:

> Just before the Y2K thing came to a conclusion I read, with the
> renewed interest in COBAL, that there was an object oriented COBAL
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> j_p6@yahoo.com

1. Firstly it is COBOL (Common Business Oriented Language)

2. At the current time there are three compilers that offer OO COBOL (1)
IBM (sort of !) and on PCs, (2) Fujitsu and (3) Micro Focus

3. The draft COBOL Standard COBOL 200x, has been produced and hopefully
will be known as COBOL 2002 - due for final rubber stamp by December 2002.
This includes updates to Procedural COBOL and Object Oriented COBOL.
(Both Fujitsu and Micro Focus include collections/dictionaries (Lists) in
their current versions - and the setting of the standard for these is
currently under discussion by J4 - the prime COBOL Standards Committee).

4. You can see the draft for COBOL 2002 at :-

        http://www.incits.org/tc_home/j4.htm

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Search in Fujitsu Cobol Power Cobol

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-11T12:12:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5PlN8.205418$Oa1.19307305@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<nDjN8.967$Qf4.1981563@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```

"PorTyB" <zxpt40br@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:nDjN8.967$Qf4.1981563@newsserver.ip.pt...
> I want to do a search of string in a Fujitsu Cobol file (indexed). I think
> that the best way is use a grid control that can make a search for column.
> Somebody knows a control (OCX), that can make this?
>

I'm not sure I understand. You have an indexed file. Somewhere in this file
is a string. You want to know whether/where the string appears. Further, the
needed string is not the key of the file. Have I got that right?

By loading the file into a 'table,' you're simply changing how you access
the file's data - from being in a file to being in memory (or the Windows
spill-file). Why bother? The search won't be any faster or easier to code.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Search in Fujitsu Cobol Power Cobol

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-11T16:35:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qFpN8.15706$gs4.2471@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<nDjN8.967$Qf4.1981563@newsserver.ip.pt> <5PlN8.205418$Oa1.19307305@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse <nospam@invalid.com> wrote in message
news:5PlN8.205418$Oa1.19307305@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "PorTyB" <zxpt40br@hotmail.com> wrote in message
> news:nDjN8.967$Qf4.1981563@newsserver.ip.pt...
> > I want to do a search of string in a Fujitsu Cobol file (indexed). I
think
> > that the best way is use a grid control that can make a search for
column.
> > Somebody knows a control (OCX), that can make this?
> >
>
> I'm not sure I understand. You have an indexed file. Somewhere in this
file
> is a string. You want to know whether/where the string appears. Further,
the
> needed string is not the key of the file. Have I got that right?
>
> By loading the file into a 'table,' you're simply changing how you access
> the file's data - from being in a file to being in memory (or the Windows
> spill-file). Why bother? The search won't be any faster or easier to code.


Well, maybe not... If you load the entire file into the columns of a
Listview control,  the Windows Listview control API includes "search for
exact" and "search for like" functions which are one-line calls. The actual
"search" code would be pretty easy to code then. (Of course, you have to
write the "create control and load data to it" code...).

Why OCX is a mystery to me, too... assuming I would think this the best way
to go (which I don't), I would write this in a "real" DLL using a
non-visible control.  But.....The Windows API includes a good number of text
search/comparison functions which operate on buffers and don't require a
control.

Call me old-fashioned, but why bother with a control if all you want to do
is search text?
```

```
OK.

For example:  i have a file with names

Record1  ---> Key = 0001

Record2  ---> Key = 0002

"Key" is the primary key, and "name" is field in the record.

Thanks
Joe M.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Search in Fujitsu Cobol Power Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-11T18:48:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YBrN8.40370$fM.20124@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<nDjN8.967$Qf4.1981563@newsserver.ip.pt> <5PlN8.205418$Oa1.19307305@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <qFpN8.15706$gs4.2471@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <SGqN8.983$Xw7.2284581@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```
PorTyB <zxpt40br@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:SGqN8.983$Xw7.2284581@newsserver.ip.pt...
> OK.
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> I need to search for example "Mouse" or "Mattias"

Then your file is designed in a manner for which I would have  "minimum high
regard." (phrase courtesy Secy. Don Rumsfeld)

If you want to search by name, the name should be either a primary or
secondary key.

Unfortunately, you are not even searching on "name" as a key: you are
searching "lots of text" for "contains."  (e.g., you will not find "mouse"
with a START/READ NEXT on an alternate key). You are essentially reduced to
this:

PERFORM UNTIL String-found or EOF-file
  READ TheFile
   AT END
     SET EOF-FILE TO TRUE
   NOT AT  END
    MOVE 0 TO TALLY
    INSPECT File-Record TALLYING TALLY for SEARCH-STRING
   IF TALLY GREATER ZERO
      SET STRING-FOUND TO TRUE
   END -IF
  END-READ
END-PERFORM

Now, if the file is not going to change during the program run, you can do a
lot of cool stuff by reading the file once at the beginning of the program
run and storing the names in a table or creating an indexed file with
keys=possible search parameters.

Ain't no control on the planet gonna make this job any easier. But if you
have to load all the data to a control anyway (e.g., to facilitate a lookup
by a real key), then you have some options.

Depending on application needs, you might want to reconsider your file
design, your control usage, your search parameters,etc.  FWIW, how many
records are in this file to be searched, and does it change during the
program run? Does it even matter if it changes during the program run?

Spend some time thinking before you spend it coding.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Search in Fujitsu Cobol Power Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-11T21:54:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AkuN8.69552$Qg.5711421@bin6.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<nDjN8.967$Qf4.1981563@newsserver.ip.pt> <5PlN8.205418$Oa1.19307305@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <qFpN8.15706$gs4.2471@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <SGqN8.983$Xw7.2284581@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```

"PorTyB" <zxpt40br@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:SGqN8.983$Xw7.2284581@newsserver.ip.pt...
> OK.
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Joe M.

OK. Given the above file (or something close), if you have to look up one
name, read the file sequentially until you find the desired name.

If you have to find many names, first read the file and create a new ISAM
file like:

01 TEMP-FILE.
   02  TEMP-KEY
      05  TEMP-NAME    PIC X(40).
      05  TEMP-RECNUM  PIC 9(6).

Then go after the required names in the TEMP file using the TEMP-RECNUM to
get the key of the original file. I'm assuming here that the original file
contains much more data than a customer number and a name.

Trying to load the entire file into memory may ultimately fail when the
database becomes too large (think Reader's Digest subscription list or calls
to DELL tech support).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Search in Fujitsu Cobol Power Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-12T12:59:51+01:00

```
JerryMouse wrote:
```

```
> ISAM
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> situation.

Then please do explain how it is any improvement at all.  All you have
done is add the overhead of creating a file indexed by the whole name,
in order to find any embedded strings (as is required) the whole file
still needs to be read, and this will likely be slower due to it having
a much larger index. 

Of course you could have suggested writing out the text as a flat file
(relative or sequential) after determining that this would indeed be
faster to process by avoiding indexes.  Or even as a line sequential
file and then using grep (reknowned for it fast text matching) to create
extracts.

You could have suggested building an index file (as above) and then
rotating the name to each non-space so that it created up to 40
temporary index records per name - and uppercasing everything.  Then the
search would be easy and fast against a trade off of a huge file create.

> I did read the requirement, and the follow-up ("I need to search for example
> 'Mouse'...").

Then you failed to provide a solution that is any closer to the
requirement.
 
> My solution, while admittedly not elegant, is, at least, a contribution to
> the solution 

Only if 'making it take longer to run' is a 'contribution'.
```
