Pretty much.. But with one major drawback, heavily regulated language law.
But that's another story! Most of Cobol jobs openings in Quebec are in
goverments (federal and provincial). Others are from banks(RBC, National
bank and Desjardins). Your best chances in Quebec is to be hired by some
consulting firm (CGI, fujitsu, EDS etc..). If you are bilingual (french
english) have some experience (1+year) in Cobol your chances are good to be
hired from them. From where I live (Quebec City) if you have experience in
Cobol (specially on mainframe) those firms will kill to have you. But you
must speak french.

Take a look at the Canadian job newgroups...

tor.jobs
ott.jobs
mtl.jobs

or try the provincial job groups...

ont.jobs
qc.jobs

Some Cobol, but not much.  Quebec is basically a dead IT market in
legacy.  Their job groups have been shrinking and the use of French in
business is practically legislated.  The Maritimes will have nothing.
In the West, the odd job.  Ottawa has little Cobol outside the
government and I hear contracts are on hold.  The most activity will
be in the Toronto area - banks and large manufacturing. 

Your friend is going to have to compete with lots of people with
plenty of experience.   The embassy is correct.

In my experience, there is a lot of city-to-city variation.  COBOL had already lost a 
lot of ground in Vancouver by the late 1970s, but Calgary was still almost a 100% "COBOL 
Town".  I can remember a bunch of us IT managers having a meeting with the U of C 
computing faculty to answer their question "Why do you guys hire people from SAIT (the 
local tech) and not our grads?".  I can remember us all answering "Because they teach 
them COBOL and we can put them right to work - we have to re-train your grads first".

Jimmy Gavin (frequent contributor here) is in Calgary and can probably jump in and tell 
you the current situation there.
```

<a id="t-26a58c6174"></a>
## Thread: IBM & COBOL 2002 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### IBM & COBOL 2002

- **From:** david.silber@hpdsoftware.com (David Silber)
- **Date:** 2002-06-13T08:19:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ad85f189.0206130719.5a877bef@posting.google.com>`

```
Further to messages I have posted in the past, I am now informed by a
senior IBM person that the current thinking is to provide only a
subset of the forthcoming ANSI/ISO COBOL 2002 standard on all
platforms including i, p and zSeries, the subset being those features
that users really want. IBM personnel are now working on the NATIONAL
support feature for example.

It seems apparent that interested programmers will have to raise their
heads out of the trenches and persuade their managers/directors to
lobby IBM very seriously to (possibly) get what you want.  COBOL i
Series users (AS/400) will know only too well that the intrinsic
functions were never fully implemented; this serves as an indication
of the task ahead. Good luck.
```

- **References:** `<ad85f189.0206130719.5a877bef@posting.google.com>`

```

<a id="t-954b6743dd"></a>
## Thread: IS:: COBOL and PL/1 compiler for Unix — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: IS:: COBOL and PL/1 compiler for Unix

- **From:** "Richard S. Shuford" <shuford@list.stratagy.REM0VE-TH1S-PART.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-13T15:20:01-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.sys.stratus,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rshu_20020613_152001@stratagy.com>`
- **References:** `<3DF14987E656FE4094C1CBC293B40AFE01C1E042@MAILST1>`

```
JArbonel(at)nymex.com wrote:
|
| Does anyone know of a COBOL or PL/1 compiler for Unix?
| I am looking at a COBOL solution from Microfocus and
| need to weigh my options.


Jean:

If you can get to Chicago next weekend (21-23 June), you might
benefit from attending the COBOLexpo trade fair...

    http://www.CobolExpo.com/homepage.html
    (or call 1-800/270-9339 in the U.S. for information)

..but you can at least see some of the sponsors here:
        
    http://www.CobolExpo.com/pavilion.html
 
Obviously, you are aware of MicroFocus International Ltd., the
vendor of MicroFocus COBOL, which was spun off last year from Merant.

    http://www.microfocus.com/
    (phone: +1 301/838-5000 or 1-877/772-4450)
    
MicroFocus tells how to get registration help for COBOLexpo:

    http://www.microfocus.com/mfnewsletter/

The other notable independent vendor of which I am aware is:
Liant Corporation (the successor to Ryan-McFarland), which has
both COBOL and PL/I compilers.

    http://www.liant.com/

Of particular interest to you may be the tools Liant sells for
translating PL/I programs to COBOL.

If you have any CICS appliations, you will want to check out  
UniKix:

    http://www.unikix.com/

Most COBOL users should be aware of the existence of the COBOL FAQ
(Frequently Asked Questions) list: 

    http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

According to it, two other vendors of UNIX COBOL compilers are Acucorp:
 
    http://www.acucorp.com/

and Fujitsu:

    http://www.adtools.com/products/unix/cobolux.htm
```

## Thread: How Can I Interpret a Dump of a VSAM File — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### How Can I Interpret a Dump of a VSAM File

- **From:** dajames66@hotmail.com (dazza)
- **Date:** 2002-06-13T20:52:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8040053e.0206131952.7e0aec19@posting.google.com>`

```
I am trying to access a VSAM file that has been downloaded onto a PC.
To do this with the software that I have (ACL) I need to be able to
understand the information stored within the VSAM file that defines
the record lengths, number of records etc. I do not know what type of
VSAM file that I have. Can someone post or point me to a source that
will explain byte-by-byte how to read a dump of a VSAM file?

Thanks
Darren
```

#### ↳ Re: How Can I Interpret a Dump of a VSAM File

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-14T09:05:43+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gxp5pj0.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`
- **References:** `<8040053e.0206131952.7e0aec19@posting.google.com>`

```
On 13 Jun 2002 20:52:59 -0700, dazza wrote:

>I am trying to access a VSAM file that has been downloaded onto a PC.
>To do this with the software that I have (ACL) I need to be able to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Darren

A few questions

How did you extract the data from the VSAM file
How did you get the data in your PC
Is there any EBCDIC - ASCII conversion done
Does the file contain any packed decimal fields

Without knowing the followed process, there is no way to interpret the data.


Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

- **Date:** 2002-06-14T13:59:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8040053e.0206141259.1685b652@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8040053e.0206131952.7e0aec19@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gxp5pj0.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`

```
"Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com> wrote in message news:<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gxp5pj0.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>...

No conversion has been performed

> Does the file contain any packed decimal fields

Yes
>

I am familiar with packed and binary fields etc - it is more the
record length and changes in record type that I would appreciate
assistance in determining.

> 
> 
> Willem Clements
> Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
> http://www.brainbench.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How Can I Interpret a Dump of a VSAM File

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-14T17:50:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aedrvi$ivr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8040053e.0206131952.7e0aec19@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gxp5pj0.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <8040053e.0206141259.1685b652@posting.google.com>`

```
On the mainframe, it is "relatively" easy to get this information via the
IDCAMS utility.  Once you have "FTP'ed" a "dump" of the file to the PC, you
are OUT OF LUCK.

If, on the other hand, the file was "REPRO'ed" on the mainframe and a "flat"
copy was sent to the PC, then things should be medium easy.

Clearly if you have mixed character, packed, and binary data in records,
then there is NO way to process them on the PC without also knowing the
"record layouts".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How Can I Interpret a Dump of a VSAM File

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-15T07:45:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D0AF0A4.48A8375B@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8040053e.0206131952.7e0aec19@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gxp5pj0.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <8040053e.0206141259.1685b652@posting.google.com>`

```
dazza wrote:
> (snip)
> > How did you extract the data from the VSAM file
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> No conversion has been performed

It is possible to determine if a VSAM file has fixed-length or
variable-length records, and the maximum LRECL by running a LISTCAT
command using the IDCAMS utility.  It would also be possible to
determine the keylength for a KSDS file and the relative key position
(keys don't have to start at a zero displacement relative to the
beginning of each record).  I believe this kind of information is
stored in the cluster component of the VSAM dataset.  If you have a
KSDS VSAM file and did a binary ftp of the cluster, data, and index
components to a PC, you might be able to interpret the cluster
component to obtain LRECL, Key Length, and RKP.  

VSAM does not store any definition of fields within records other than
the key.

If I had to transfer a VSAM file to a PC, I would print the LISTCAT on
the mainframe first, then REPRO the data component to a flat file, and
only transfer the data records.
  
> (rest snipped)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How Can I Interpret a Dump of a VSAM File

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-17T09:25:19+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gxuqm70.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`

On 14 Jun 2002 13:59:29 -0700, dazza wrote:

>"Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com> wrote in message news:<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gxp5pj0.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>...
>> On 13 Jun 2002 20:52:59 -0700, dazza wrote:
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Darren,

If you just FTP'd the VSAM cluster to a PC, you have the data in VSAM CI's.
To extract the data from these CI's, you should know the CI size. Even then,
it is quite compilcated to extract the records, especially if you don't know
record lengths and types.. Also, to use the data you'll have to perform an
EBCDIC - ASCII conversion on the non-packed fields and convert the packed
fields to numbers yourself.

It is much easier to extract the data on the mainframe and create a flat file
with all files in DISPLAY format and transfer this file to the PC.


Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

options).

"Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com> wrote in message news:<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gxuqm70.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>...
> On 14 Jun 2002 13:59:29 -0700, dazza wrote:
> 
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.brainbench.com

Unfortunately I do not have access to the mainframe from where the
file came or the method of taking the file from the mainframe. What I
have to work with is what I have.

Upon further investigation I found that the numeric fields were binary
not packed - the remainder of the fields are EBCDIC text.

I have made some progress using a 'brute force' approach - I have been
able to work my way through the file dynamically defining the length
of each record. Basically I have found the position of the first byte
of a data record, defined everything up to that point as a single
record, and then defined from that byte until the end of that data
chunk as a series of fixed length records.

So I have something like this:

record 1      - record length = 2500 
recs 2 -> 16  - record length = 251 (data records)
record 17     - record length = 160
recs 18 -> 32 - record length = 251 (data records)
record 33     - record length = 160
recs 34 -> 48 - record length = 251 (data records)
and so on

There does appear to be a pattern. I have defined the bytes between
each group of data records as a single record - I don't know that this
is correct but it allows me to get the data records to align.

I have found that for the data records the fourth and fifth byte are a
two byte binary field that seems to define the record length.

The data records are in groups of 15 - the first byte of the first
data record in each group is binary 15 - not sure if this is
coincidence or not.

I have also found that the file came from a DB2 system. It appears to
have a header record but I have been unable to determine what
information is stored in it. Likewise for the data between the groups
of records. I did find one field in the inbetween data which appeared
to consecutively number the groups of data records.

Any more thoughts as to interpreting the information stored around the
data records would be appreciated.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How Can I Interpret a Dump of a VSAM File

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-18T13:42:16+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gxwx6g2.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`
- **References:** `<8040053e.0206131952.7e0aec19@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gxp5pj0.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <8040053e.0206141259.1685b652@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gxuqm70.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <8040053e.0206180236.1a40c126@posting.google.com>`

```
On 18 Jun 2002 03:36:38 -0700, dazza wrote:

>
>Unfortunately I do not have access to the mainframe from where the
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>data records would be appreciated.


Dazza,

If your file is an exact copy of a VSAM file, the first thing to do is to
find out the CI size. A CI (Control Interval) is a fixed size container of
512, 1024, 2048, 4096 etc. bytes.
Each CI contains at the beginning some records, and at the end control
information.

The last 4 bytes in each CI contain the CIDF (Control Interval Definition
Field), a field that contains information about the freespace in the CI.
Just before the CIDF are 3-byte RDFs (Record Definition Field). They are
stored from right to left, with the last RDF contining information on the
first record in the CI and so on.
If the records in the CI are fixed length, there are just two RDFs, the
rightmost giving the length of the records in the CI, the other one the
numbre of records.
If there is just one fixed length record in the CI, there is just one RDF.
If the records are variable size, there will be one or more pairs of RDFs,
each pair containing a length in the rightmost RDF and the number of
consecutive records with this length in the leftmost RDF.

I think that with this information you can keep on playing a bit more :)

P.S. I wrote some years ago a program that reconstructed records from VSAM
clusters. If you want me to mail it to you, send me a mail with your address
Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

#### ↳ Re: How Can I Interpret a Dump of a VSAM File

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-14T11:48:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uKkO8.14$Ss3.8@nwrddc04.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<8040053e.0206131952.7e0aec19@posting.google.com>`

```
dazza <dajames66@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:8040053e.0206131952.7e0aec19@posting.google.com...
> I am trying to access a VSAM file that has been downloaded onto a PC.
> To do this with the software that I have (ACL) I need to be able to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> will explain byte-by-byte how to read a dump of a VSAM file?

Dumps of VSAM data files do not contain information about record lenghts,
field lengths or data types stored in the file.

You MUST know "something" about the way the file was created.

While not directly on point, see my tutorial on converting COBOL-created
data for use in other programs. That will explain numeric data format and
character set conversion issues. (If the file is IBM mainframe VSAM, good
bet it was created with a COBOL program).

Go to www.talsystems.com, click to enter the site, click on "tech corner"
and read article C2IEEE.htm.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0206152030.2e8fc66a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c10d0806.0206140139.2b1712ac@posting.google.com>`

```
Just a little more near useless info in the Lilian date debate:

Your "integer of date" calculation will use Jan 1, 1601 or Oct 15,
1582 depending on whether you choose INTDATE(ANSI) or INTDATE(LILIAN)
as a compile time option. I don't think it amounts to a hill of beans,
unless you happen to be a descendant of Aloysius Lilius. I plucked the
following from a technical web site.

"...This method of time reckoning is named for Aloysius Lilius, an
advisor to Pope Gregory XIII. Lilius and his brother constructed the
Gregorian calendar."

On the other hand, I'd guess using a turn of the century date is less
cumbersome when checking calcs longhand.

Regards, Jack.

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

## Thread: Calling DLL's within COBOL — 6 msgs

### Calling DLL's within COBOL

- **From:** davidrodan18@hotmail.com (David Rodan)
- **Date:** 2002-06-16T21:35:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88a269be.0206162035.12d254b3@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello,
I am trying to call a routine within a 'C' DLL from COBOL (AcuCOBOL
5.2). I can call the DLL correctly but when I call the first routine
within the DLL it falls over with a Dr Watson error. I think the DLL
itself is OK as it works correctly with the VB and C samples provided.

Below is part of the code: 

01 qa-flags                 usage signed-long.
01 qa-return-val            usage signed-long. 

call "qatest.dll"

move zero to qa-flags
             qa-return-val
call "QA_Startup" using 
              by value qa-flags,
              returning qa-return-val
end-call


The sample 'C' equivilant is: 

int iCode;                
iCode = QA_Startup(0)

The manual for the DLL states that this startup routine should be
passed zero as a 'C' style long integer. I have tried changing the
usage to signed-int but this fails also. I have also tried to pass
zero to the routine manually, i.e. call "QA_Startup" using 0, but this
also falls over with this same error. I believe its either the way I'm
calling the DLL or the type and size of the variables I'm passing to
the routine.

Any help would be appreciated.

David Rodan.
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling DLL's within COBOL

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-17T04:11:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aek93t02gaa@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<88a269be.0206162035.12d254b3@posting.google.com>`

```
> I am trying to call a routine within a 'C' DLL from COBOL (AcuCOBOL
> 5.2). I can call the DLL correctly but when I call the first routine
> within the DLL it falls over with a Dr Watson error. I think the DLL
> itself is OK as it works correctly with the VB and C samples provided.

It's worth checking the calling convention of the DLL, as well as your
declarations in COBOL.  If this does not match, you can certainly pick up
exceptions of this order.
```

which I have been able to call correctly so I think that the call of

David Rodan.
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling DLL's within COBOL

- **From:** "Dennis Edward" <temp020305@ipipeline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-18T08:27:52-07:00

You need to have this in your program, or have the variable set in your
config:

 SET ENVIRONMENT "DLL-CONVENTION" TO 1.

"David Rodan" <davidrodan18@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:88a269be.0206162035.12d254b3@posting.google.com...
> Hello,
> I am trying to call a routine within a 'C' DLL from COBOL (AcuCOBOL
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> David Rodan.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Calling DLL's within COBOL

- **From:** davidrodan18@hotmail.com (David Rodan)
- **Date:** 2002-06-18T16:30:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

"Dennis Edward" <temp020305@ipipeline.net> wrote in message news:<ViIP8.3737$H67.20007@tor-nn1.netcom.ca>...
> You need to have this in your program, or have the variable set in your
> config:
> 
>  SET ENVIRONMENT "DLL-CONVENTION" TO 1.
>

some reason I did not have it in my config file.

Regards,

David Rodan.

## Thread: Dynamic Allocation — 8 msgs

### Dynamic Allocation

- **From:** "Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-18T13:48:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aenva8$2tl$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>`

```
Alright, dynamic allocation is now supposedly supported in OS/390 COBOL.
The manual says the name on the ASSIGN is treated as an
"environment variable" instead of a DDNAME if no JCL allocation
was done... fine. I'm supposed to put either a PATH or DSN
specification in this "environment variable" .....I code
SELECT FILE-NAME ASSIGN TO MYDDNAME. So "MYDDNAME" is
considered an "environment variable" if there's no
JCL and I presume after allocation it then is treated
as a real DDNAME... right?

I want to put in this variable:

 DSN ('MY.GDG.DATASET.NAME(+1)') NEW TRACKS SPACE(1,1)
 CATLG STORCLAS(STANDARD) MGMTCLAS(PROD)

In an OS/390 COBOL batch program How in the heck do you
define and access this "environment variable"? Where do
I put that "DSN" command? Is it another file somewhere or
what? Assuming this even works, how do you de-allocate
this DD or specify "free=close" so the DDNAME can be
re-used?
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic Allocation

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-06-18T20:50:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c21709$6409bee0$5495f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<aenva8$2tl$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>`

```
See: http://www.naspa.com/01articlesbymonth.htm

Look for:

Dynamic File Allocation with COBOL
By Ed Watson

With the advent of COBOL 2.2.0 and Language Environment (LE), the COBOL
compiler has finally entered the 21st Century. It is now possible to
perform dynamic file allocation within a COBOL batch program without
calling an external SVC99 routine.


This article is from Technical Support Magazine Dec. 2001 and is in PDF
format.  I am not sure if you have to be a member or not to get this.  If
anyone cannot get the article  then please contact me directly and I can
send the .pdf file to you.  IIRC the sample program relied on a version of
the Cobol compiler that allowed pointers to working-storage fields.  Not
having the latest compiler version I was able to get the sample to work by
using the SETPTRTO subroutine from file 331 of the cbttape. 

See: http://www.cbttape.org/cbtdowns.htm              File 331

I will hunt up my modified version of the sample program and post it.

Ron <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com> wrote in article
<aenva8$2tl$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>...

> The manual says the name on the ASSIGN is treated as an
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…

For a "complete" example of how to set environment variables, see the IBM
manual under the topic "3.4.2.4 Example: accessing environment variables" at

 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/3.4.2.4?


For how this works with dynamically allocating files see the topic, "1.8.3
Allocating files" at
 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/1.8.3

For the actual "format" expected in such environment variables, see "4.2.3.1
Assignment name for environment variable" at
 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3lr00/4.2.3.1
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic Allocation

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-06-19T23:17:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c217e7$636f06a0$28c2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<aenva8$2tl$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com> <aeo7b1$mmb$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Here is a slightly modified version of the program:

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. DYNALLC.
      *===============================================================*
      * This program dynamically allocates and deallocates a file
      * using environment variables.      No DD statements for the
      * file sould appear in the JCL:
      *
      * Needs COBOL 2.2.0
      * Compile with NODYNAM.  CALL must be static.
      *
      * PUTENV is a C function that creates an environment variable.
      * See C/C++ Run-Time Library Reference (SC28-1663)
      *
      * Language Environment Programming Reference (SC26-3312)
      *
      * COBOL Language Reference (SC26-9046-04)
      *
      *===============================================================*
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT FILEIN ASSIGN  UT-S-DYNFILE
                  FILE STATUS IS FILEIN-STATUS.
      /
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  FILEIN
           RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS
           RECORDING MODE IS F
           LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS FILEIN-REC.
       01  FILEIN-REC.
           05 FILLER             PIC X(80).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  FILEIN-STATUS         PIC 9(02).
       01  RC                    PIC 9(9) BINARY.
       01  ADDRESS-POINTER       POINTER.
       01  FILE-ENVIRONMENT-VARIABLE PIC X(31)
           VALUE 'DYNFILE=DSN(EGHF3PY.TEMP80) SHR'.
      *
       01  INPUT-REC             PIC X(80).
       01  IN-COUNT              PIC 9(9)   BINARY VALUE 0.
      /
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       INITIAL-STUFF.

***********SET ADDRESS-POINTER TO ADDRESS OF
************** FILE-ENVIRONMENT-VARIABLE.
           CALL "SETPTO" USING ADDRESS-POINTER
                               FILE-ENVIRONMENT-VARIABLE.

           CALL "PUTENV" USING BY VALUE ADDRESS-POINTER
               RETURNING RC.
           IF RC NOT = ZERO
               DISPLAY 'PUTENV FAILED'
               GOBACK
           END-IF.

           OPEN INPUT FILEIN.

       READER.
           READ FILEIN INTO INPUT-REC,
               AT END GO TO E-O-J.
           ADD +1 TO IN-COUNT.
           DISPLAY FILEIN-REC.
           GO TO READER.

       E-O-J.
           CLOSE FILEIN.
           GOBACK.


Here is the subprogram:

      *****************************************************************
      *     Documentation:                                            *
      * SETPTRTO - SET pointer-name TO ADDRESS OF working-storage-name*
      * --------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    The purpose of this general routine is to set the pointer  *
      * POINTER-TO to the address of the object POINTED-OBJECT. Since *
      * we do not touch the latter, its type is insignificant, and is *
      * reduced here to the minimum, PIC X.                           *
      *    This subroutine forces the address of the WORKING-STORAGE  *
      * data item to the pointer.  This subroutine should be used in  *
      * cojunction with a regular SET command in order to establish   *
      * the right addressing.                                         *
      *    Usage of the routine is like that:                         *
      *    ...                                                        *
      * CALL 'SETPTRTO' USING WS-PTR WS-AREA.                         *
      * SET ADDRESS OF LS-AREA TO WS-PTR.                             *
      *    ...                                                        *
      * and from than on, any reference to LS-AREA-A1 etc., will      *
      * actually refer to some part of WS-AREA.                       *
      *    The "Application programming Guide" (*) brings in part 4 - *
      * "Compiling Your Program", an example similar to my SETPTRTO,  *
      * under the name GETADDRESS.  This subroutine does              *
      * "SET pointer TO ADDRESS OF data-item",                        *
      * while both are in the LINKAGE SECTION.  Note that calling such*
      * a subroutine (IBM's or mine), must be BY REFERENCE (which is  *
      * the default).                                                 *
      ******************************************************************
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. SETPTO.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
-INC LCS001C
       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01  POINTER-TO          POINTER.
       01  POINTED-OBJECT      PIC X.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING POINTER-TO POINTED-OBJECT.
             SET POINTER-TO TO ADDRESS OF POINTED-OBJECT
             GOBACK.


William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in article
<aeo7b1$mmb$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>...
> For a "complete" example of how to set environment variables, see the IBM
> manual under the topic "3.4.2.4 Example: accessing environment variables"
at
> 
>  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/3.4.2.4?
> 
> 
> For how this works with dynamically allocating files see the topic,
"1.8.3
> Allocating files" at
>  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/1.8.3
> 
> For the actual "format" expected in such environment variables, see
"4.2.3.1
> Assignment name for environment variable" at
>  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3lr00/4.2.3.1
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Alright, dynamic allocation is now supposedly supported in OS/390
COBOL.
> > The manual says the name on the ASSIGN is treated as an
> > "environment variable" instead of a DDNAME if no JCL allocation
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic Allocation

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-19T21:21:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aere7f$4ra$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<aenva8$2tl$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com> <aeo7b1$mmb$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <01c217e7$636f06a0$28c2f943@chottel>`

```
One other (minor) requirement.  Just as you must compile this program with
the NODYNAM compiler option, you MUST also compile it with

 PGMNAME(LONGMIXED)

See:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/3.4.3

and related to this - in all the IBM examples, they use "putenv" and
"getenv" *NOT* "PUTENV" and "GETENV".  I can't guarantee that the upper-case
names will fail - but they certainly SEEM to be unsupported.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic Allocation

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-20T10:10:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aesr9s$ij0$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<aenva8$2tl$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com> <aeo7b1$mmb$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <01c217e7$636f06a0$28c2f943@chottel> <aere7f$4ra$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In spite of that, my test program (posted in another thread)
was compiled with pgmname(compat) and used "PUTENV" (all caps)
in the call. It executed without problems. NODYNAM however was required.
Maybe, longmixed is "really" only required if a program name
does not meet compat requirements? Or possibly when you are
running under U.S.S.?  Anyway, it DID work in native OS/390 batch
without it.

Also, when you use longmixed and longupper you must use
the pre-linker before the final link, otherwise the link
fails. I did not need the pre-linker however for the test
program.

Ron


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message

> the NODYNAM compiler option, you MUST also compile it with
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> "getenv" *NOT* "PUTENV" and "GETENV".  I can't guarantee that the
upper-case
> names will fail - but they certainly SEEM to be unsupported.
>
…[184 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: GETTING THE ASCII VALUE FOR CHARACTERS(NUMERIC & ALPHABET) IN COBOL — 3 this month / 9 total

### GETTING THE ASCII VALUE FOR CHARACTERS(NUMERIC & ALPHABET) IN COBOL

- **From:** araba552000@yahoo.co.uk (araba slang)
- **Date:** 2002-06-19T02:24:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<29b57f17.0206190124.3073f865@posting.google.com>`

```
I have a record of length 38 (numeric & alphabet). I need to get the
ascii value of each character (in COBOL) in the record before I XOR it
in COBOL. Please how do I go about it.


Araba Slang
```

#### ↳ Re: GETTING THE ASCII VALUE FOR CHARACTERS(NUMERIC & ALPHABET) IN COBOL

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-19T19:32:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<T_4Q8.28584$_j6.1647510@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<29b57f17.0206190124.3073f865@posting.google.com>`

```

"araba slang" <araba552000@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message
news:29b57f17.0206190124.3073f865@posting.google.com...
> I have a record of length 38 (numeric & alphabet). I need to get the
> ascii value of each character (in COBOL) in the record before I XOR it
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Araba Slang

CHAR-VALUE = FUNCTION ORD(DATA-CHAR)

i.e.,
FUNCTION ORD("A") = 65

---
the reverse function is CHAR

DATA-CHAR = FUNCTION CHAR(CHAR-VALUE)

i.e.
FUNCTION CHAR(65) = "A"
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GETTING THE ASCII VALUE FOR CHARACTERS(NUMERIC & ALPHABET) IN COBOL

- **From:** "Schroeder" <jfriedman@nc.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-23T23:34:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qWsR8.5282$wR.391086@twister.southeast.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<29b57f17.0206190124.3073f865@posting.google.com> <T_4Q8.28584$_j6.1647510@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:T_4Q8.28584$_j6.1647510@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "araba slang" <araba552000@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Jerry - he might need (ORD minus 1) rather than simply ORD - remember
low-value is the first character in the character set so has an ordinal
value of 1 not zero, but my guess is that Araba is probably looking for
zero, and not one.  But since I can't be sure what he is trying to
accomplish, hard to tell.

Jeff Friedman
```

<a id="t-144fe38ec8"></a>
## Thread: Dynamic Allocation - Works! — 1 msgs

### Dynamic Allocation - Works!

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-19T11:07:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aeqa8t$ja1$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>`

```
Thanks to all. This works very cool!


         CBL NODYNAM
       identification division.
       program-id. jpcobtst.
       environment division.
       input-output section.
       file-control.
           select test-file
             assign to DYNAMDD
             file status is test-status.
       data division.
       file section.
       fd  test-file
           label records are standard
           recording mode is F
           block contains 0 records.
       01  test-record pic x(80).
       working-storage section.
       01  test-status        pic xx.
       01  rc                 pic 9(9) comp.
       01  rc-display         pic 9(9).
       01  addr-pointer       usage is pointer.
       01  dynalloc.
           05 filler          pic x(12)  value
           'DYNAMDD=DSN('.
           05 dyn-dsn         pic x(46).
           05 filler          pic x(90)  value
           'NEW TRACKS SPACE(1,1) CATALOG STORCLAS(STANDARD) MGMTCLAS(WO
      -    'RK)'.
       procedure division.
       mission-control.
           set addr-pointer to address of dynalloc.
      *
      ***  test1
      *
           move 'D5.P.NOR.GDGTEST(+1))' to dyn-dsn.
           call 'PUTENV'
             using by value addr-pointer
             returning rc.
           if rc not = zero
               move rc to rc-display
               display 'putenv failed, rc = ' rc-display
               goback.
           open output test-file.
           if test-status not = '00'
               display 'open error test 1, status ' test-status
               goback.
           move 'This is a test record' to test-record.
           write test-record.
           close test-file
      *
      ***  test2 (Reuse the DD)
      *
           move 'D5.P.NOR.GDGTEST2(+1))' to dyn-dsn.
           call 'PUTENV'
             using by value addr-pointer
             returning rc.
           if rc not = zero
               move rc to rc-display
               display 'putenv failed, rc = ' rc-display
               goback.
           open output test-file.
           if test-status not = '00'
               display 'open error test 2, status ' test-status
               goback.
           move 'This is a test record' to test-record.
           write test-record.
           close test-file
           goback.
```

---

## Thread: Student Lost — 22 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Student Lost

- **From:** "Joseph Ostreicher" <joseph@professional-data.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-19T13:22:44-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d10bed5$1@news.mhogaming.com>`

```
Hello everybody, im a complete novice to programming, and I need a few
answers from the pros for guidance to get started

Im currently working as a Network administrator in a consultant company, im
a Certified with Novell, Microsoft and Citrix, Im not sure why I ended up in
the networking field, I always thought of programming, it was always in the
back of my mind, but I somehow ended up in the networking field, anyway, the
company I currently work for has a programs that the owner has written him
self about 20 years ago, the program is written in COBOL and compiled with
RMCOBOL, the company is looking in to upgrading this to a GUI interface,  I
saw this as an opportunity to jump  in to programming, the company is
willing for me to give it a try. I really want to do this but I don't know
where to start.

To start I found "Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours" on Amazon, this
book is based on Fujitsu.

questions.
1)Will Fujitsu recompile the old code that we have, without a complete
rewrite?

2)Should I rather find learning material that focuses on Accucobol?

3)What will I not be able to do with the free compiler from Fujitsu?

4)From an experience point of view, will I be able to become a complete COBL
programmer to create full and new GUI interface program from books, or
should I rather find a school?

5)Is there any reason why I cant really find a school in NYC that teaches
COBOL?

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Student Lost

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-06-19T14:38:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gh4Q8.4300$TL6.1292157@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3d10bed5$1@news.mhogaming.com>`

```
"Joseph Ostreicher" <joseph@professional-data.com> wrote in message
news:3d10bed5$1@news.mhogaming.com...
> Hello everybody, im a complete novice to programming, and I need a few
> answers from the pros for guidance to get started
>
> Im currently working as a Network administrator in a consultant company,
im
> a Certified with Novell, Microsoft and Citrix, Im not sure why I ended up
in
> the networking field, I always thought of programming, it was always in
the
> back of my mind, but I somehow ended up in the networking field, anyway,
the
> company I currently work for has a programs that the owner has written him
> self about 20 years ago, the program is written in COBOL and compiled with
> RMCOBOL, the company is looking in to upgrading this to a GUI interface,
I
> saw this as an opportunity to jump  in to programming, the company is
> willing for me to give it a try. I really want to do this but I don't know
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You can probably convert relatively painlessly.  It will really depend on
how the screen I/O was handled, but most Cobol code is pretty well the same
until you get into screen stuff.  Even there, there it tends to be similar
enough that the conversion can be mechanical.

> 2)Should I rather find learning material that focuses on Accucobol?
>

No, not neccessarily.  I would decide what I was going to use first.  It
would probably be a good idea to do your learning with the compiler you
intend on using for the project.

>3)What will I not be able to do with the free compiler from Fujitsu?
>

GUI.  You are going to have to come up with a screen interfacing mechanism
... if you go with Fujitsu, then your choices are probably PowerCobol (also
Fujitsu) or Flexus.  I use Flexus.  You should keep in mind though, that the
free Fujitsu Cobol compiler is several versions old.  Anything you buy is
going to be up-to-date, and may not work with software that old.  Whatever
Cobol you decide to use, I would suggest getting and up-to-date compiler.
Deciding to convert to a compiler already five or six years out of date is
no favour to your employer.

> 4)From an experience point of view, will I be able to become a complete
COBL
> programmer to create full and new GUI interface program from books, or
> should I rather find a school?

If you are going to actually write code for 8 hours a day, you will outpace
a typical school in about a week.  The only real way to learn is to write
lots of code.  Just be willing to do the first couple of modules over after
you finish the last few.

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: Student Lost

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-19T19:50:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Nf5Q8.310619$Gs.24562904@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3d10bed5$1@news.mhogaming.com>`

```

"Joseph Ostreicher" <joseph@professional-data.com>
>
> questions.
> 1)Will Fujitsu recompile the old code that we have, without a complete
> rewrite?

Something in between, skewing toward recompile. There may be slight dialect
differences between the old RMCOBOL and Fujitsu, but the differences will be
obvious. If you go with the current Fujitsu product, the company offers
translators for most dialects to Fujitsu.

By 'dialects,' I mean slight differences between the languages. Some (very
few) parts of COBOL were not fixed by the standard and left as 'implementor
defined.' Other (even fewer) parts of COBOL are peculiar to a specific
vendor, the most common oddity being the hook from an internal name (such as
a file) to an external device (such as a physical printer). Compiler vendors
have different ways of implementing or defining the 'hook.' But that sort of
conversion is almost mechanical.

>
> 2)Should I rather find learning material that focuses on Accucobol?

To learn, probably not.

>
> 3)What will I not be able to do with the free compiler from Fujitsu?

GUI. But Fujitsui's current compiler has PowerCOBOL - a GUI interface. Visit
www.adtools.com for more info.

>
> 4)From an experience point of view, will I be able to become a complete
COBL
> programmer to create full and new GUI interface program from books, or
> should I rather find a school?

Once you've got a handle on COBOL, Fujitsu has 'Boot Camp' for PowerCOBOL.

There is a big conceptual difference between the old COBOL programs your
boss has and the interrupt-driven programming of a GUI interface and it's
much easier to master if you have someone at hand to point out the
differences. I had an experienced VB programmer who helped quit a bit with
concepts when I made the switch a few years ago.

Then, too, there's this group for questions.

>
> 5)Is there any reason why I cant really find a school in NYC that teaches
> COBOL?

Think junior college course, not a commercial school (unless the boss is
paying for it).

>
> Thanks
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student Lost

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-20T00:19:51+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d1111a8_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3d10bed5$1@news.mhogaming.com> <Nf5Q8.310619$Gs.24562904@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3d1106e6@news.mhogaming.com>`

```
You can use PowerCOBOL version 3 which comes with the version 3 COBOL
compiler, to develop GUI applications. It is really very simple. You can
define GUI forms and drag and drop controls onto them. You then select the
events you are interested in (like "Click" for example) and write COBOL code
to handle the event. It is a little diferent from normal procedural code
because it is event driven, but you may be able to cut and paste the
existing code into the events. So, for example, when a certain button is
clicked, update the master file. You have all the existing code to do that.
All you need do is cut and paste it into the Click event of the "update"
button. PowerCOBOL is a superb product and saves many hours of work by using
prebuilt controls with their own events, Methods and Properties. Be aware
that version 3 is 16 bit and you may need to get a later copy (current is
version 6.1) to run under Win 32. (You can probably buy it from e-bay if you
search...)

further comments below...
Joseph Ostreicher <josephos@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:3d1106e6@news.mhogaming.com...
> Thanks Donald and Jerry for your response, let me make sure I understand
> this
>
> The free Fujitsu compiler that comes with the book (I think its version 3)
> is a GUI interface compiler but it doesn't allow you to create GUI
interface
> programs, in other words I can only create full dos based COBOL programs.
>
PowerCOBOL version 3 should be included on the CD. Try downloading from
www.adtools.com if it isn't.

> PowerCOBOL sounds like a really nice product, but boot camp is way beyond
> any budget, plus its in CA, there has to be a school or tutor (for less
> money) I can find someplace in NYC that will help me trough this
>
You really don't need Boot Camp. It is simple. If you get stuck, people here
(me for one) will help.

> Will Accucobo create GUI apps.?
>
> Donald, why would the screen stuff be different?, isn't the free Fujitsu
> compiler dos based?
>

Donald is not using PowerCOBOL. He is using standard COBOL (Object Oriented)
with a third party interface to handle GUI objects. This is a very
legitimate and sensible way to do things. However PowerCOBOL may be easier
for someone wanting to get into GUI COBOL quickly (I certainly found it
so... ) I still use PowerCOBOL and love the product.


> Sorry if my questions are stupid.....
>

The only stupidity is in not wanting to know <G>. You are not stupid.

Pete.

```
On Wed, 19 Jun 2002 18:27:15 -0400, "Joseph Ostreicher"
<josephos@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Thanks Donald and Jerry for your response, let me make sure I understand
>this
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sorry if my questions are stupid.....
(No you're fine, We have seen some really bad ones around here)

Sorry to jump so late.

If the program were written in RM/COBOL, you can always upgrade the
compiler/runtime to the newest version (7.5 very soon).
this will allow you to have GUI interface if you use either Liant's
COBOL/WOW, or Flexus SP2 (see the prices and differences of usage
between them).

As you've said that the programs were done 20 year ago, I will assume
that you have RM/COBOL 74 (maybe version 2.01, or 2.1). In this case
you will need to convert any data files to the new file format.
(very easy to do, and there is a tool supplied by Liant that does it,
altough I prefer to use my own program because of possible signed
decimal errors conversion). You will also need to have a look at
file-status used (if you are indeed using 74 COBOL).
If you post the output of issuing a "runcobol" or whatever the
runtime is called in your shop (it can be renamed), it may help
in determining what type of problems you may encounter while
converting.


FF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student Lost

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-06-19T19:53:18-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sO8Q8.3520$Rf7.1157868@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3d10bed5$1@news.mhogaming.com> <Nf5Q8.310619$Gs.24562904@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3d1106e6@news.mhogaming.com>`

```
"Joseph Ostreicher" <josephos@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:3d1106e6@news.mhogaming.com...
> Thanks Donald and Jerry for your response, let me make sure I understand
> this
>
> The free Fujitsu compiler that comes with the book (I think its version 3)
> is a GUI interface compiler but it doesn't allow you to create GUI
interface
> programs, in other words I can only create full dos based COBOL programs.
>

Character based would be a better way of putting it.  Plain Jane Cobol is a
character based language. You could still size your windows, and make them
pop-up style, etc., etc., but you are talking an X by Y character based
terminal.  Windows is quite capable of charcater based I/O.  It is just old
fashioned and out of style.  For some things, like a cash register, it may
be more effective.  It is certainly easier to write.  To give you an idea,
in my current OEM software package, the GUI screen DLL is about 4 megs.  The
data base module is about 0.6 megs.  The business logic (what you are
starting with) is about 0.3 megs.  The Cobol runtime is three times the
total.

> PowerCOBOL sounds like a really nice product, but boot camp is way beyond
> any budget, plus its in CA, there has to be a school or tutor (for less
> money) I can find someplace in NYC that will help me trough this
>

It is beyond mine as well.  There are on-line courses if you really want
one.  I have always found the best way to learn a language was to write an
application.  Write something that you can throw away, or use for the
project (like a good calculator) as a learning thing.  You learn to talk by
talking.  You learn to code by writing code.  Following Thanes book through
is probably all you need.

> Will Accucobo create GUI apps.?
>
Do not know, I have never used it.  Thane is probably your best bet for that
question.

> Donald, why would the screen stuff be different?, isn't the free Fujitsu
> compiler dos based?
>

The screen I/O has never quite solidified into a standard.  For example, to
clear a screen, it may be "DISPLAY SPACE" or it may be "DISPLAY (1, 1)
SPACE", or it mat be DISPLAY SPACE AT LINE 1 COLUMN 1, depending on the
compiler (and the era the program was written in). The stuff is always
similar enough that conversion is something that can be done with a good
text editing macro, but there will be differences.  The files could be
different as well, as Jerry said.
The differnces tend to be relatively trivial, but you should be able to get
the current programs working without any logic changes at all.  The changes
will be changes in syntax (like replacing all instances of "COLOUR with
COLOR", because Fujitsu did not recognize the former in version 3.

> Sorry if my questions are stupid.....
>

The only stupid questions are the ones you ask too late.  ;<)

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student Lost

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-20T11:14:01+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d12f21d_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3d10bed5$1@news.mhogaming.com> <Nf5Q8.310619$Gs.24562904@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3d110fbb@news.mhogaming.com> <3d1113b1_7@Usenet.com> <3D11B90F.167D3E26@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
No it shouldn't be a "No" <G>.

Event code in PowerCOBOL is written in standard COBOL complete with all
divisions.

It is just that what may have been a single program as a "batch" solution,
may be "split" to a series of functions or modules (actually nested
subprograms, although this is transparent to the User) which will be
activated by events in the GUI interface.

It is still COBOL, unlike MicroFocus DIALOG, which has it's own language
(albeit a good one) to handle event code.

Pete.

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3D11B90F.167D3E26@Azonic.co.nz...
> Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Shouldn't that be a 'No' then ?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student Lost

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-22T10:20:19+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d143f31_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3d10bed5$1@news.mhogaming.com> <Nf5Q8.310619$Gs.24562904@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3d110fbb@news.mhogaming.com> <3d1113b1_7@Usenet.com> <3D11B90F.167D3E26@Azonic.co.nz> <3d12f21d_5@Usenet.com> <3D14264C.293F78C4@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3D14264C.293F78C4@Azonic.co.nz...
> Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > It is just that what may have been a single program as a "batch"
solution,
> > may be "split" to a series of functions or modules (actually nested
> > subprograms, although this is transparent to the User) which will be
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Nope. I never mentioned the word "rewriting" because it DOESN'T require
rewriting.

I have done this exercise with a number of old COBOL batch programs and I
never rewrote one of them.

There is some re-structuring because of the event driven model, and there is
some cutting and pasting blocks of code to place them under the appropriate
event, but any actual re-write of existing code is minimal, provided the
original code was modular and organized around separate functions. (Mine
always is and that's one of the reasons I write Sectioned code).


> > It is still COBOL, unlike MicroFocus DIALOG, which has it's own language
> > (albeit a good one) to handle event code.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> supported.

PowerCOBOL is not LIKE COBOL, it IS COBOL (actually, it is OO COBOL). I have
used it since Version 3 and currently use 6.1. I have never noticed changes
to the COBOL, although I'm sure you're right and there probably have been
some. There have been improvements to the Methods and Properties of
controls, and that's all I've noticed. I tend to write standard (whatever
the Hell that means <G>) COBOL that will run on most platforms and I avoid
platform specific code. I don't have problems with any of it and am
sometimes amazed at what I see here in this Newsgroup.

I believe that any competent COBOL programmer on any platform could pick up
PowerCOBOL and write Windows applications with it within a couple of days.
The main "adjustment" is a mental one and involves understanding the
event-driven model. Fujitsu provide copious documentation and tutorials to
guide  people through this, but many just don't read it. PowerCOBOL (in my
opinion) is an excellent tool for leveraging existing COBOL code to a GUI
environment. That was the desire of the original poster to this thread. I
also accept that many people won't want to go this way, and that's OK too.

It works for me.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student Lost

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-23T14:21:07+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D15CB43.E56DD2AE@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3d10bed5$1@news.mhogaming.com> <Nf5Q8.310619$Gs.24562904@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3d110fbb@news.mhogaming.com> <3d1113b1_7@Usenet.com> <3D11B90F.167D3E26@Azonic.co.nz> <3d12f21d_5@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> Nope. I never mentioned the word "rewriting" because it DOESN'T require
> rewriting.
> 
> I have done this exercise with a number of old COBOL batch programs and I
> never rewrote one of them.

'Batch Programs' implies that they work on a 'batch' of data without any
user interaction, for example reading a batch of transactions from a
file (or card reader!) after being started from a batch file (shell
script) or even from an application menu.  It seems that the actual
programs under discusion are interactive using accept/display.

'Batch programs' generally have no 'User Interface', though they may
display a progress message.  These can be started from a GUI panel menu
and can run with only the progress message mechanism being replaced.

Interactive ACCEPT/DISPLAY programs require all the ACCEPT/DISPLAYs and
all the control logic implied by that to be discarded and replaced by
code that will interact with the GUI panels.

> There is some re-structuring because of the event driven model, and there is
> some cutting and pasting blocks of code to place them under the appropriate
> event, but any actual re-write of existing code is minimal, provided the
> original code was modular and organized around separate functions. (Mine
> always is and that's one of the reasons I write Sectioned code).

In what way is 'cutting and pasting' _some_ of the code into a
completely new structure, and replacing all the accept/display code,
function key checking and screen navigation code with completely
different event driven code not 'rewriting' ?

Certainly a small amount of the code would be salvaged, the amount of
this would depend on whether the existing code has the UI parts
intertwined with the business logic parts.

It is not only whether the existing code is 'modular' or not, but also
_how_ it was made 'modular'.  If it had modules that handled the
interaction and then completely different and independant modules that
handled the business logic then, yes, only the interaction modules would
need to be discarded and completely written anew.

Your argument is like saying that you can take a dictionary and turn it
into a novel without rewriting.

> PowerCOBOL is not LIKE COBOL, it IS COBOL (actually, it is OO COBOL).

Yes it is a form of OO Cobol, though not necessarily the one that will
become the standard.

> I believe that any competent COBOL programmer on any platform could pick up
> PowerCOBOL and write Windows applications with it within a couple of days.

If you look at the subject you will see that it is a 'lost student'
which is far from your criteria.

> The main "adjustment" is a mental one and involves understanding the
> event-driven model. 

I am not even sure that this 'lost student' currently understands the
interactive model in his current system, though this may in fact be an
advantage.

> PowerCOBOL (in my
> opinion) is an excellent tool for leveraging existing COBOL code to a GUI
> environment. 

Probably, but I am not convinced that it is such an excellent tool for a
beginner tasked with taking an existing older system to GUI, mainly
because he would have to take the whole system there and hasn't had your
30+ years experience in determining what would need to be changed to
create a working system.

> I have never noticed changes to the COBOL, 

In v3 PowerCobol used:

      MOVE value   TO POW-attribute-name  OF control-name
      CALL method-name OF control-name USING parameter-list

In v4 this changed to:

      MOVE value   TO "property-name" OF control-name
      INVOKE control-name "method-name" USING parameter-list

Note that quotes are used.

Also though most POW- attributes simply converted to property-names by
removing the POW- and adding quotes, many attribute-names were replaced
by different property-names:

      POW-BACKCOLOR    ->   "backColor"
      POW-FONTSIZE     ->   "Size" OF "Font"
      POW-ITALIC       ->   "Italic" OF "Font"
      POW-TEXT         ->   "Caption"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student Lost

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

```
This is a pointless argument, Richard.

However I accept that your use of "Interactive" for programs that use
ACCEPT/DISPLAY is a better description than my use of "batch".

I use "batch" to be anything that is not "on-line". ACCEPT/DISPLAY via the
console is not on-line (there is no TP monitor or GUI front end involved) so
for me, at least, that constitutes "batch". (I don't consider the extended
uses of ACCEPT/DISPLAY via the screen section as on-line either, but then, I
never use Screen Sections...). I agree this is loose usage, but it serves
for all but the most pedantic arguments...

Further quick comments below.

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3D15CB43.E56DD2AE@Azonic.co.nz...
> Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> > Nope. I never mentioned the word "rewriting" because it DOESN'T require
> > rewriting.
> >
> > I have done this exercise with a number of old COBOL batch programs and
I
> > never rewrote one of them.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
OK.

> 'Batch programs' generally have no 'User Interface', though they may
> display a progress message.  These can be started from a GUI panel menu
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes. BTW, isn't it an assumption on your part that he is referring to
"interactive" code? About as valid as me assuming he was referring to
batch...

> > There is some re-structuring because of the event driven model, and
there is
> > some cutting and pasting blocks of code to place them under the
appropriate
> > event, but any actual re-write of existing code is minimal, provided the
> > original code was modular and organized around separate functions. (Mine
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
In the way that the user interface represents a small percentage of the
code. Even if you are right and he is referring to what you term interactive
code, how many lines of his program will be ACCEPT/DISPLAY statements and
the consequent processing/navigation? It will be a small percentage. The nub
of the processing happens AFTER the user interaction or dependent upon it.
My point was that this underlying  processing does not require rewriting
(or, it shouldn't if it is modular).


> Certainly a small amount of the code would be salvaged, the amount of
> this would depend on whether the existing code has the UI parts
> intertwined with the business logic parts.
>
This is where we differ. I don't think it is a small part. But as neither of
us has any solid data on this, the argument (as noted above) is pointless.

> It is not only whether the existing code is 'modular' or not, but also
> _how_ it was made 'modular'.  If it had modules that handled the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Hahaha! (Excellent rhetoric!). The flaw in the analogy is that a dictionary
and a novel are two completely different things. A GUI based program, and a
non-GUI based program that does the same function, are NOT two different thi
ngs. Therefore the analogy reduces to: "Your argument is like saying you can
take a dictionary and turn it into another dictionary without rewriting."
Obviously, this could be accomplished by cutting and pasting.

> > PowerCOBOL is not LIKE COBOL, it IS COBOL (actually, it is OO COBOL).
>
> Yes it is a form of OO Cobol, though not necessarily the one that will
> become the standard.
>

If we ever see a Standard, and if we actually care about what is in it. I
don't...

> > I believe that any competent COBOL programmer on any platform could pick
up
> > PowerCOBOL and write Windows applications with it within a couple of
days.
>
> If you look at the subject you will see that it is a 'lost student'
> which is far from your criteria.
>
Fair comment. However, I don't believe the person concerned is any more
likely to succeed by NOT using PowerCOBOL than he is by using it. I
recommended it's use (with qualifications), because I have found it to be a
good product and I well remember how helpful it was to me when I was
learning about the GUI environment.

> > The main "adjustment" is a mental one and involves understanding the
> > event-driven model.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > opinion) is an excellent tool for leveraging existing COBOL code to a
GUI
> > environment.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
OK, so we disagree. I think it is; you think it may not be. That's why
forums like this are valuable. It is certainly arguable. The reader may
examine the arguments and decide for him/herself.


> > I have never noticed changes to the COBOL,
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>       POW-TEXT         ->   "Caption"

Thanks for that. I probably took these changes in my stride (they are hardly
"earthshattering" <G>) and it is only now when you point them out that I
remember something along these lines. Certainly the change from CALL to
INVOKE is significant and, in my mind I associate that with the move from 16
to 32 bit.

I think it is fair to say that any changes have only served to make
PowerCOBOL even closer to standard COBOL than it was before. My point was
that PowerCOBOL IS COBOL. It is certainly much more COBOL than MF DIALOG....

Having said everythng I want to on this topic, I won't be responding
further.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student Lost

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-24T08:59:58+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D16D17E.A01C15BD@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3d10bed5$1@news.mhogaming.com> <Nf5Q8.310619$Gs.24562904@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3d110fbb@news.mhogaming.com> <3d1113b1_7@Usenet.com> <3D11B90F.167D3E26@Azonic.co.nz> <3d12f21d_5@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:

> This is a pointless argument, Richard.

Well obviously I don't think that it is 'pointless'.  A lost student
comes here seeking advice on moving to a GUI and asks if his existing
programs can just be used without rewriting.  I found your answers to be
quite misleading.
 
> I use "batch" to be anything that is not "on-line". ACCEPT/DISPLAY via the
> console is not on-line 

When I run my terminal console session over a dial-up line the modem
tells me it is 'on-line'.  This is the actual definition of what
'on-line' means, though it is also used when connected without a modem.

'On-Line' and 'Interactive' and _not_ equivalent terms except in so far
as it needs to be on-line to be interactive.  2780 terminals for example
were _batch_ card reader and printer and were never 'interactive' even
when 'on-line'.  In fact printers often have an 'on-line' button and/or
light, this doesn't mean that they are not batch devices.

> (there is no TP monitor or GUI front end involved) so
> for me, at least, that constitutes "batch". (I don't consider the extended
> uses of ACCEPT/DISPLAY via the screen section as on-line either, but then, I
> never use Screen Sections...). I agree this is loose usage, but it serves
> for all but the most pedantic arguments...

It is not 'pedantic', it is a matter of definition that everyone can use
and apply.  Batch is, by definition, where a 'batch' of several
transactions are processed at one time.  Interactive is where a user
inputs a transaction, or part of a transaction, and interacts with the
computer in some way to get that accepted or processed.

Whether it is interactive using a TP Monitor or is directly interacting
with the user via a GUI, TUI or just ACCEPT/DISPLAY is irrelevant.

> BTW, isn't it an assumption on your part that he is referring to
> "interactive" code? About as valid as me assuming he was referring to
> batch...

No.  Batch programs do not have any significant user interface (they
don't interact with the user), thus whether this non-existant UI is GUI
or TUI is irrelevant.  The only reason that a GUI would be requested is
that at least some of the programs are _interactive_ and the GUI would
be different from the existing UI.

Actually, I find that when account type system users request GUI they
mostly don't care whether it is Graphical or Text as long as it is event
driven with pull-down menus, push buttons, list boxes etc. I do TUIs
using SP2 that run on 'console', on telnet terminals, and on real
green-screen VT100 type terminals.  Of course these could also run as
GUIs on Windows or over the internet, but there is no loss of UI
functionality just because it is 'console' (except showing pictures).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student Lost

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-20T13:54:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<z8lQ8.305340$%y.24861039@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3d10bed5$1@news.mhogaming.com> <Nf5Q8.310619$Gs.24562904@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3d110fbb@news.mhogaming.com>`

```

"Joseph Ostreicher" <josephos@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:3d110fbb@news.mhogaming.com...
> Also will PowerCOBOL recompile the old COBOL codes

Ah, yes. BUT it would be like using a 747 to taxi down the tarmac to visit
the coffee shop. You would not be taking advantage of the other, powerful
features.

To put it another way, basic COBOL (like you've got with the older programs)
is but a small subset of the capabilities of PowerCOBOL (or other GUI
processor).

In a Windows environment, there could be literally millions of events
happening every minute (disk access, mouse movement, incoming network
traffic, etc.). Not only, but Windows is a pre-emptive multi-tasker (the
events are not necessarily processed sequentially). A GUI front end
continually monitors these events and sorts out the ones in which your
program is interested, tries to reconcile conflicts within your program to
assign priorities, and only then does your program get an opportunity to do
something.

For example: In your application, you enter an account code (leaving aside,
for a moment, that other parts of your program can get control when you
press each key or when you release the pressed key) and press RETURN. Your
program gets a record and begins filling various boxes on the screen. When
one particular box gets filled, a field-validation routine triggers
automatically, that, in turns, needs information in yet another field. By
accessing field #3, the validation routine in turn triggers a spell-checker.
Finally, everything collapses back to you. Or not.

The whole thing is immensely complicated and quite beyond the range of human
comprehension. Fortunately, PowerCOBOL (et al) reduces your programming
effort to:

Perform Until DONE
   Call "GET"
   Call "MULL"
   Call "PUT"
End-Perform
LayBack.

With suitable macros, the above can be shortened to:

Ponder.
LayBack.
```

#### ↳ Re: Student Lost

- **From:** "Joseph Ostreicher" <josephos@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-19T20:41:14-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d112634$1@news.mhogaming.com>`

#### ↳ Re: Student Lost

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-20T12:10:59+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D11B843.3A5A248A@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3d10bed5$1@news.mhogaming.com>`

```
Joseph Ostreicher wrote:
 
> questions.
> 1)Will Fujitsu recompile the old code that we have, without a complete
> rewrite?

That often depends on what extensions were used.  It is possible that
some programs will recompile with only minor changes, others may need
extensive rewriting or conversion.  eg if the existing programs are
console based and use 'DISPLAY xxx AT nnnn' you will find Fujitsu has
'DISPLAY xxxx LINE nn COLUMN nn'.  These are both extensions to ANS
Cobol and are often different in detail and exact operation even when
they look similar. 

There may also be small but annoying differences in how files are opened
(ref OPTIONAL) and how file sharing and locking are implemented.
 
> 2)Should I rather find learning material that focuses on Accucobol?
> 
> 3)What will I not be able to do with the free compiler from Fujitsu?

* Access the RMCobol data files. 

Each vendor's indexed file structure is implemented differently. This
means that you would have to convert the files, probably writing an
RMCobol program to extract to a flat file then reading this into a
Fujitsu program to create and indexed file.

This makes it an all-or-nothing conversion.

* Multi-Platform

One of the major advantages of RMCobol, ACCuCobol and (slightly less so)
is that a compiled program can be distributed to very many environments
without even having to recompile.  Your system should just run on DOS,
Windows, OS/2, Unix, or whatever you can get a 'runtime' for.

Fujitsu does have systems for Sun and HPUX, but it is primarily a
Windows system now.

> 4)From an experience point of view, will I be able to become a complete COBL
> programmer to create full and new GUI interface program from books, or
> should I rather find a school?

Fujitsu has PowerCobol GUI.  This is 'based on Cobol' and is Fujitsu and
Windows only.  

Because you would not be able to interwork the system between the
existing RMCobol and the new Fujitsu it would mean that the only way to
progress is:

      Learn all new Cobol, PowerCobol
      Rewrite whole system into, say, PowerCobol
         every nuance of the system being reproduced
      Write data conversion programs
      Convert data files
      Implement new system
      Rewrite to cater for user complaints, bugs, 
         oversights, design problems, user
         interface issues.

      At some time in the future:
         Rewrite to new compiler for new environment.

There are GUIs that cater for several different Cobol vendors and
different operating environments, such as SP2 from Flexus
(www.Flexus.com).  You may even be able to get a version of this that
can be used with your exist RM Cobol compiler (depending on version).

This may allow you to, say, build an SP2 version of one or two programs
and have them used alongside the existing system while you develop your
skills and test out the GUI interface ideas.  The existing programs
would still work.  It may be that only the day to day programs are
actually required as full GUI, infrequent programs could stay as
DOS/console programs.

Process would be:

      Learn Cobol and GUI (eg SP2)
      Repeat:
          Write sample programs to work with 
               existing system
          Implement alongside existing programs
          Get user feedback as to how well it
               works/ additional requirements
      Until enough has been done

This would seem, to me, to give earliest feedback as to how well you are
learning the language and the processes and the earlist and risk free
benefits.
```

---

<a id="t-2bfceb5027"></a>
## Thread: ODBC — 10 msgs

```
On Fri, 21 Jun 2002 01:18:33 GMT, "Daniel Yataco"
<dymcs@earthlink.net> wrote:

>I have one customer with a Financial application using  RMCobol 85 version
>5.24, he wants to migrate the data to SQL database and I dont want to write
>programs to create text files from the cobol data files. So it is more easy
>using ODBC and DTS from SQL.
If this is the situation then I would sugest that you do just that. It
is far more cost effective than to set-up all ODBC requirements (e.g.
field definition, record types, nullable fields, date fields and
so on). Some of the other possible solutions would take even more time
than creating the file convertion programs.

If you personally do not have the time or knowledge to do it, then any
COBOL programmer with some experience will be able to to it for a fee.


Regards

FF
```

If you can call the Windows API (and I know someone has posted COBOL
examples of this somewhere), you can download the SDK for free from
Microsoft and write all the ODBC access code yourself. The  ODBC susbsystem
is also free (and included since Win 95/second release as part of the basic
Windows install.)

No out of pocket expense at all!

(You should be able to master the ODBC calls in only, say 50-100 hours.)
(Assuming you are already comfortable with Windows API calls).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** j.bradley@liant.com (John Bradley)
- **Date:** 2002-06-24T13:10:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<25fb80a6.0206241210.1dffde23@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<Dd9Q8.6100$Fv1.640370@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <k843hu810q97kqmucji6qo7urhm8n4hbik@4ax.com> <S6sQ8.8993$uH2.2137@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <b5l4hukpa903ofmm6igqfkdggm76k34oig@4ax.com> <J9vQ8.9370$uH2.4136@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <0ck5hu4e37btef9v066bbt7a86qepl8d3k@4ax.com> <aILQ8.14969$x82.476040@weber.videotron.net> <ssMQ8.11418$uH2.4078@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Daniel,

As Frederico alluded, Relativity is primarily targeted at the COBOL
developer who wants to expose his applications' data as a
(well-normalized) relational database.  It can be used for conversion,
but the cost justification for a one-time conversion is sometimes (but
not always) difficult.

Just to clarify, Relativity is not $5,000, especially for use in a
one-time conversion.  The Data Designer (which is the part that allows
you to remodel a very unrelational COBOL database into a good
relational database) is $2,600 (US list) and the Data Manager (the
actual ODBC Driver) is $150 (US list).  Ordinarily, the (COBOL)
application provider would need the Designer, but their customers
would need only the ODBC driver for $150 (or less).  For a one-time
conversion, you would need both ($2,750).  Whether or not this is
better than the alternative (custom COBOL application) depends on how
complex the COBOL database is.  If it is a typical COBOL application
with hundreds of files, each of which could have dozens of record
types, it is quite often the case that the $2,750 for Relativity and a
few dozen hours of data modeling is much, much less expensive that a
custom application in COBOL.

The thing to remember is that COBOL's "flat files" are anything but
flat, and they are typically not at all normalized.  Unlike many
proprietary 4GL's which have files that correspond to more-or-less
relational tables, COBOL applications typically have very complex
native COBOL structures embedded in the files.  It is the unraveling
of these (and the knowledge of the application necessary to do so)
that is expensive.

Also, we have spent over $5,000,000 developing the ODBC driver, so I
think you would probably have to spend a little more than 50-100 hours
to write your own, even for a single COBOL application.

Good luck, let us know if we can help.

Regards,
John Bradley
Liant Software Corporation

> client using CLARION and  I just paid around $249 for the stand alone ODBC
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…

## Thread: Calling MF COBOL-DLL from VB — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Calling MF COBOL-DLL from VB

- **From:** klaas_zwaan@yahoo.com (Klaas Zwaan)
- **Date:** 2002-06-20T06:04:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2f897a57.0206200504.2c48274@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi there,
Our team is building an application with a VB-frontend and a
COBOL-backend.
Sofar no problem.
But a small part is using OO-COBOL and when we build this into a DLL
we have to supply a part of the application-server from the NetExpress
environment to get it working. How can we build the COBOL-DLL without
the use of any part of the application-server?

Thanks in advance,
Klaas
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling MF COBOL-DLL from VB

- **From:** "Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr>
- **Date:** 2002-06-21T16:53:31+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aevb5m$4dt$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`
- **References:** `<2f897a57.0206200504.2c48274@posting.google.com>`

```
As far as I know, Net Express will not allow you to statically link an
Object Cobol class to a DLL or EXE. This means that you will always have to
ship components of the app-server. I'm afraid you can't overcome this.

Panos
