###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Losing my mind over simple syntax (non)error

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-27T12:34:19-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B940B6CB0167D1EA10E07880@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3d1a01c5.22414660@ausnews.austin.ibm.com> <3D1AB29D.3A689A93@Azonic.co.nz> <JtFS8.63690$UT.4369785@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Thu, 27 Jun 2002 10:40:41 -0400, Russell Styles wrote
> I always thought that rule was rather dumb.  What would
> be the harm in allowing an end-perform, even if redundant?

You have to either require it or prohibit it -- no optional -- because 
otherwise END-PERFORM becomes ambiguous. For example, in

  PERFORM UNTIL WHATEVER
    PERFORM 1100-PARAGRAPH
  END-PERFORM

which PERFORM does the END-PERFORM match?

But you can't require it for an out-of-line PERFORM, because that would 
be incompatible with existing programs (COBOL74 programs).

So it can't be optional and can't be required, thus it must be 
prohibited.

A lot of this mess is because PERFORM is already overloaded -- it's 
both a procedure call (albeit a poor one) and a looping mechanism. I 
see frequent confusion in programmers due to this overloading.

Edward Reid
```

---

<a id="t-4e80138b56"></a>
## Thread: Shared Memory and Virtual Heaps — 11 msgs

### Shared Memory and Virtual Heaps

- **From:** dmckeon@ameritas.com (Dan McKeon)
- **Date:** 2002-06-26T14:19:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<61ad99f6.0206261319.6a297933@posting.google.com>`

```
I need to write a program that will write a data string to memory so
it can be read by another program.  I tried using Virtual Heaps
(CBL_OPEN_VFILE, CBL_WRITE_VFILE, CBL_READ_FILE, CBL_CLOSE_VFILE).  It
works great until I need one executable to write to memory and another
one to read it.  It must somehow know the handle of the thread that
wrote the heap because I can get a dll to read from the heap that the
executable wrote to but cannot get another executable to read it. 
Also, I can run two executables at the same time, and the
CBL_OPEN_VFILE will use the same heap-id.  If the function was
querying Windows for this information, it could not use the same
heap-id or we would have a memory leak.

I also tried using shared memory (CBL_ALLOC_MEM, CBL_PUT_SHMEM_PTR,
CBL_GET_SHMEM_PTR, CBL_FREE_MEM).  In theory, this should work better
for me, but it does not work.  I can allocate a buffer, but I am a bit
confused on how the CBL_PUT_SHMEM_PTR function works.  You pass it the
node-value (which is a pointer), and then the name of the node.  Well,
where do you tell it what data to write to memory??  Also, what would
the node-value be?  If I knew the name of the pointer to the data, I
wouldn't need this function.  On top of that, the MicroFocus
documentation really leaves something to be desired.  I would
appreciate any information and code examples if you have them. 
Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: Shared Memory and Virtual Heaps

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-26T16:41:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<afdcdt$pug$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<61ad99f6.0206261319.6a297933@posting.google.com>`

```
Is there a reason that you aren't simply using the (standard COBOL) EXTERNAL
phrase?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Shared Memory and Virtual Heaps

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-27T14:04:35+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D1B0D63.EE91B317@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<61ad99f6.0206261319.6a297933@posting.google.com> <afdcdt$pug$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
 
> > works great until I need one executable to write to memory and another
> > one to read it.  

> Is there a reason that you aren't simply using the (standard COBOL) EXTERNAL
> phrase?

There is a very simple reason: it won't work.  EXTERNAL is only shared
to programs _within_ an executable not between two different
executables.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Shared Memory and Virtual Heaps

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-27T11:39:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<afff57$5j1$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<61ad99f6.0206261319.6a297933@posting.google.com> <afdcdt$pug$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3D1B0D63.EE91B317@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Sorry but EXTERNAL data is shared between executables - as long as they are
in the same run-unit.  It is true that if they programs are not within the
same run-unit, it won't work.
```

#### ↳ Re: Shared Memory and Virtual Heaps

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2002-06-27T00:35:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0206262335.5d8c23b4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<61ad99f6.0206261319.6a297933@posting.google.com>`

```
I cannot speak from experience of these routines themselves (but I've
used other memeotry handlers...)

Looking at the specifications it seems to me that what you should be
doing is:

1. get address of memory that you want to make visible to others:
either by ADDRESS OF or CBL_ALLOC_MEM, you just need the POINTER to it

2. use PUT_SHMEM_PTR to associate the POINTER from (1) with a name
that is then visible by other programs.

3. the other program(s) use GET_SHMEM_PTR to recover the POINTER
associated with the name. Of course these "other" program have to
"know" tha names and what the named node refers to, i.e. its structure
etc. so that it can use SET to map the POINTER to some linkage section
memory (as with other POINTERS)

So: if program A has two blocks of memory it wishes to share; it calls
PUT_SHMEM_PTR to tell the world about "nodes" FRED and MARGARET
passing the name and the address of the area with each call. The
program that wants to access FRED refers to it in a GET_SHMEM_PTR, but
that only retrieves the POINTER and it has to be mapped to a correctly
structed memory area (a good case for COPYfiles, of course). If it
doesn't want MARGARET it doesn't need to access it at all. And so on.

I don't have any experience of VFILEs...

graham


dmckeon@ameritas.com (Dan McKeon) wrote in message news:<61ad99f6.0206261319.6a297933@posting.google.com>...
...
> I also tried using shared memory (CBL_ALLOC_MEM, CBL_PUT_SHMEM_PTR,
> CBL_GET_SHMEM_PTR, CBL_FREE_MEM).  In theory, this should work better
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: Shared Memory and Virtual Heaps

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-27T15:23:00+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D1B1FC4.6AE2184C@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<61ad99f6.0206261319.6a297933@posting.google.com>`

```
Dan McKeon wrote:
> 
> I need to write a program that will write a data string to memory so
> it can be read by another program.  

All the methods you mentioned can be used to pass data between programs
within one run-unit. Though the SHMEM calls will pass the pointer to
another 'run-unit' this is part of the concurrency functionality and I
suspect that it only works between run-units within the same executable,
ie when the 2nd has been created with the CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT.

To pass data to another executable you need to use the OS mechanisms
such as DDE, clipboard, pipes or streams.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Shared Memory and Virtual Heaps

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-27T15:37:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0jGS8.32309$cE5.30851@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<61ad99f6.0206261319.6a297933@posting.google.com> <3D1B1FC4.6AE2184C@Azonic.co.nz> <61ad99f6.0206270507.1f15bff1@posting.google.com>`

```
Dan McKeon <dmckeon@ameritas.com> wrote in message
news:61ad99f6.0206270507.1f15bff1@posting.google.com...
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:<3D1B1FC4.6AE2184C@Azonic.co.nz>...
> > Dan McKeon wrote:
> > >
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> drawingboard.

I have followed this thread with some interest, as data sharing under
Windows/32 is one of my "hot buttons." You can read my cleverly-titled
article, "Data Sharing in 32-bit Windows" if you obtain a copy of the
October, 1999 issue of BASICally Speaking magazine. Reprints available from
the publisher www.infoms.com, follow links. (Reprints also available from
me, since I own the copyright).

But I digress...

If you are working under 32-bit Windows and your brand of COBOL can call a
standard Dynamic Link Library (the whole Windows API is a bunch of DLLs),
Windows itself contains a vast number of functions to allow *cooperating*
processes to share memory: DDE, memory mapped files and atoms among others.

I emphasize *cooperating* processes in that most of these methods require
the creation of a named object of some sort; the cooperating applications
would each need to know the name used. (via COPYLIB, or even reading a disk
file to get the current name).

There is no reason these functions could not be incorporated into a single
DLL, and it could be written so that the COBOL programmer's total set of
"things to do" would be:

1. WS entry for name of object.
2. WS table of action/return/error codes and literal values.
3. These "call" rules (subject to design refinement; also your syntax may
vary for WinAPI calls)
     CALL 'foo'  USING
          ACTION,
          Dll-name
          Object-name,
          Pointer-to-data,
          length-of-data
     RETURNING Result

Action = Action-initialize >>  gives the program access to the
library/shared data
          = Action-get        >>   returns pointer-to-data and data-length
(fills a buffer)
          = Action-store    >>    pass pointer-to-data (buffer) and
data-length
         = Action-terminate >>  close off this program's access to the
shared data

Result would be based on the value of action; i.e, success, failure,
something like that.  Gets and Stores would have mutual exclusion to
guarantee data integity.

Using this kind of design, you don't even have to worry about linking the
code (although you 'd no doubt have to make some entry somewhere to indicate
an external DLL is used by this application), and you'd have to insure the
DLL was available at run time. (There is a way around that, too, but that
would require a direct call to Windows from your code).

To make this useful across multiple-source-code-language programs you
currently have installed, the DLL used should not rely on the COBOL or C or
whatever run-times are required to operate the individual programs - it
should use only "pure" Windows calls. (The same DLL could be used by
programs written in different languages as long as they all have the ability
to call a WIndows DLL).

If you wanted, this DLL could simultaneously support multiple buffers; this
might add a parameter to the CALL, but would not otherwise present any
significant technical challenge.

Data limit? 2GB  (more if you can accept the use of Windows objects
requiring hard-disk space).(And have such disk space available!).

If you (or anyone else) are are interested in obtaining such a DLL, drop me
a note. I could create one to this spec for a pretty modest price (agreeing
on the specs will probably take longer than coding and testing).

(Sorry, no: I could not create this DLL with a Component Object Model
interface).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Shared Memory and Virtual Heaps

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

- **References:** `<61ad99f6.0206261319.6a297933@posting.google.com> <3D1B1FC4.6AE2184C@Azonic.co.nz> <61ad99f6.0206270507.1f15bff1@posting.google.com> <0jGS8.32309$cE5.30851@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <bfdfc3e8.0206290638.1fd7febd@posting.google.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0206290638.1fd7febd@posting.google.com...
> Michael,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> it to.

I am glad this is working out for you.

It's just as well you are having trouble with the "shared memory in the DLL"
because that is done using some jimmying around with entry-points when the
DLL is compiled; i.e., this performance can be dependent on the compiler.
(i.e., if the chosen address defined in the C++ compiler is used for some
other purpose by the FJ compiler...)

Beyond that, those "Active X DLLs" have some of their own rules, in that
they are not really pure DLLs, they are OLE servers.

If you can read BASIC language, I placed in the public domain some code to
access - with mutex locking - memory-mapped files. I could probably find the
direct link but since I am currently off line I'll just give you directions:

Go to the PowerBASIC Peer Support BBS at:
http://www.powerbasic.com/support/forums/Ultimate.cgi
Search
Forum name: source code (forum name is a pick and choose list thing)
Search phrase : memory-mapped
Search in: message title

OR...

You can search the source code forum by user name "Michael Mattias" (pretty
clever, huh?) and there's about a page or so of stuff I've contributed and
one of those will be the one you want.

Note: Getting the code off the BBS is kinda weird because of line breaks,
etc. The easiest way - using IE - is to hit the "edit this message" button,
then right-click in the box, select all, copy; then paste into notepad or
something. You will not be able to edit the post because you are not the
author, so you just use the "back" function of your browser to get out.

If you want to see this code in action but don't have a PB compiler, I can
recompile the EXE here and could email it to you.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Shared Memory and Virtual Heaps

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-28T07:47:28+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D1C0680.9033A5EC@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<61ad99f6.0206261319.6a297933@posting.google.com> <3D1B1FC4.6AE2184C@Azonic.co.nz> <61ad99f6.0206270507.1f15bff1@posting.google.com>`

```
Dan McKeon wrote:
> 
> Since it sounds like COBOL cannot
> write to memory in the same way, I guess I am back to the C++
> drawingboard.

In general Cobol can do anything that C can do.  It is just that C does
almost everything by calling library routines, so it hard to tell the
difference between what is 'part of C' and what is add on libraries.  In
Cobol all that is required is to call those same library routines.  Of
course it is not always easy to work out what is actually going on and
emulate it exactly.

> I did not try the EXTERNAL command (although it sounds like it does
> not work the way I need it to) because I did not know it existed.  Do
> you have some sample code utilizing this command?  Also, can you give
> me a brief run down as to what it does?  Again, thank you both very
> much!

EXTERNAL is like C's external in allowing several programs within one
run unit (one set of programs that call each other) to mutually share
blocks of memory (or files).  The easiest way to do this is to include a
COPY book in each program where this contains:

         01  Shared-Block           EXTERNAL.
             03  some-data          PIC ...
             ....

Each program can then use this one block of global data, even
dynamically loaded programs.

If the program is Windows API or is multi-threading then it will need a
protocol to read or write to the block (or blocks) as several parts of
the run-unit may be active at any time.

EXTERNAL files are much the same, they can be opened in one program and
used in others.  This may mean that if one program READs a record from a
file and then CALLs another program the record area (of which there is
only one in the run-unit) may now have a quite different record in it
(or none).
```

<a id="t-49de903fbf"></a>
## Thread: ASP -> VB -> Cobol architecture — 1 msgs

### ASP -> VB -> Cobol architecture

- **From:** "Mike Vendel" <mike_vendel@premierintl.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-26T18:35:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uhkjv7iear2ea9@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi!

 Just curious if anyone has ever implemented a project using ASP, VB, and
Cobol dlls.
 We have such a project and are getting some perplexing results.

 A short summary which I can expound upon for the interested listener. VB
COM objects house our new bizness logic. Cobol dlls house our legacy bizness
logic. ASP provides the web presentation.
We have a weird occurrence wherein the VB COM objects that call cobol dlls
must be run as a COM+ application when called from ASP, otherwise cobol
bombs.
 The cobol dlls do share information that is loaded into memory but this
shouldn't really be an issue for the regular COM objects. The dlls should be
loaded into the COM objects process space.
 A vb executable test project works fine. But the only way this architecture
will work with ASP is if the VB objects are housed in COM+.

 Any ideas or arcane references to some documentation that would help me
understand where the cobol dlls are getting loaded when dealing with VB and
ASP?

 Thanks,
 Mike
```

- **Message-ID:** `<aff9nu$fhg$00$1@news.t-online.com>`

Hi all,

my Cobol-Compiler is MicroFocus NetExpress 3.1, my operating-system is
Windows-NT 4.0 Sp6a.

So, I use the "'PC_PRINTER_OPEN" - Routine zu select the Printer that the
client need to print. I have a programm who use this routine. I wrote this
programm about 1 year - and never change the code but this code is tested
only by the operation-system WIN-NT 4.0.

We must change the operation-system of the clientï¿½s to Windows 2000
Professional. Now my routine donï¿½t work.

Has anybody one idee ??

Thanks

Ralph
```

#### ↳ Re: PC_PRINTER_OPEN Routine

- **From:** "Mike Kinasz" <mkinasz@cbspayroll.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-28T04:18:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asRS8.18625$Uu2.2880@sccrnsc03>`
- **References:** `<aff9nu$fhg$00$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
If it were me in that particular situation and using NE3.1 then i'd convert
your print routines to use Windows API (i only say this because we use these
routines and they work quite well.  i'm sorry i can't post sample source for
this particular item...)

Here's a list of calls you'd need

OpenPrinterA
StartDocPrinterA
StartPagePrinter
WritePrinter
EndPagePrinter
EndDocPrinter
ClosePrinter

GetLastError helps too if you have a problem.  (TIP:  Also pass a null
termed "RAW" string as the datatype for your StartDocPrinterA call in the
Datatype field.)   I'm pretty sure MF has some examples in their
knowledgebase.......

Maybe someone else has some better advice...... who knows..

Good Luck,
Mike

#### ↳ Re: PC_PRINTER_OPEN Routine

- **From:** jean.villemaire@microfocus.com (Jean Villemaire)
- **Date:** 2002-06-28T12:00:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b9b30ab.0206281100.395b22a5@posting.google.com>`

```
"Ralph Leiherr" <leiherr@vwda.de> wrote in message news:<aff9nu$fhg$00$1@news.t-online.com>...
> Hi all,
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ralph

Ralph,

Make sure that the printer-handle and windows-handle parameters of
your PC_PRINTER_OPEN call are initialzed properly;

           move 0 to Window-Handle
           move 0 to Printer-Handle

           call "PC_PRINTER_OPEN" using
               by reference Printer-Handle
               by reference Document-Title
               by value     Printer-Flags
               by value     Window-Handle
           end-call


You can find examples of the PC_PRINTER calls at
http://supportline.microfocus.com under Self Services click on Net
Express examples & Utilities and go on the samples page and choose the
printing category.  A zip file called pcprintercalls.zip will have
several demo programs.

Hope this helps.
```

```
 hi! 

i'm wondering if it's possible to do cobol coding on home
computer/laptop. i won't be able to work full-time. just occasionally.
i'm thinking there must be people doing this from home - full time -
wired up to their employers' computers.... 

if so, can anyone recommend what laptop i should get. (we'll still make
use of it, if i'm not able to do it, or unable to find clients or
subcontracting company willing to allow me to do this.) 

then, i was a cobol programmer for 12 years, on IBM, HP, & DEC
mainframes, then on IBM sys36, and probably a couple others i've
forgotten. i don't mean to brag, but i think was pretty darn good at,
before i became too ill to work. i've now been out of it for another
12yrs. i thought cobol would've been gone by now - who knew LOL anyway,
i've found the FAQ and newsgroups, and a whole bunch of other stuff. 

so...... i've seen several book lists - can ya'll recommend the best
books to get for refreshing my memory and updating it? 

thank you very much for any info, and i'll be eternally grateful! 

debbie s.
```

#### ↳ Re: general questions

- **From:** leclaire <leclaire@nospam.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-28T00:41:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D1BAF06.3864FB14@nospam.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<5497-3D1B7C61-161@storefull-2354.public.lawson.webtv.net>`

```

For the past decade I've relied upon Microsoft COBOL v3.0, which was
actually a repackaged version of Microfocus COBOL.  It is relatively
simple yet powerful and absolutely reliable.  It creates stand-alone
sexcutable files, and best of all, the entire compiler and all its
libraries, plus several invaluable tools (text editor, sort/merge etc)
all fit easily on a single floppy disk.  

Most of my work was done on 386's, but I still keep a copy installed on
my newer laptop for throwing together an occasional quick & dirty app.  
I've tried newer versions of Microfocus but instead now use Fujitsu's
COBOL suite to develop commercial apps.  It has a nice database
interface and people like the radio buttons and scroll bars and such.

Which COBOL compiler you choose really depends on just what you intend
to develop with it.  Wishing you the best of luck...
```

#### ↳ Re: general questions

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-06-28T05:46:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c21e56$30953220$fe95f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<5497-3D1B7C61-161@storefull-2354.public.lawson.webtv.net>`

```
If you are  just going to dial-up and use the PC as a 3270 terminal with
all the work being done on the employers computer, the get the fastest
modem/connection you can.  There are many 3270 emulators for the PC.  One I
have used is Citrix.  Don't know the cost as y company paid for it.  At
home will need a dedicated telephone line so you can receive telephone
calls while on your computer.

If you are going to code, compile, and test on your PC  then the isssue is
whitch Cobol vendors product is most compatible with your employers
version.  Each vendor has their own extensions, but you should be ok if you
code within the Standard. Then again you employer may want to use the
extensions,  Many Cobol compilers are available for the PC and most any new
computer has the necessary resources.  See the Cobol FAQ at:
http://cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm.for a list.

Thane Hubbells Cobol in 24 hours book comes with a Fujitsu compiler

http://www.murach.com/books/cobol/microfocus.htm 
book comes with MF Personal Cobol

http://www.murach.com/books/eooc/index.htm
book come with MF NetExpress, University Edition

http://www.murach.com/books/ecwp/index.htm
book come with MF NetExpress, University Edition

These are just some that I know of. Shop around for best price.  Of those
above Thanes book was the cheapest.  Be sure to check out the limitations
as there might be some you cannot accept.

Other full feature compilers will cost more.

PERCobol compile to the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) and will work on many
platforms.  I have no idea how much it costs.

Good luck!

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@microfocus.nospam.com>

```
Best thing to do is get a quote for the run times from your MF account
manager - they are not expensive. Discuss your situation with him/her. ISVs
include this in the price they charge to thier customers. MF, like any other
company, needs revenue to fund R&D.

Run-times were chargable in Net Express 3.0 but this was not clear in the
wording of the license agreement so a run time amnesty was announced for 3.0
users.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus! Me so angry?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-29T08:12:58+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D1D5DFA.FCFBE10E@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<a68c53f0.0206272247.f095a13@posting.google.com> <1025263477.52637.0@doris.uk.clara.net> <afiai2026d0@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
Grinder wrote:
> >
> > I think this changed between NE 3.0 and NE3.1  IIRC, 3.0 had freely
> > distributable runtime whereas 3.1 does not.
> 
> MicroFocus/Merant would be the ones to ask,

Merant is no longer the one to ask, they sold it and it is now
MicroFocus again (with new owners).  Actually Merant was _never_ the one
to ask as the sales staff (that I talked to) were/are completely
clueless about the actual licencing terms (and the products), the only
bit they understood was where money had to be paid.

> but it's my understanding that
> there is/was a licensing fee for the 3.0 runtime.  It was, however, not as
> aggressively enforced.

From MF Cobol/2 1.x until NE 3.1 and including derivitives such as
Microsoft Cobol 4.x and 5.x and IBM Cobol 1.x, it was possible to build
a distributable executable with no run-time fee.  The licence to do this
was in the manual and it specified what features could be used.  Some
additional features, if used, would require that a run-time licence be
obtained for the client.  

Even though it was clearly stated in the manual, and on some sales
brochures that 'free runtimes' could be distributed, the sales staff
denied this, claimed that it did not apply, stated it had been
superceeded and said it was wrong, and you must buy from them.

I understand that at NE 3.1 it has finally been removed and run-times
are required per user.  However it was agreed that the standard 10 user
site run-time could be 'split' and it was not necessary to use a 10 user
licence for each site if only one or two users needed it.

I have also been variously told that 'one user' is one instance of one
application running if they are separately started. So if Payroll and
Debtors are separate .EXEs and one user has each running in seaparte
windows on one machine that is two user licences.

I keep within the allowable 'free' licence and don't worry about
upgrading to newer versions.

Fujitsu also has a split in run-time.  The standard is freely
distributable but additional features (such as multi-threading) requires
run-time fee and/or that the Enterprise edition be purchased.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus! Me so angry?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-29T12:41:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0WhT8.446956$Gs.32301506@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<a68c53f0.0206272247.f095a13@posting.google.com> <1025263477.52637.0@doris.uk.clara.net> <afiai2026d0@enews3.newsguy.com> <3D1D5DFA.FCFBE10E@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
>
> Fujitsu also has a split in run-time.  The standard is freely
> distributable but additional features (such as multi-threading) requires
> run-time fee and/or that the Enterprise edition be purchased.

But even there, one has choices.

For most applications, multi-threading is no big deal. The other major item
for which Fujitsu anticipates a run-time is their fast sort. "BSort" is
installed automatically on your development machine, but "ClunkySort" is
part of your created distribution.

(This leads to the confusing situation where an application runs like a
scalded cat on the development machine, but not nearly so well when the
application is deployed!)

Anyway, for large sorts, we implemented an Active-X control from Sort
Solutions (I think is was about $60) which is as fast as the Fujitsu sort -
and has no runtime fees. Thanks to Thane Hubble for the heads-up for this
swell product.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus! Me so angry?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-06-30T07:59:31+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D1EAC53.40D1AF5C@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<a68c53f0.0206272247.f095a13@posting.google.com> <1025263477.52637.0@doris.uk.clara.net> <afiai2026d0@enews3.newsguy.com> <3D1D5DFA.FCFBE10E@Azonic.co.nz> <0WhT8.446956$Gs.32301506@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <afkosj$jcs$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Lee Unterreiner wrote:
> > >
> > > Fujitsu also has a split in run-time.  The standard is freely
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> are FREE including that for .NET

The standard run-times are free with the Standard and Professional
editions (Professional includes PowerCobol).  The multi-threaded
run-time is free _if_ you have the Enterprise edition (which is why I
said ..).  

My understanding of the Academic edition is that it does not include a
deployment licence, so they don't get the free run-time.

The .NET deployment is:

"""NetCOBOL for .NET Deployment Fees1(per processor) 

     Number of Processors   List Price       Maintenance
                1		$0.		$0.
                2          	$1,500.         $300.
                4       	$2,250.         $450.
                8        	$3,250.         $650.
               16       	$5,250.         $1,050.
               32    		$9,250.         $1,850."""
```

```
> > Fujitsu also has a split in run-time.  The standard is freely
> > distributable but additional features (such as multi-threading) requires
> > run-time fee and/or that the Enterprise edition be purchased.

William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> It is my understanding that the most recent release(s) of Fujitsu do *not*
> require separate run-time license for the multi-threading support.  They do
> (I think) for some other features (certainly their "enhanced" sort, for
> example)

"""*Production execution of ISAPI, SAF, MTS and invoking COBOL from
Active Server Pages requires the multi-threaded run-time which is
provided in the Enterprise Edition."""

If you purchase the Enterprise edition then the multi-threaded run-time
is free for distribution.  With the other editions you would either have
to upgrade to Enterprise or pay for run-times.
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus! Me so angry?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-06-28T21:52:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D1CDB64.323C9E4D@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<a68c53f0.0206272247.f095a13@posting.google.com>`

```


The Twitcher wrote:

> Maybe I don't understand it right, but one of my co-workers showed me
> some information about licensing from MicroFocus....
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks, Twitcher

Unfortunately this is a recurring theme.

First, you have to ask yourself, why did we buy Net Express in the first
place ? Without doubt it is a superb product, but that licensing GRRRRH.
Get the e-mail address for Tony Hill their new head honcho and let him
know your views about licensing. (Just like the rest of us you say to
yourself, I paid my dues by buying a not too cheap product and then they
want to sting me for royalties).

Having asked yourself, why Net Express, then evaluate alternatives. Can
you dumb down, taking a less effective compiler, or do you want one that
closely matches. Depends what you want to program, but Fujitsu POWER COBOL
is the nearest competitor with an equal number of features. That's not
knocking compilers like Acu or R/M (Liant) etc. which still do a good job.

Whichever compiler you choose, there will be a price tag, albeit no
royalties. How much effort have you put into coding in Net Express - this
is an additional cost factor when considering a switch.

Very important - when and if you do decide on another compiler, ask the
vendor very specifically, "Which features included in the draft of COBOL
2002 will you NOT be supporting ?".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus! Me so hungry....

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-06-29T07:14:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0206290614.2168be71@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a68c53f0.0206272247.f095a13@posting.google.com> <7q6T8.26876$Uu2.4527@sccrnsc03>`

```
CA-Realia COBOL and Fujitsu COBOL (in the US) are the only two COBOL
compiler vendors that do NOT charge a runtime fee.  (Oh - and KOBOL as
well).  Some past versions of MF COBOL only charged a runtime fee if
you distributed certain componants.  If you use Dialog system though -
you fall into that category.  Finally - even if you "Static link" -
you will have to distribute part of the runtime support files.  I have
an extreme dislike for runtime fees, especially on inexpensive
software.

There was a small developer amnesty program with NetExpress 3.1 - not
sure you can quailfy but ask your MicroFocus sales rep.


"Mike Kinasz" <mkinasz@cbspayroll.com> wrote in message news:<7q6T8.26876$Uu2.4527@sccrnsc03>...
> What if you are not deploying the application server nor any of the files
> which make up the application server?  What if all you deploy is just one
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Thanks, Twitcher
```

<a id="t-fc68cd4576"></a>
## Thread: above the line and below the line — 7 this month / 13 total

### above the line and below the line

- **From:** pakku@soccermail.com (Thomas Charles)
- **Date:** 2002-06-28T17:35:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89355d6f.0206281635.6a79d48a@posting.google.com>`

```
I guess this topic has been flogged to death.  I did a lot of searches
of the archives but couldn't find the answers I wanted.

I am working in an OS/390 environment where nearly all the programs
have been recompiled, relinked etc for LE.  We have some programs that
call vendor supplied subroutines that are untouched because we think
those subroutines are old and compiled "below the line".

So my remit is to find out what can be done.  To this end what is the
answer to the question
Can programs compiled with AMODE 31 statically be linked to older
programs that are compiled almost certainly with AMODE 24?  Does the
RMODE matter?

I know this is one of the many aspects of migration to LE but if a
reasonably simple answer is possible to assist someone who is
primarily a cobol programmer (and having limited Systems programming
knowledge) it will be much appreciated.

Thank you
```

#### ↳ Re: above the line and below the line

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-29T09:55:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D1D842A.372CF34A@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<89355d6f.0206281635.6a79d48a@posting.google.com>`

```
Thomas Charles wrote:
> 
> I guess this topic has been flogged to death.  I did a lot of searches
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> those subroutines are old and compiled "below the line".

If in doubt, run AMBLIST on the subprogram, or just open up the
library in View mode in TSO/ISPF and you should be able to see the
AMODE and RMODE for each program.

> 
> So my remit is to find out what can be done.  To this end what is the
> answer to the question
> Can programs compiled with AMODE 31 statically be linked to older
> programs that are compiled almost certainly with AMODE 24?  

Yes you can statically link AMODE(31) programs with AMODE(24)
programs.  AMODE is Adressing Mode, meaning that the object code is
generated to support 31-bit addresses, AMODE(31), or 24-bit addresses,
AMODE(24).  I have seen COBOL II load modules that were marked
AMODE(ANY),RMODE(24).  Your older programs are almost certainly
AMODE(24),RMODE(24).

> Does the
> RMODE matter?

Yes it does.  RMODE is residency mode, meaning where the program must
reside in memory, either below the 24-bit address boundary, RMODE(24),
or anywhere above or below the 24-bit address boundary, RMODE(ANY). 
The 24-bit address boundary is also known as the 16-megabyte line,
hence "above or below the line".  Some IBM services still require
control blocks below the line, so you may see some programs built as
AMODE(31),RMODE(24).  It is possible to have an AMODE(31),RMODE(ANY)
program reside above the line and dynamically call an
AMODE(31),RMODE(24) program, which will be loaded below the line at
runtime.

I am not aware of any cases where an AMODE(24) program can be anything
but RMODE(24).  If you statically bind any number of object modules
into a load module, the presence of even one RMODE(24) program should
result in the linkage editor downgrading the entire Load module to
RMODE(24).  If you generally have COBOL main programs calling non-LE
assembler programs built many years ago, this should work.  You can
also override the linkage editor to explicitly build the load module
as RMODE(24).   

> 
> I know this is one of the many aspects of migration to LE but if a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you

In my shop they've converted every LPAR to have the Language
Environment option ALL31(ON) in order to improve runtime performance. 
In that environment any LE-enabled program that calls an AMODE(24)
program will abend immediately.  There is a runtime parameter that can
be added to those programs, something like this:

//STEP010 EXEC PGM=MYPROG,PARM='/ALL31(OFF),,STACK=BELOW' 

If your program already has a PARM statement, then the existing
parameter data is placed before the "/" character.  Everything after
the slash character is passed to LE.  The "ALL31(OFF)" override
parameter should allow the program to run, but with some slight
performance penalty compared to an AMODE(31) program.  

The PARM won't work for a non-LE main program calling an LE-enabled
subprogram, because the PARM doesn't get passed through to LE.  In
that case you can statically link in an LE option override module for
that program.  You might still have issues with vendor supplied code.

If you are using the LE ALL31(ON) option, you can still run
AMODE(24),RMODE(24) non-LE programs (Assembler, Easytrieve) without
any problems.

I have been told that an older assembler program may not be able to
call an LE-enabled COBOL subprogram, because the old prolog code
tyically creates 24-bit addresses in the register savearea, and LE
requires all savearea chains to support 31-bit addresses.  You may
need assembler expertise for some of those older programs.  

I hope that helps!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: above the line and below the line

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-29T06:51:06-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D1D911A.4080205@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<89355d6f.0206281635.6a79d48a@posting.google.com> <3D1D842A.372CF34A@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:
> Thomas Charles wrote:
>
(snip)
> 
>>Does the
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> but RMODE(24).

That's because if the RMODE were "31" or "ANY", the program would be 
loaded above the line and the program would be unable to address its own 
code, instant S0C4 (in IBM's MVS & successors).

(snip)

> I have been told that an older assembler program may not be able to
> call an LE-enabled COBOL subprogram, because the old prolog code
> tyically creates 24-bit addresses in the register savearea, and LE
> requires all savearea chains to support 31-bit addresses.  You may
> need assembler expertise for some of those older programs.  

Curious what you mean here by the "old prolog code". The standard 
linkage macros CALL, SAVE & RETURN don't generate any 24-bit adcons. 
Maybe your shop had some macros developed a zillion years ago which did?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: above the line and below the line

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-30T08:33:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D1EC25A.A46E26EC@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<89355d6f.0206281635.6a79d48a@posting.google.com> <3D1D842A.372CF34A@worldnet.att.net> <3D1D911A.4080205@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`

```
Liam Devlin wrote:
> 
> Arnold Trembley wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Maybe your shop had some macros developed a zillion years ago which did?

Well, I'm not the world's greatest System 370 assembler programmer,
but I will try to repeat the explanation that was given to me.

Imagine a S/370 assembler subprogram with the following prolog code:


90ECD00C = STM   14,12,12(13)        SAVE THE REGISTERS 
18CF     = LR    12,15               COPY OUR BASE ADDR TO R12
1821     = LR    2,1                 COPY PARMLIST ADDR TO R2
4510C074 = BAL   1,116(12)           BRANCH AROUND SAVEAREA &
EYECATCHER
           DC    18F'0'
           DC    CL32'** MYPROGRM &SYSDATE &SYSTIME **'  
50D10004   ST    13,4(1,0)           STORE CALLER'S SAVEAREA ADDR
501D0008   ST    1,(8,13)            STORE LOCAL SAVEAREA ADDR
18D1       LR    13,1                POINT TO LOCAL SAVEARA
1812       LR    1,2                 RESTORE PARMLIST ADDR 
05C0       BALR  12,0                MAKE R12 AS OUR BASE ADDR
           USING *,12

If this program receives control in 24-bit addressing mode, the BAL
(Branch And Link) instruction in the fourth line loads the 24-bit
address of the local savearea into R1.  The BALR (Branch And Link
Register) instruction in the penultimate line loads the 24-bit base
address of the subprogram into R12.  If this subprogram calls a COBOL
program (and that COBOL program has been recompiled in an LE-enabled
COBOL compiler) LE will abend your program, because the savearea chain
address is invalid.  It's a 24-bit address when LE requires a 31-bit
address.

The way it was explained to me was that you had to use LA (Load
Address) or LR (Load Register) rather than BAL or BALR, in order to
preserve the addresses as 31-bit. 

Converting 24-bit assembler to 31-bit is tricky.  In AMODE(24), there
are bit switches and masks stored in the high-order byte.  Sometimes
clever programmers stored their own byte or bit flags in the
high-order byte, since the computer wasn't "using" it.

Our oldest assembler routines came from DOS/VSE.  At any rate, I
didn't see them using SAVE macros.  We had our own ENTER macro,
although that was not universally used.  The sysprogs changed the
ENTER macro to remove any use of BAL or BALR.  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: above the line and below the line

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-06-30T22:06:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c22082$5d90c040$2dc2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<89355d6f.0206281635.6a79d48a@posting.google.com> <3D1D842A.372CF34A@worldnet.att.net> <3D1D911A.4080205@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <3D1EC25A.A46E26EC@worldnet.att.net>`

```
All savearea chains have always had room to save the entire register of 32
bits.  The addressing mode bit in the PSW affects how some instructions
work.  In amode=24 LA  only stores a 24 bit address into the register and
set the leftmost bits to zero.  Some programmers took advantage of this to
pass a parameter in those high order bits.  In amode=31 LA places a 31 bit
address into the register and sets the leftmost bit to zero.

The real problem with an amode=24  program calling an amode=31 mode program
is that the program receiving control expects to receive it in 31 bit
addressing mode BALR cannot do this, hence the newer instructions BSM and
BASSM instructions.  See Advanced Assembler Language and MVS Interfaces by
Carmine Cannatello Chapter 14 for a more detailed explanation.

Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in article
<3D1EC25A.A46E26EC@worldnet.att.net>...
> Liam Devlin wrote:
> > 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Maybe your shop had some macros developed a zillion years ago which
did?
> 
> Well, I'm not the world's greatest System 370 assembler programmer,
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: above the line and below the line

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-29T13:48:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<afkvd9$miq$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<89355d6f.0206281635.6a79d48a@posting.google.com>`

```
One part of the equation not discussed in other responses is the ability in
VS COBOL II and later programs to compile your programs with DATA(24) - but
link-edit it with RMODE(ANY) and AMODE(31).

With DATA(24), you *may* dynamically call AMODE(24) programs and "pass"
parameters to them (below the line) while still keeping this program "above"
the line.  This can be QUITE useful if you have "vendor provided" AMODE(24)
programs that you need/want to interface with.

P.S.  There is also a "new" term that you may run into and that is "above
and below the BAR".  This refers to 64-bit addressing.  So far COBOL doesn't
"play" in that issue, so you shouldn't have to worry about it.  However, you
may run across it in some "current" information.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: above the line and below the line

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-29T17:58:38-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D1E2D8E.1030008@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<89355d6f.0206281635.6a79d48a@posting.google.com> <afkvd9$miq$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

## Thread: free(string) — 2 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### free(string)

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>
- **Date:** 2002-06-29T16:49:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<afl6r4$cqq$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net>`

```
I am trying to determine the windows APi equivalent to the free(string) line
of code that follows...My app interfaces with a C program that is allocating
a string...I do not have the source code the module that allocates the
string...My cobol module is a callback for the main C module)...What I get
from the C module is an address...i set the address of a linkage section 01
level to that address and can reference the data just fine...BUT...

The string needs to be freed by my app, not the C Lang program that
allocates the string...They supply a sample app that simply does
free(string)...But I am unable to translate that into an windows API call...

                char *string;

                        free(string);

Please copy any response to this to my email account...My news reader does
not seem to like this group for some reason...it displays new messages every
now and then at best...
```

#### ↳ Re: free(string)

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-30T10:59:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ewBT8.13202$ry2.11595@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<afl6r4$cqq$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net>`

```
free is the complement to malloc; and the particular method used by malloc
is compiler-dependent.

malloc returns a pointer to a block of memory; the compiler may do this with
either one of (at least) two methods:

GlobalAlloc (GMEM_FIXED||(any other atributes), size) , which is
complelmented by calling GlobalFree
or
GlobalLock(GlobalAlloc(any attributes other than GMEM_FIXED), size)), which
is complemented by GlobalUnlock followed by GlobalFree. In this case, you
need to get the global handle from the pointer using GlobalHandle

MSDN says GlobalUnlock has no effect on memory allocated with the GMEM_FIXED
attribute; so it would appear you would be safe to always use the second
method, even if  GlobalUnlock  has no effect.

The more 'modern' (and according to MSDN, now recommended method) Windows
method of allocating memory and obtaining a pointer is HeapAlloc;  if your
compiler users this API, you free the memory with HeapFree.

If you can read the C program's import table you may be able to figure out
exactly which method the C program uses; but if ambuguity remains, you might
just end up writing test code to figure out how the C program is obtaining
that pointer it passes to your program.

If GlobalFree (pointer value) returns non-zero, then it has returned a
handle to the object, and you have to repeat the operation.

Easier way to know: specify which C compiler and version you are using, and
ask this same question in a 'c' newsgroup. Chances are someone there will
know how the particular compiler works.

I do not edit email addresses and I believe
"**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s" is not your real email
address, so this is posted to the newsgroup only.

MCM



Ken Mullins <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s> wrote in
message news:afl6r4$cqq$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net...
> I am trying to determine the windows APi equivalent to the free(string)
line
> of code that follows...My app interfaces with a C program that is
allocating
> a string...I do not have the source code the module that allocates the

01

>
> The string needs to be freed by my app, not the C Lang program that
> allocates the string...They supply a sample app that simply does
> free(string)...But I am unable to translate that into an windows API
call...
>
>                 char *string;
>
>                         free(string);
>

## Thread: acc-method runtime error in netcobol for .net — 2 msgs

### acc-method runtime error in netcobol for .net

- **From:** "student" <blah@blah.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-30T01:30:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OatT8.53180$xy.17199316@twister.socal.rr.com>`

```
Hi, just wondering if I missed something in netcobol. When I try to open a
line sequential file made with notepad, I get an Open error acc-method
message. But when I created a line sequential file with a netcobol program,
I am able to open up the file with another program. When I opened up the
file made with the netcobol program there is extra bytes at the beginning of
the file. Is this normal? Is there a way to have netcobol not require these
bytes to be there in order to read the file?

Any help or explanation will be much appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: acc-method runtime error in netcobol for .net

- **From:** "Lee Unterreiner" <flu@nospam.flu-ent.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-29T22:08:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<afm3tl$cb9$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<OatT8.53180$xy.17199316@twister.socal.rr.com>`

```
Thats a known problem to be fixed in the next release.  The Fujitsu RTS is
treating it as Unicode instead of ASCII.  In the meantime you can either use
the StreamReader class or use Notepad to change the encoding.

<a id="t-96b77e2110"></a>
## Thread: Freeware Visual Cobol — 3 this month / 8 total

### Freeware Visual Cobol

- **From:** "John Hastings" <johnhastings@knology.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-30T12:31:57-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uhucn71nrs0e84@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Visual Cobol by fujitsu is free.  I love it.  It makes applications that
have a beautiful windows interface.  The design method is just like
microsoft's visual basic.  You just click and drag buttons, list box's,
labels, edit boxes, etc.  But it has many more features than that, including
SQL, DDE, OLE, Animation, etc.  Very powerful.  You can design gui apps and
compile them (make executables) on the pc, then take the same code, and port
it to AIX, HPUX, Solaris, for a nice gui app there...  This product is
incredible.  After you click and drag your items to the sheet, you then
right click them and set each items procedure using COBOL !!!!  It's
awesome!
```

#### ↳ Re: Freeware Visual Cobol

- **From:** "Joe Dzurinda" <jdzurinda@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-30T17:33:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mhHT8.96069$hF5.4591038@news2.east.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<uhucn71nrs0e84@corp.supernews.com>`

```

news:uhucn71nrs0e84@corp.supernews.com...
> Visual Cobol by fujitsu is free. > incredible.  After you click and drag
your items to the sheet, you then

And how do I get a free version???

Joe
```

#### ↳ Re: Freeware Visual Cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-06-30T13:26:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<afnihi$nne$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<uhucn71nrs0e84@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Fujitsu did not, does not, and probably never will sell a product called
"Visual COBOL" (or "Visual Cobol").  The product by that name was sold first
by Mbp, then Micro Focus, then MERANT, then "removed" from their product
line.

If you are interested in getting the "free" version (Version 3) of Fujitsu
COBOL, check out the COBOL FAQ (or look for it at www.adtools.com)
```

<a id="t-ac7198edb6"></a>
## Thread: Accessing multiple databases in Net Express V3.0 — 2 this month / 5 total

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Accessing multiple databases in Net Express V3.0

- **From:** "Bill Gentry" <billgentry2@comcast.net>
- **Date:** 2002-06-30T21:33:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yOKT8.471307$Gs.33516106@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Hello all,

I've been working up an app that needs to process 2 MS Access databases
concurrently.   It will connect to both DB's via ODBC "connect" statements,
issue SQL's against both of them, then disconnect.

First off, I'm not sure if this will even work or not, at least with the IDE
I'm using.  I've been into MicroFocus' Knowledge base, and All I saw was one
program sample where you could setup multiple connections, but you had to
"swap" the connections, similar to closing one file before opening another.

In any case, here's a couple of code snippets.  I'm not posting the whole
program for the sake of brevity... if anyone wants to see the whole thing,
I'll post it.

First, I declare my two databases like this:
       110-DECLARATIVES.

           EXEC SQL
               DECLARE KCONN DATABASE
           END-EXEC.
           PERFORM 950-CHECK-SQLCODE.
           EXEC SQL
               DECLARE TCONN DATABASE
           END-EXEC.
           PERFORM 950-CHECK-SQLCODE.

       120-CONNECTIONS.
           MOVE 'KSYS'           TO KSERVER.
           MOVE ' '              TO KUSER.
           EXEC SQL
               CONNECT TO :KSERVER
                       AS KCONN
                     USER :KUSER
           END-EXEC.
           PERFORM 950-CHECK-SQLCODE.

           MOVE 'TRANS1'       TO TSERVER.
           MOVE ' '            TO TUSER.
           EXEC SQL
              CONNECT TO :TSERVER
                      AS TCONN
                    USER :TUSER
           END-EXEC.
           PERFORM 950-CHECK-SQLCODE.

Then, later in the program, I attempt to fetch some data from the database
I'd declared as "KCONN"

           EXEC SQL
              AT KCONN
               DECLARE SUBMCURS CURSOR
                  FOR
                     SELECT TranFile FROM Submitted
                     Where ExtractID = 'ZZ'
           END-EXEC.
           PERFORM 950-CHECK-SQLCODE.
           EXEC SQL
               OPEN SUBMCURS
           END-EXEC.
           PERFORM 950-CHECK-SQLCODE.
           EXEC SQL
               FETCH SUBMCURS INTO :TRAN-FILE-NAME
           END-EXEC.
           PERFORM 950-CHECK-SQLCODE.

The last SQL statement is giving me an SQLCODE of -0000019514 "cursor is not
prepared".   The netexpress documentation lists the "AS" as valid code in
the declare cursor, if you have a "db_name" in use, as I do in this program.
It's not listed as valid code for the "OPEN" or the "FETCH" commands.

Anyway, if anyone has had any luck with this, I'd appreciate the input.  I'm
going to keep working on it and I'll post the "fix" if I ever find one.   I
may have to re-think the app, though:  Dump the contents of the first
database to text files, then connect to the second database for processing.

Thanks,
Bill
```

#### ↳ Re: Accessing multiple databases in Net Express V3.0

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-06-30T23:08:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D1F903E.406C4E58@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<yOKT8.471307$Gs.33516106@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```


Bill Gentry wrote:

> Hello all,
>
…[75 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill

I'm a real 'newbie' at SQL so I'm using the ESQL Assistant to generate my
'basic' queries. The following is OO but see if it ties in similar to what you
are doing. The only change I have made is to substitute SQLTATE for SQL:CODE :-

$set sql (ansi92entry) sql (dbman=ODBC)

 *>-----------------sqlconct.cbl----------------------------------

 Class-id.          SQLConnection
                    inherits from SQLErrors.

 Class-control.     SQLConnection         is class "sqlconct"
                    SQLErrors             is class "sqlerror"
                    .
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
 FACTORY.
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 Method-id. "new".
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 Linkage section.
 01 lnk-self                         object reference.
 Procedure Division returning lnk-self.
   invoke super "new" returning lnk-self
 End Method "new".
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 END FACTORY.
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 OBJECT.
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 copy "sqlerror.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ws==.
 01 os-Parent                            object reference.

 *> DB IDs

 01 ws-dbID              pic 9 value 0.
    88 NoID              value 0.
    88 GeneralID         value 1.          *> Server1
    88 FacilityID        value 2.          *> Server2

 *> DB Names

 01 ws-CurrentName       pic x(10) value spaces.
 01 ws-DbName occurs 2.
    05 pic x(10)         value "Corrosion".
    05 pic x(10)         value "Facility".

 EXEC SQL BEGIN DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.
 01  SQLSTATE    PIC X(5).
 EXEC SQL END DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.

 01 charx                    pic x.

 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 Method-id. "DBConnect".
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 Linkage section.
 01 lnk-id                              pic 9.
 01 lnk-result                          pic 9(03).

 Procedure Division using     lnk-id
                    returning lnk-result.

   Move zeroes to lnk-result

   Evaluate true
     when lnk-id = ws-dbID
          EXIT METHOD
     when lnk-id = 1
           move lnk-ID to ws-dbID
           move ws-DbName(ws-dbID) to ws-CurrentName
           EXEC SQL SET CONNECTION Server1 END-EXEC
     when other
           move lnk-ID to ws-dbID
           move ws-DbName(ws-dbID) to ws-CurrentName
           EXEC SQL SET CONNECTION Server2 END-EXEC
   End-evaluate

   if    SQLSTATE <> '00000'
         invoke super "sqlstateMessage"
           using ws-dbID, SQLSTATE, ws-CurrentName
         move 99 to lnk-result
   End-if

 End Method "DBConnect".
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 Method-id. "DBDisconnect".
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 Procedure Division.

   EXEC SQL DISCONNECT Server1 END-EXEC
   EXEC SQL DISCONNECT Server1 END-EXEC

 End Method "DBDisconnect".
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 Method-id. "setDBs".
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 Linkage section.
 01 lnk-result                              pic 9(03).
 Procedure Division returning lnk-result.

  move zeroes to lnk-result

  set GeneralID to true
  EXEC SQL CONNECT TO "Corrosion" as Server1 END-EXEC

   if   SqlState <> "00000"
        perform ERROR-MESSAGE
   End-if

  set FacilityID to true
  EXEC SQL CONNECT TO "Facility"  as Server2 END-EXEC

   if   SqlState <> "00000"
        perform ERROR-MESSAGE
   End-if

 EXIT METHOD.

 ERROR-MESSAGE.

  invoke super "sqlstateMessage"
  using ws-ErrorID, SqlState, ws-CurrentName
  move 99 to lnk-result
  .

 End Method "setDBs".
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 End OBJECT.
 End CLASS SQLConnection.
 *>---------------------------------------------------------------

From my Business Logic Class 'EditClients' I  invoke the Client Table :-

$set sql (ansi92entry) sql (dbman=ODBC)

 *>-----------------clitable.cbl---------------------------------

 Class-id.          ClientsTable
                    inherits from SqlErrors.

 Class-control.     ClientsTable            is class "clitable"
                    SqlErrors               is class "sqlerror"
                    .
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 FACTORY.
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------
 End FACTORY.
 *>------------------------------------------------------------
 OBJECT.
 *>----------------------------------------------------------
 OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
 EXEC SQL BEGIN DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.
 01  SQLSTATE                    pic x(5).
 EXEC SQL END DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.

 copy "clients.cpy".
 copy "CliRecrd.cpy" replacing ==(tag1)== by ==01 ClientsRecord==
                               ==(tag)==  by ==Clients==.
 copy "sqlerror.cpy" replacing ==(tag)==  by ==ws==.

 01 ws-dbID              pic 9 value 1. *> General
 01 os-DBI               object reference.
 01 os-SqlConnection     object reference.
 *>--------------------------------------------------------------

I *think* you only need the cursor where you are going to FETCH more than one,
so I''ve included methods for 'one' and 'cursor'. BTW if you are *new* to DB, it
might pay to try using the ESQ: Assistant to ensure 100% syntax :-

*>---------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "getClientDefaults".
*>---------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-key                       pic x(03).
01 lnk-record.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==05 lnk==.
copy "clideflt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.

Procedure Division using     lnk-Key
                   returning lnk-record.

set ResultOK to true
invoke os-SqlConnection "DBconnect"
       using ws-dbID returning lnk-SqlResult

if TableError
   EXIT METHOD
End-if

move lnk-key to Clients-cliID, ws-ErrorID(1:3)
move "00"    to Clients-FacID, ws-ErrorID(4:2)

     EXEC SQL
      SELECT DISTINCT
            ,`A`.`LifeYears`
            ,`A`.`Percent1`
            ,`A`.`Percent2`
            ,`A`.`Range1`
            ,`A`.`Range2`
            ,`A`.`Range3`
            ,`A`.`Range4`
      INTO
           :Clients-LifeYears:Clients-LifeYears-NULL
          ,:Clients-Percent1:Clients-Percent1-NULL
          ,:Clients-Percent2:Clients-Percent2-NULL
          ,:Clients-Range1:Clients-Range1-NULL
          ,:Clients-Range2:Clients-Range2-NULL
          ,:Clients-Range3:Clients-Range3-NULL
          ,:Clients-Range4:Clients-Range4-NULL
        FROM `Clients` A
       WHERE ( `A`.`CliId` = :Clients-CliId )
        AND  ( `A`.`FacId` = :Clients-FacId )
     END-EXEC

Evaluate true
    when SQLSTATE = "00000"
     move Clients-ClientSubData to lnk-ClientSubData
    when SQLSTATE = "02000"              *> No more rows
         set RecNotFound to true
    when other
         invoke self "setErrorMessage"
         set TableError to true
End-Evaluate

End Method "getClientDefaults".
*>---------------------------------------------------------

And for more than "one" -  Note that ESQL Assistant generates the next cursor
number that you need for each query, plus each of my queries invokes "DBConnect"
to check I'm accessing the correct database:-

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "makeClientCollection".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
copy "clilistrec.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ls==.
Linkage section.
01 lnk-PrimeKey.
   05 lnk-ClientNumber               pic x(03).
   05 lnk-FacilityNumber             pic x(02).
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division using     lnk-PrimeKey
                   returning lnk-SqlResult.

set ResultOK to true
initialize ls-CliListrecord
invoke os-SqlConnection "DBconnect"
       using ws-dbID returning lnk-SqlResult

if TableError
   EXIT METHOD
End-if

EXEC SQL
 DECLARE CSR29 CURSOR FOR SELECT
        `A`.`CliId`
       ,`A`.`FacId`
       ,`A`.`Shortname`
   FROM `Clients` A
  WHERE ( `A`.`FacId` = '00' )
END-EXEC
EXEC SQL OPEN CSR29 END-EXEC

PERFORM UNTIL SQLSTATE <> '00000'
  EXEC SQL
  FETCH CSR29 INTO
      :Clients-CliId:Clients-CliId-NULL
     ,:Clients-FacId:Clients-FacId-NULL
     ,:Clients-Shortname:Clients-Shortname-NULL
  END-EXEC

   Evaluate true

      when SQLSTATE = "00000"
        initialize ls-CliListRecord
        move Clients-CliID     to Cli-ID
        move Clients-FacID     to Fac-ID
        move Clients-shortname to Cli-ShortName
        invoke os-DBI "newListRecord" using ls-CliListRecord

        *> Note : above data is returned to os-DBI to add
        *> to the appropriate collection/dictionary entry

     when SQLSTATE = "02000"               *> no more rows
          EXIT PERFORM
     when other
          move Clients-CliID to ws-ErrorID(1:3)
          move Clients-FacID to ws-ErrorID(4:2)
          invoke self "setErrorMessage"
          set TableError to true
          EXIT METHOD
   End-evaluate
END-PERFORM
EXEC SQL CLOSE CSR29 END-EXEC

End Method "makeClientCollection".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

HTH

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```
